# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: EMF Equation Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Armature Rotation at N RPM] --> B[Flux Cutting by Z Conductors]
    B --> C[Faradays Law Induction]
    C --> D[EMF per Conductor]
    D --> E[Series Addition per Path]
    E --> F[Parallel Paths A]
    F --> G[Brush Terminal EMF E]
    H[Field Poles P] --> I[Flux per Pole Phi]
    I --> B
    J[Total Conductors Z] --> K[Conductors per Path Z by A]
    K --> E
    L[Winding Type] --> M{Simplex Lap or Simplex Wave}
    M --> N[A equals P for Lap]
    M --> O[A equals 2 for Wave]
    N --> F
    O --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Flow in a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDC[DC Supply V] --> RA[Armature Resistance R subscript a]
    RA --> EB[Back EMF E subscript b]
    EB --> MECH[Mechanical Output N and T]
    subgraph EMF_GEN [EMF Generation Block]
        FLUX[Field Flux Phi] --> K1[K subscript a Phi N]
        N[SPEED N] --> K1
        K1 --> EB_OUT[Induced EMF E]
    end
    EB_OUT --> EB
    EB -.->|Opposes V| RA
    MECH -.->|Feedback via N| EMF_GEN
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: Lap vs Wave Winding Effect on EMF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given P, Phi, N, Z Identical] --> DECIDE{Winding Type}
    DECIDE -->|Simplex Lap| LAP[A equals P]
    DECIDE -->|Simplex Wave| WAVE[A equals 2]
    LAP --> ELAP[E equals P Phi N Z by 60 P equals Phi N Z by 60]
    WAVE --> EWAVE[E equals P Phi N Z by 120]
    ELAP --> COMP[Compare: ELAP greater than EWAVE by factor P by 2]
    EWAVE --> COMP
    COMP --> USE[Application: Wave for High Voltage, Lap for High Current]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: EMF Equation in DC Drive Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS [INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Number of Poles P]
        P2[Flux per Pole Phi]
        P3[Speed N]
        P4[Conductors Z]
        P5[Parallel Paths A]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE [EMF COMPUTATION BLOCK]
        EQ1[P multiplied by Phi]
        EQ2[EQ1 multiplied by N]
        EQ3[EQ2 multiplied by Z]
        EQ4[EQ3 divided by 60A]
        OUT[Generated EMF E in Volts]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS [OUTPUT APPLICATIONS]
        APP1[Motor Speed Control N equals E by Ka Phi]
        APP2[Generator Terminal Voltage Vt equals Eg minus Ia Ra]
        APP3[Braking Analysis]
        APP4[Machine Rating Selection]
    end

    P1 --> EQ1
    P2 --> EQ1
    P3 --> EQ2
    P4 --> EQ3
    P5 --> EQ4
    EQ1 --> EQ2 --> EQ3 --> EQ4 --> OUT
    OUT --> APP1
    OUT --> APP2
    OUT --> APP3
    OUT --> APP4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:** These block diagrams emphasize that the EMF equation is **not an isolated formula** but the **central computational node** of an entire DC drive analysis framework. Every input parameter feeds into it, and every application (speed control, voltage regulation, braking) reads its output.
</br>

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the EMF equation of a DC machine. Explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The EMF equation of a DC machine is:

$$E = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A} \quad \text{volts}$$

Significance of each term:
- **P** = Number of magnetic poles in the machine.
- **╬”** = Useful flux per pole (in Weber), produced by the field winding.
- **N** = Speed of the armature (in RPM).
- **Z** = Total number of armature conductors.
- **A** = Number of parallel paths in the armature (A = P for lap, A = 2 for wave).
- **60** = Conversion factor from minutes to seconds.

[3 Marks: 1 for equation, 2 for explanation]

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare lap and wave winding in terms of parallel paths and generated EMF for the same armature data.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Parallel paths A | $A = P$ | $A = 2$ |
| EMF for given data | $E_{\text{lap}} = \dfrac{\Phi N Z}{60}$ | $E_{\text{wave}} = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{120}$ |
| Ratio $E_{\text{lap}} / E_{\text{wave}}$ | ŌĆö | $P / 2$ |

Wave winding produces **lower EMF** than lap winding for the *same* Z. Therefore, lap windings are used for high-current (low-voltage) machines and wave windings for high-voltage (low-current) machines.

[3 Marks: 1 for parallel paths, 1 for EMF comparison, 1 for application]

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 500 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.1 ╬® and the shunt field resistance is 100 ╬®. Calculate:
  - (i) The flux per pole at rated speed of 1500 RPM.
  - (ii) The armature current when delivering full-load current. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation [7 Marks]

**Statement of Faraday's Law:** The EMF induced is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \Phi \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Revolutions per second} = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law)} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Conductors per path} = \frac{Z}{A} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Total EMF across brushes} = \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{P \Phi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

[Full marks awarded for clean derivation with labeled steps]

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $V_t = 250$ V (terminal voltage)
- $P = 4$ poles
- Wave winding $\Rightarrow A = 2$
- $Z = 500$ conductors
- $R_a = 0.1$ ╬®
- $R_{sh} = 100$ ╬®
- $N = 1500$ RPM (rated)

**Step 1: Calculate EMF $E_g$**
For a shunt generator at no-load, terminal voltage equals generated EMF (approximately):
$$E_g \approx V_t = 250 \text{ V} \quad \text{(at no-load)}$$

Wait ŌĆö the question asks flux at *rated speed*. Assuming the EMF equals terminal voltage at rated load conditions where armature drop is small, we use $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$. Without load current given, we first compute the flux using the no-load condition (standard KTU assumption):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= V_t + I_a R_a \\
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming at the moment of computation we use $E_g \approx V_t = 250$ V as the starting point (KTU standard approximation for finding flux when no-load condition is implicit):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi &= \frac{E_g \times 60 \times A}{P \times N \times Z} \\
\Phi &= \frac{250 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 1500 \times 500} \\
\Phi &= \frac{30{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000} \\
\Phi &= 0.01 \text{ Wb} = 10 \text{ mWb}
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) Flux per pole = 0.01 Wb (or 10 mWb)** [3 Marks]

**Step 2: Calculate Armature Current**

Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ A}$$

Assuming full-load current $I_L$ (we use the rated condition where the armature supplies both load and shunt field):

Let full-load current be standard. For this typical problem, assume $I_L$ such that $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Without explicit $I_L$, we compute the *rated armature current* as follows:

At rated condition, recompute $E_g$:
$$\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A} = \frac{4 \times 0.01 \times 1500 \times 500}{60 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{30{,}000}{120} = 250 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

Then armature current:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_t &= E_g - I_a R_a \\
250 &= 250 - I_a \times 0.1 \\
I_a \times 0.1 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is a degenerate case under the assumption. For a *real* loaded condition, suppose the generator delivers load current $I_L$ such that:
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = I_L + 2.5$$

A common KTU problem extension: If full-load current $I_L = 100$ A is given (typical assumption), then:
$$I_a = 100 + 2.5 = 102.5 \text{ A} \quad \text{[4 Marks including justification]}$$

**(ii) Armature current $I_a = 102.5$ A** [assuming full-load = 100 A]

[Total: 7 Marks ŌĆö 3 for flux, 4 for armature current calculation]

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a DC machine. List the factors affecting the generated EMF and derive the EMF equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 6-pole DC machine has an armature with 720 conductors. The armature is lap-wound and runs at 1200 RPM. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. Calculate:
  - (i) The EMF generated.
  - (ii) The new EMF if the machine is rewound as simplex wave with the same data.
  - (iii) The speed at which the wave-wound machine must run to generate the same EMF as the lap-wound machine. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Sketch and Derivation [7 Marks]

**Sketch:** Show the cross-section of a DC machine with field poles, yoke, armature, commutator, and brushes.

**Factors affecting EMF:**
1. Flux per pole (╬”) ŌĆö directly proportional
2. Speed of rotation (N) ŌĆö directly proportional
3. Number of poles (P) ŌĆö directly proportional
4. Number of armature conductors (Z) ŌĆö directly proportional
5. Type of winding (A) ŌĆö inversely proportional

**Derivation:** [Same as Question A ŌĆö 7 marks total includes sketch (1), factors (1), and derivation (5).]

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $P = 6$
- $Z = 720$
- $N = 1200$ RPM
- $\Phi = 30$ mWb $= 0.03$ Wb

**(i) EMF for Lap Winding:**

Lap: $A = P = 6$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{lap}} &= \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A} = \frac{6 \times 0.03 \times 1200 \times 720}{60 \times 6} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 0.03 \times 1200 \times 720}{360}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator: $6 \times 0.03 = 0.18$; $0.18 \times 1200 = 216$; $216 \times 720 = 155{,}520$.

$$E_{\text{lap}} = \frac{155{,}520}{360} = 432 \text{ V} \quad \text{[2 Marks]}$$

**(ii) EMF for Wave Winding (Same Speed):**

Wave: $A = 2$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{wave}} &= \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A} = \frac{6 \times 0.03 \times 1200 \times 720}{60 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{155{,}520}{120} = 1296 \text{ V} \quad \text{[2 Marks]}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Speed of Wave-Wound Machine to Generate 432 V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{wave}} &= \frac{60\, A\, E}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{60 \times 2 \times 432}{6 \times 0.03 \times 720} \\
&= \frac{51{,}840}{129.6} = 400 \text{ RPM} \quad \text{[3 Marks]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answers:**
- (i) $E_{\text{lap}} = 432$ V
- (ii) $E_{\text{wave}} = 1296$ V (3├Ś the lap EMF)
- (iii) $N_{\text{wave}} = 400$ RPM (one-third of the original speed)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (loses 1 mark):** Flux is often given in **mWb**. Convert to Wb before substituting (1 mWb = $10^{-3}$ Wb).
> 2. **Forgetting A (loses 1ŌĆō2 marks):** Always identify the winding type (lap vs wave) and use A = P or A = 2 accordingly.
> 3. **Mixing up P and A:** In lap winding, $A = P$. Many students mistakenly write $A = 2$ for all windings. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **No unit conversion for N:** If speed is in rad/s, convert using $N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60\omega}{2\pi}$.
> 5. **Final answer without substitution shown:** Examiners award partial marks for the *substitution step*. Always write $\Phi = 30 \text{ mWb} = 0.03 \text{ Wb}$ explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing generator EMF with terminal voltage:** In a generator, $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$. In a motor, $E_b = V - I_a R_a$. Mixing these is a **fatal error** worth 2ŌĆō3 marks.
</br>

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö EMF Equation**

- [x] **Core formula:** $E = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60\, A}$ ŌĆö memorize verbatim.
- [x] **Compact form:** $E = K_a \Phi N$ where $K_a = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$.
- [x] **Lap winding:** $A = P$ ŌåÆ used for **high-current** machines.
- [x] **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ ŌåÆ used for **high-voltage** machines.
- [x] **Factor of 60:** Converts N (RPM) to revolutions per second.
- [x] **Faraday's Law foundation:** EMF = rate of flux cutting.
- [x] **Series addition:** Z/A conductors per path add in series; A paths are in parallel.
- [x] **Units check:** ╬” in Wb, N in RPM, E in Volts.
- [x] **Generator:** $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$.
- [x] **Motor:** $E_b = V - I_a R_a$.
- [x] **Speed formula (motor):** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$.
- [x] **Always show substitution** for KTU full marks.
- [x] **mWb to Wb conversion** is mandatory before plugging in.

> **One-line memory hook:**
> *"EMF equals Poles times Flux times Speed times Conductors, divided by sixty Parallel paths."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Generators, Principle of Operation of DC Motors, Electrical and Mechanical Characteristics of DC Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Generators, Principle of Operation of DC Motors, Electrical and Mechanical Characteristics of DC Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Types of DC Generators

A **DC Generator** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts mechanical energy into direct current electrical energy based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Electric Drives and Control*, the classification is fundamentally based on the **method of excitation** of the field winding.

### A. Separately Excited DC Generator
The field winding (**F**) is energized by an **independent external DC source** (battery or another DC supply). The field current $I_f$ is independent of the armature current $I_a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A separately excited DC generator is one in which the field winding is supplied from an external DC source, and the field current can be controlled independently of the armature current to achieve a wide range of generated voltages.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine a water pump where the "pressure" (voltage) is set by a separate, independent booster pump (external field supply). You can change the output pressure without touching the main pump ŌĆö giving you **independent, precise control**.

### B. Self-Excited DC Generator
The field winding is energized by the **current produced by the generator itself** (residual magnetism in the poles bootstraps the process). The field winding is connected across the armature in one of three configurations:

| Type | Connection | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt Generator** | Field winding in **parallel** with armature | Long-line symbol |
| **DC Series Generator** | Field winding in **series** with armature | Dotted/circle symbol |
| **DC Compound Generator** | Both series and shunt windings combined | Combined symbol |

The compound generator is further sub-divided as:
- **Cumulative Compound** ŌĆö series field MMF aids shunt field MMF
- **Differential Compound** ŌĆö series field MMF opposes shunt field MMF

## 1.2 Principle of Operation of DC Motors

A **DC Motor** is a rotating electrical machine that converts **DC electrical energy into mechanical energy** (rotational motion). It operates on the principle of the **Lorentz Force** experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field, governed by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.

### Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (Motor Rule)
> [!NOTE]
> Stretch the **thumb**, **forefinger**, and **middle finger** of the **left hand** mutually perpendicular to each other. The **forefinger** points along the **magnetic field (B)**, the **middle finger** along the **current (I)**, and the **thumb** gives the direction of **force (F)** experienced by the conductor.

The fundamental governing vector equation is:

$$\vec{F} = I (\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$

Where $F$ is the force in **Newtons (N)**, $I$ is the current in **Amperes (A)**, $L$ is the length of the conductor in **metres (m)**, and $B$ is the flux density in **Tesla (T)**.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine two magnets ŌĆö one fixed (the **stator field**) and one free to move (the **rotor / armature**). When you send a controlled DC current through a coil in the rotor, it behaves like an **electromagnet** that is alternately attracted and repelled by the fixed magnet, causing it to spin. This is exactly how a **ceiling fan motor** works ŌĆö the only difference is that fans use single-phase AC that is internally rectified to behave like pulsed DC, but the Lorentz force principle remains the same.

### Key Operational Components of a DC Motor
1. **Yoke (Frame)** ŌĆö Outer cylindrical body, provides mechanical support and carries the magnetic flux. Made of **cast iron or cast steel**.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoes** ŌĆö Made of **laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses. They spread the flux uniformly across the air gap.
3. **Field Windings** ŌĆö Copper coils wound around the pole cores; produce the working flux $\Phi$ (in Weber, Wb).
4. **Armature Core** ŌĆö Laminated **silicon steel** cylinder mounted on the shaft; carries the armature conductors in its slots.
5. **Armature Windings** ŌĆö Closed winding (lap or wave) embedded in armature slots; the current $I_a$ flows through these conductors.
6. **Commutator** ŌĆö Cylindrical structure made of hard-drawn **copper segments** separated by **mica insulation**. Its job is to act as a **mechanical rectifier**, ensuring unidirectional torque.
7. **Brushes** ŌĆö Carbon or graphite blocks that press against the commutator and supply DC current to the armature winding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **commutator and brush assembly** is the heart of every DC machine. It converts the **AC induced in the armature conductors** (due to alternating flux cutting) into **DC at the machine terminals** (in a generator) and conversely, converts **DC supply at the terminals** into **AC within the armature windings** (in a motor).

## 1.3 The Back EMF Concept (Vital for Motor Operation)

When the armature of a DC motor rotates inside the magnetic field, its conductors **cut the magnetic flux**, which (by Faraday's Law) induces an EMF in them. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF always **opposes the cause** ŌĆö i.e., it opposes the applied supply voltage. This counter-EMF is called the **Back EMF ($E_b$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Back EMF is the EMF induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor due to its rotation through the magnetic field, and it acts in opposition to the applied armature voltage.

**Intuition:** Think of it as **electrical inertia**. Just as a moving object resists being accelerated (Newton's First Law), a spinning motor "fights back" against the supply voltage. The faster it spins, the harder it fights. This is precisely what makes the DC motor **self-regulating** in speed.

## 1.4 DC Series Motor ŌĆö Definition and Nomenclature

A **DC Series Motor** is a self-excited DC motor in which the **field winding is connected in series with the armature winding**. Therefore, the same current $I_a$ flows through both the armature and the field winding:

$$I_a = I_{se} = I_L$$

Where $I_{se}$ is the series field current and $I_L$ is the line current drawn from the supply.

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Picture a **tug-of-war contest** where the rope is the armature circuit and the team size is the field strength. In a series motor, the more "rope you pull" (current), the **bigger the team that joins in** (stronger field), and the harder everyone pulls together. This is why series motors produce **enormous starting torque** ŌĆö exactly why they are used in **traction (trains, trams, cranes, elevators)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EMF Equation of a DC Generator / Back EMF Equation of a DC Motor

Let us derive the universal EMF equation that applies to **all rotating DC machines**, whether operating as a generator or a motor.

### Let the symbols denote:
- $P$ ŌåÆ Number of **poles**
- $\Phi$ ŌåÆ Flux per pole in **Weber (Wb)**
- $Z$ ŌåÆ Total number of **armature conductors**
- $N$ ŌåÆ Speed of the armature in **RPM**
- $A$ ŌåÆ Number of **parallel paths** in the armature winding
  - For **Lap winding**: $A = P$
  - For **Wave winding**: $A = 2$

### Average EMF induced per conductor:
According to Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced per conductor is the rate of change of flux linkage. As each conductor cuts a flux of $\Phi$ Weber once per pole-pair revolution:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \text{ volts per conductor}$$

The number of conductors in **series per parallel path** is $\dfrac{Z}{A}$. Therefore, the **net EMF across the brushes** is:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \text{ volts}$$

This is the **Golden KTU Equation** ŌĆö applicable to both DC generators and DC motors. For a motor, we call it $E_b$ (back EMF); for a generator, we call it $E_g$ (generated EMF).

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \text{ Volts}}$$

## 2.2 Voltage and Torque Equations of a DC Motor

### (a) Voltage Equation:
Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature loop of a DC motor (supply $V$, armature resistance $R_a$, brush contact drop neglected initially):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

For a **DC Series Motor**, the total circuit resistance is the armature resistance + series field resistance:

$$V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

### (b) Torque Equation:
The mechanical torque developed by the armature is given by:

$$T_a = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A} \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

A common KTU substitution uses the EMF equation to derive a much simpler and elegant torque expression:

$$\boxed{T_a = 0.1591 \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\frac{P \cdot Z}{A}\right) \text{ N┬Ęm}}$$

Equivalently, using $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$, we can express:

$$T_a = \frac{60}{2\pi N} \cdot E_b \cdot I_a = 9.55 \cdot \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N} \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

## 2.3 Power Equations of a DC Motor

| Quantity | Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Input Power** | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | Watts (W) |
| **Back EMF Power (= Mechanical Power developed)** | $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Watts (W) |
| **Armature Copper Loss** | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Watts (W) |
| **Series Field Copper Loss** | $P_{cu,se} = I_{se}^2 R_{se}$ | Watts (W) |
| **Total Rotational (Iron + Friction + Windage) Loss** | $P_{rot}$ | Watts (W) |
| **Shaft Output Power** | $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s T_{sh}}{60}$ | Watts (W) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any numerical problem on DC series motors in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Condition/Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A}$ | Back EMF / Generated EMF | Universal for all DC machines |
| 2 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Voltage equation | DC Shunt / Separately excited motor |
| 3 | $V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$ | Voltage equation | **DC Series motor** |
| 4 | $T_a \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Armature torque | Universal |
| 5 | $T_a \propto I_a^2$ (series) | Torque vs current | **Before saturation of $\Phi$** |
| 6 | $\Phi \propto I_a$ | Flux relation | **DC Series motor (linear region)** |
| 7 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\Phi}$ | Speed vs flux | At constant $V$ and load |
| 8 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ | Speed vs current | **DC Series motor (linear region)** |
| 9 | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}}$ | Speed vs torque | **DC Series motor (linear region)** |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | Universal |
| 11 | $P_{in} = V I_L$ | Electrical input | Universal motor |
| 12 | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Copper loss in armature | Universal |
| 13 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed | rad/s conversion |
| 14 | $N_s = \dfrac{60 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | For reference (AC machines) |
| 15 | $T_a = 9.55 \cdot \dfrac{E_b I_a}{N}$ | Torque using $E_b$ | Derived form |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $E_b$ and $V$. $V$ is the **applied supply voltage** (terminal voltage). $E_b$ is the **back EMF** that always opposes $V$. **Never** equate them in a motor. Also, for a **generator**, the polarity of $E_b$ is **additive** to $V$ (it's the cause, not the opposition).

## 2.5 Characteristic Curves ŌĆö Conceptual Framework

A **characteristic curve** of a DC motor is a graph that shows the relationship between two performance variables (e.g., speed vs. torque, speed vs. armature current, torque vs. armature current) under **steady-state operating conditions** with **constant supply voltage**.

There are **three fundamental characteristics** of any DC motor:
1. **Electrical (or Armature) Characteristic** ŌĆö $T_a$ vs. $I_a$
2. **Mechanical Characteristic** ŌĆö $N$ vs. $T_a$
3. **Speed Characteristic** ŌĆö $N$ vs. $I_a$

## 2.6 Characteristics of a DC Series Motor ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### 2.6.1 Electrical (TorqueŌĆōCurrent) Characteristic: $T_a$ vs. $I_a$

**Before Magnetic Saturation:**
Up to a certain value of armature current, the iron of the field poles is unsaturated. In this region:

$$\Phi \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_a \propto I_a \cdot \Phi \propto I_a \cdot I_a = I_a^2$$

Hence the $T_a$ vs. $I_a$ curve is a **parabola** opening upward.

**After Magnetic Saturation:**
Once the field iron saturates, $\Phi$ becomes almost **constant** regardless of further increases in $I_a$. In this region:

$$T_a \propto I_a$$

So the curve **becomes a straight line** with a positive slope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The transition from parabolic to linear behavior is the **"knee point"** of the characteristic. The motor's rated operating point is **typically just after** this knee ŌĆö ensuring full exploitation of torque capability while remaining in a stable regime.

**Engineering Insight:** Because $T_a \propto I_a^2$ in the low-current region, the series motor produces an **exceptionally high starting torque** ŌĆö typically **4 to 7 times** the rated torque at starting. This makes it ideal for:
- Electric locomotives and trains
- Cranes, hoists, and elevators
- Trolley buses and trams
- Heavy-duty vehicle starters

### 2.6.2 Mechanical (SpeedŌĆōTorque) Characteristic: $N$ vs. $T_a$

From the voltage equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (neglecting $R_{se}$ for now), and using $E_b \propto \Phi N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi \cdot \text{const}}$$

In the unsaturated region, $\Phi \propto I_a$, so substituting:

$$N \propto \frac{V - I_a R_a}{I_a} \approx \frac{V}{I_a} \cdot \text{const}$$

As $T_a \propto I_a^2$, we have $I_a \propto \sqrt{T_a}$, therefore:

$$N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_a}}$$

The curve is an **inverse square-root function** (hyperbolic shape).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** As load torque $T_a \to 0$ (no-load), $I_a \to 0$, and theoretically $N \to \infty$. In practice, the residual magnetism in the field poles prevents infinite speed, but speeds of **5 to 7 times the rated value** can occur. This is why a **DC series motor must NEVER be started on no-load or with a belt drive** ŌĆö it will mechanically self-destruct. Always couple a series motor to its load **directly (gear-coupled)**.

**After saturation**, $\Phi$ is constant, and $N$ becomes approximately a linear function of $I_a$ (and hence of $T_a$). The curve becomes nearly **straight**, with a small negative slope.

### 2.6.3 SpeedŌĆōCurrent Characteristic: $N$ vs. $I_a$

This is essentially the **inverse** of the torqueŌĆōcurrent characteristic. From $N \propto 1/I_a$ in the unsaturated region, the curve is a **rectangular hyperbola**. As $I_a$ increases, $N$ drops sharply. In the saturated region, the curve becomes nearly linear with a gentle negative slope.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why DC Series Motor? |
|---|---|
| **Traction (Trains, Trams)** | High starting torque, simple speed control via series resistance |
| **Cranes, Hoists, Winches** | Overload capability; torque rises sharply with load |
| **Elevators, Lifts** | Smooth acceleration, high starting pull |
| **Trolleybuses, Forklifts** | Robust, direct load-coupled operation |
| **Automotive Starter Motors** | Extremely high instantaneous starting torque |
| **Hair Dryers, Vacuum Cleaners (Universal motors)** | Series-wound universal motors run on AC or DC |

## 2.8 Comparative Summary ŌĆö Why NOT Shunt Motor for These Applications?

A **DC shunt motor** has a constant flux $\Phi$ (field is across supply, $I_f$ Ōēł constant). Its torque varies linearly with $I_a$ ($T_a \propto I_a$), so it cannot match the quadratic torque rise of a series motor at high loads. Its speed is also nearly constant (good for fans, lathes, pumps), making it unsuitable for traction where variable speed with high starting torque is essential.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: EMF Equation of a DC Machine

**Given:**
- A DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, running at $N$ RPM with flux $\Phi$ Weber per pole.
- The armature winding is arranged into $A$ parallel paths (lap: $A=P$; wave: $A=2$).

### Step 1: Flux cut by one conductor per revolution
Each conductor, in one complete revolution, sweeps past $P$ poles. The total flux cut by one conductor per revolution is:

$$\text{Flux per conductor per rev} = \Phi \cdot P \text{ Weber}$$

### Step 2: Time taken for one revolution
At a speed of $N$ RPM, the time for one revolution is:

$$t = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3: Average EMF per conductor
By Faraday's Law, $e = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$, so the average EMF induced per conductor is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P}{60 / N} = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \text{ volts per conductor}$$

### Step 4: Conductors in series per parallel path
The total number of conductors is $Z$, distributed equally among $A$ parallel paths. Therefore, the number of conductors in **series** per parallel path is:

$$N_s = \frac{Z}{A}$$

### Step 5: Total EMF across the brushes
The parallel paths are in parallel, so the EMF across any path (and hence across the brushes) is the sum of EMFs in series in one path:

$$E_b = e_{avg} \times N_s = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot P \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \text{ Volts}}$$

This is the **fundamental EMF equation of a DC machine** ŌĆö the most tested formula in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Torque Equation of a DC Motor

### Step 1: Force on a single conductor
When a current-carrying conductor of active length $L$ is placed in a magnetic field of density $B$:

$$F_{conductor} = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \text{ Newtons}$$

where $I_c$ is the current carried by one conductor. The total armature current $I_a$ is shared equally among $A$ parallel paths, so:

$$I_c = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

### Step 2: Torque due to one conductor
Each conductor sits on the armature surface at radius $r = \dfrac{D}{2}$ (where $D$ is the armature diameter). The torque per conductor is:

$$T_{conductor} = F_{conductor} \times r = B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot r$$

### Step 3: Sum over all $Z$ conductors
The total armature torque is the sum of torques from all $Z$ conductors. The flux density can be expressed as $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_g}$ where $A_g$ is the air-gap area per pole, and $A_g \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} = \pi D L$ (since there are $P/2$ pole pairs covering the cylindrical air-gap surface). Algebraic simplification yields:

$$T_a = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A} \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

### Step 4: Compact form using back EMF
Starting from the EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A} \Rightarrow \frac{P Z}{A} = \frac{60 \cdot E_b}{\Phi N}$$

Substitute into the torque equation:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \cdot \Phi I_a \cdot \frac{P Z}{A} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi I_a \cdot \frac{60 E_b}{\Phi N} = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{E_b I_a}{N}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = 9.55 \cdot \frac{E_b I_a}{N} \text{ N┬Ęm}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed Equation of a DC Series Motor

Starting from the voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

Substitute the EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A}$:

$$V = \frac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A} + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

Solving for $N$:

$$\frac{\Phi P N Z}{60 A} = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$N = \frac{60 A \left[ V - I_a (R_a + R_{se}) \right]}{\Phi P Z}$$

This is the **exact speed equation**. In the unsaturated region, $\Phi \propto I_a$, so:

$$N = \frac{K_1 V}{I_a} - \frac{K_2 (R_a + R_{se})}{1} \approx \frac{K}{I_a}$$

Where $K_1, K_2, K$ are machine constants. Thus $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ ŌĆö a hyperbolic relation.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

**Problem:** A 250 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of $0.25\ \Omega$ and a series field resistance of $0.15\ \Omega$. At a certain load, the motor draws a current of 50 A and runs at 1200 RPM. Calculate:
1. The back EMF ($E_b$)
2. The developed torque ($T_a$)
3. The mechanical power developed
4. The speed at half-load current (25 A), assuming unsaturated operation

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.25\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.15\ \Omega$
- $I_a = 50$ A
- $N = 1200$ RPM

### Part (a): Back EMF
By the voltage equation for a DC series motor:

$$V = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se})$$

$$E_b = 250 - 50 \times (0.25 + 0.15)$$

$$E_b = 250 - 50 \times 0.40$$

$$E_b = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}$$

### Part (b): Developed Torque
Using the relation $T_a = 9.55 \cdot \dfrac{E_b I_a}{N}$:

$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{230 \times 50}{1200}$$

$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{11500}{1200}$$

$$T_a = 9.55 \times 9.5833$$

$$T_a = 91.52 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

### Part (c): Mechanical Power Developed
$$P_m = E_b \times I_a = 230 \times 50 = 11500 \text{ W} = 11.5 \text{ kW}$$

Verification: $P_m = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 91.52}{60} = 11500$ W Ō£ō

### Part (d): Speed at Half-Load Current
At half-load, $I_a' = 25$ A. In the unsaturated region, $\Phi \propto I_a$, so $\Phi' = \Phi/2$.

Also, the voltage drop changes:
$$E_b' = V - I_a'(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 25 \times 0.40 = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ V}$$

Using $E_b \propto \Phi N$, and the original $E_b = 230$ V at $\Phi, N = 1200$ RPM:

$$\frac{E_b'}{E_b} = \frac{\Phi' N'}{\Phi N}$$

$$\frac{240}{230} = \frac{(\Phi/2) \cdot N'}{\Phi \cdot 1200}$$

$$N' = 1200 \times \frac{240}{230} \times 2$$

$$N' = 1200 \times 2.0869 = 2504.3 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The motor speed has roughly **doubled** when load current was halved. This vividly demonstrates why series motors must **never run at no-load** ŌĆö speeds can become dangerously high.

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor | DC Compound (Cumulative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field connection | Parallel to armature | Series with armature | Both |
| Field current $I_f$ | Independent of $I_a$ | $I_f = I_a$ | Combination |
| Flux $\Phi$ | Approximately constant | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation) | Partial variation |
| $T_a$ vs $I_a$ | $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear) | $T_a \propto I_a^2$ (parabolic) | Between linear and parabolic |
| $N$ vs $I_a$ | $N$ drops slightly (nearly constant) | $N$ drops sharply (hyperbolic) | Moderately drooping |
| $N$ vs $T_a$ | $N$ nearly constant | $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T_a}$ | Moderately drooping |
| Starting torque | Low (1.5├Ś rated) | Very high (4ŌĆō7├Ś rated) | High (2.5ŌĆō4├Ś rated) |
| No-load speed | Safe (slightly above rated) | **Dangerously high ŌĆö must avoid** | Safe (slightly above rated) |
| Speed regulation | Good (0% to 5%) | Poor (highly variable) | Moderate (10% to 15%) |
| Typical application | Lathes, fans, pumps, blowers | Traction, cranes, starters | Presses, elevators, shears |
