### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Explain the principle of operation of a three-phase induction motor in 5ŌĆō6 lines. State the role of slip."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a balanced 3-phase AC supply is applied to a 3-phase stator winding spatially displaced by 120┬░, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** of constant magnitude (3/2 ╬”<sub>m</sub>) and synchronous speed N<sub>s</sub> = 120f/P is produced. This RMF cuts the short-circuited rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's law). The induced current in the rotor interacts with the RMF (Lenz's law) to produce a torque that drags the rotor in the direction of RMF rotation. The rotor accelerates to a steady speed N<sub>r</sub> < N<sub>s</sub>, where the developed torque equals the load torque. The difference between N<sub>s</sub> and N<sub>r</sub> is what sustains the induction ŌĆö quantified as **slip s = (N<sub>s</sub> ŌłÆ N<sub>r</sub>)/N<sub>s</sub>**. Without slip, there is no relative motion, no induced EMF, no current, and hence **no torque**. *(3 marks ŌĆö full credit if the slipŌĆōtorque linkage is clearly stated.)*

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question has *internal choice* between two full-length alternatives. Below, both **Choice A** and **Choice B** are presented as fully-worked 14-mark model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the principle of operation of a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the condition for maximum torque and starting torque. (Module 1, KTU 2024 reference)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**
- The 3-phase stator winding, when energized by a balanced 3-phase supply, produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** rotating at synchronous speed N<sub>s</sub> = 120f/P.
- This RMF links the short-circuited rotor conductors, inducing an EMF E<sub>2</sub> = 4.44 f<sub>r</sub> ╬” k<sub>w2</sub> T<sub>2</sub> where f<sub>r</sub> = sf.
- The induced current I<sub>2</sub> = sE<sub>2</sub>/ŌłÜ(R<sub>2</sub>┬▓ + (sX<sub>2</sub>)┬▓) flows in the rotor.
- By Lenz's law, this current experiences a force in the RMF, producing a torque that accelerates the rotor in the direction of RMF.
- The rotor settles at N<sub>r</sub> < N<sub>s</sub> (slip s > 0) where motor torque = load torque.

**Derivation of Maximum Torque (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}
$$

Differentiating dT/ds = 0 yields the condition:

$$
s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}
$$

Substituting s<sub>m</sub> back:

$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}
$$

**Starting Torque (1 mark):** At s = 1:

$$
T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often forget the factor *3* (for 3-phase) and write the per-phase formula ŌĆö *lose 1 mark*.
> 2. Some students incorrectly write T<sub>max</sub> as depending on R<sub>2</sub> ŌĆö T<sub>max</sub> is **independent of R<sub>2</sub>**; *only s<sub>m</sub> depends on R<sub>2</sub>*.
> 3. Missing units (N┬Ęm) ŌĆö *lose ┬Į mark*.

---

#### (b) **[7 marks]** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase of 0.2 ╬® and 1.0 ╬® respectively. Calculate: (i) slip at maximum torque, (ii) maximum torque, (iii) starting torque. Assume turns ratio of stator to rotor as 1:0.5 and supply voltage per phase = 200 V. (Module 1 numerical, KTU pattern)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** P = 4, f = 50 Hz, R<sub>2</sub> = 0.2 ╬®, X<sub>2</sub> = 1.0 ╬®, V<sub>1</sub> (phase) = 200 V, turns ratio k = N<sub>1</sub>/N<sub>2</sub> = 1/0.5 = 2.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rotor induced EMF referred to stator:**

At standstill (s = 1), approximate E<sub>2</sub>ŌĆ▓ Ōēł V<sub>1</sub> = 200 V (small rotor impedance at start).

**Step 3 ŌĆö (i) Slip at maximum torque:**

$$
s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 \quad \text{(or 20\%)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö (ii) Maximum torque:**

$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times 200^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 1.0} = \frac{120000}{314.16} = 381.97 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö (iii) Starting torque (s = 1):**

$$
T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)} = \frac{3 \times 40000 \times 0.2}{157.08 \times (0.04 + 1.0)} = \frac{24000}{163.36} = 146.94 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** T<sub>st</sub>/T<sub>max</sub> = 146.94/381.97 Ōēł 0.385, consistent with the design (T<sub>st</sub> is always less than T<sub>max</sub> for normal cage IM).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Describe the construction and principle of operation of a synchronous motor. Derive the torque equation and explain the significance of the load angle ╬┤. (Module 1, KTU 2024 reference)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Stator:** 3-phase distributed armature winding identical to that of a 3-phase IM.
- **Rotor:** Two types ŌĆö (i) **Salient-pole** (projecting poles, low speed, hydro/generator applications) and (ii) **Cylindrical/non-salient** (uniform air gap, high speed, turbo applications).
- **Exciter:** DC supply to rotor field winding via slip-rings (or brushless exciter using rotating diodes and a small auxiliary AC exciter on the same shaft).

**Principle of Operation (2 marks):**
- DC excitation of rotor ŌåÆ produces a constant (DC) magnetic field with NŌĆōS poles on the rotor.
- Stator 3-phase supply ŌåÆ RMF at N<sub>s</sub> = 120f/P.
- The rotor poles **lock** with the stator RMF; rotor rotates at **exactly N<sub>s</sub>** (s = 0).
- For a motor to develop torque, the rotor field axis must lag the stator field axis by an angle **╬┤ (load angle / torque angle)** ŌĆö this is a static angular offset, not a speed difference.

**Torque Derivation (2 marks):**
Power input per phase (assuming cylindrical rotor, ignoring Ra):

$$
P_m = \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

For 3-phase:

$$
P_m = \frac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

Torque developed:

$$
T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta
$$

**Significance of ╬┤ (1 mark):**
- ╬┤ is the angle between **E<sub>f</sub>** (internal EMF, in phase with rotor field) and **V** (terminal voltage, in phase with resultant stator flux).
- For 0 < ╬┤ < 90┬░ ŌåÆ **stable motoring operation**.
- At ╬┤ = 90┬░ ŌåÆ **pull-out torque** T<sub>max</sub> = 3VE<sub>f</sub>/(Žē<sub>s</sub>X<sub>s</sub>) ŌĆö stability limit.
- If load torque exceeds T<sub>max</sub>, the motor **loses synchronism** and stalls.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:**
> 1. Writing T = (3V E<sub>f</sub>/X<sub>s</sub>) sin(╬┤) without dividing by Žē<sub>s</sub> ŌĆö wrong units (this is *power*, not torque).
> 2. Confusing "synchronous speed" with "synchronous reactance" ŌĆö X<sub>s</sub> is the reactance, not a speed.

---

#### (b) **[7 marks]** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor operates on a 400 V line. The synchronous reactance is 5 ╬®/phase, and field excitation produces an EMF of 350 V/phase. Calculate: (i) the load angle for a developed power of 10 kW, (ii) the maximum (pull-out) torque. Assume star connection. (Module 1 numerical, KTU pattern)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** P = 6, f = 50 Hz, V<sub>L</sub> = 400 V (line) ŌåÆ V<sub>ph</sub> = 400/ŌłÜ3 = 230.94 V, E<sub>f</sub> = 350 V, X<sub>s</sub> = 5 ╬®, P<sub>m</sub> = 10 kW.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö (i) Load angle for P<sub>m</sub> = 10 kW:**

From the developed power equation:

$$
P_m = \frac{3 V_{ph} E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

$$
10000 = \frac{3 \times 230.94 \times 350}{5} \sin\delta
$$

$$
10000 = \frac{242487}{5} \sin\delta = 48497.4 \, \sin\delta
$$

$$
\sin\delta = \frac{10000}{48497.4} = 0.2062
$$

$$
\delta = \sin^{-1}(0.2062) = 11.89┬░ \approx 11.9┬░
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö (ii) Pull-out (maximum) torque:**

At ╬┤ = 90┬░:

$$
T_{max} = \frac{3 V_{ph} E_f}{\omega_s X_s} = \frac{3 \times 230.94 \times 350}{104.72 \times 5}
$$

$$
T_{max} = \frac{242487}{523.6} = 463.13 \text{ N┬Ęm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** ╬┤ Ōēł 11.9┬░ is well within the stability margin (0 to 90┬░). The motor can handle load surges up to 463 N┬Ęm without losing synchronism.

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö High-Loss Areas

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in 3-phase torque equations (cost: 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing synchronous speed with synchronous reactance** in synchronous motor derivations (cost: 1ŌĆō2 marks).
> 3. **Writing torque in N┬Ęm without showing the division by Žē<sub>s</sub>** ŌĆö units error (cost: ┬Į mark).
> 4. **Failing to clearly link Lenz's law to induction motor operation** ŌĆö examiners expect the phrase *"Lenz's law ŌĆö the rotor is dragged in the direction of relative motion"* (cost: 1 mark).
> 5. **Not mentioning slip explicitly** when discussing 3-Žå IM ŌĆö slip is the "key concept" of induction (cost: 1 mark).

---

## ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1 (Applications, AC Machines ŌĆö Principle of Operation):**

- ŌśÉ **Electric drive** = source + converter + motor + control + load. Not just the motor.
- ŌśÉ **Industries using drives:** Steel, paper, cement, textile, traction, machine tools, EV, process, renewable.
- ŌśÉ **3-Žå IM** is the workhorse ŌĆö cage IM most common; slip-ring for high starting torque.
- ŌśÉ **RMF** is produced by balanced 3-phase stator supply ŌĆö magnitude (3/2)╬”<sub>m</sub>, speed N<sub>s</sub> = 120f/P.
- ŌśÉ **Induction requires slip** ŌĆö without slip (s = 0), no torque.
- ŌśÉ **Faraday + Lenz** = working principle: induced EMF + opposing torque.
- ŌśÉ **Torque eqn** $T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$ ŌĆö must remember the 3.
- ŌśÉ **Max torque** is independent of R<sub>2</sub>; **slip at max torque** is directly proportional to R<sub>2</sub>.
- ŌśÉ **Synchronous motor** runs at constant N<sub>s</sub> (no slip) ŌĆö over-excited mode ŌåÆ leading pf.
- ŌśÉ **Synchronous motor torque** $T = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ ŌĆö pull-out at ╬┤ = 90┬░.
- ŌśÉ **Single-phase IM** produces *pulsating* flux, not rotating ŌĆö needs auxiliary winding/capacitor for self-start.
- ŌśÉ **Double revolving field theory (Ferraris)** explains zero starting torque in single-phase IM.
- ŌśÉ **Types of single-phase IM:** Split-phase, capacitor-start, capacitor-run, shaded-pole, universal.
- ŌśÉ **Universal motor** (AC + DC) ŌĆö used in mixers, drills, blenders; high speed, high power density.
- ŌśÉ **Motor choice for drives:** depends on (i) starting torque, (ii) speed range, (iii) power factor, (iv) efficiency, (v) cost.
- ŌśÉ **~65ŌĆō70% of industrial electrical energy in India** is consumed by electric drives ŌĆö *commonly asked fact*.
- ŌśÉ **Empirical design rules to memorize:**
  - Cage IM ŌåÆ simple, robust, low maintenance.
  - Slip-ring IM ŌåÆ high starting torque, used in cranes, hoists.
  - Synchronous ŌåÆ constant speed, pf correction.
  - PMSM/BLDC ŌåÆ servo, robotics, EV.
  - Capacitor-start single-phase ŌåÆ compressors, washing machines.
  - Shaded-pole ŌåÆ cheap fans, hair dryers, small blowers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotating Magnetic Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rotating Magnetic Field ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF):** A magnetic field of **constant magnitude** that **rotates continuously in space at a uniform angular velocity** (known as the **synchronous speed**, $N_s$) is called a Rotating Magnetic Field. It is the foundational operating principle of all polyphase AC machines, including **three-phase induction motors**, **synchronous motors**, and **brushless DC drives**. Mathematically, a vector field is considered "rotating" if its tip traces a **circle of constant radius** at a uniform angular velocity $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ rad/s. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a bar magnet at the center of a clock. Now, instead of keeping the magnet still, suppose the magnet itself spins smoothly like the second-hand of a clock. Any small iron filing placed near the clock face will experience a changing magnetic field, and if the iron filing is free to rotate (like the rotor of an induction motor), it will be dragged along in the same direction as the spinning magnet. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a Rotating Magnetic Field as a **magnetic "whirlpool"** in the stator of an AC machine. The three-phase AC supply acts like three hands pushing the whirlpool in sequence (one hand at a time, every 60┬░), creating a smooth, continuous spin. The rotor (the floating object) just gets pulled along by this invisible whirlpool. ### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - The electrical supply frequency in India is **$f = 50\ \text{Hz}$** (industrial standard). - The corresponding synchronous angular velocity in mechanical rad/s for a 2-pole machine is $\omega_s = 2\pi f = 314.16\ \text{rad/s}$. - Synchronous mechanical speed for a 2-pole, 50 Hz machine is **$N_s = 3000\ \text{RPM}$**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Essential Conditions for the Production of an RMF For a balanced three-phase system, the following **three mandatory conditions** must be satisfied: 1. **Balanced Three-Phase Supply:** Three identical AC voltages, displaced by **$120┬░$ electrical** in time, must be applied. The phasors are $\mathbf{V}_a$, $\mathbf{V}_b$, $\mathbf{V}_c$. 2. **Spatially Displaced Windings:** The three stator windings (phases A, B, C) must be **physically displaced by $120┬░$ electrical in space** around the air-gap periphery. 3. **Identical Windings:** Each of the three-phase coils must have an **equal number of turns**, identical resistance, and identical geometric distribution ŌĆö ensuring equal MMF magnitudes per phase. ### 2.2 Operating Logic ŌĆö The "Why" and "How" - **Why 120┬░?** Because a full mechanical revolution of the field must be completed in **one electrical cycle ($360┬░$ in time)**, and the three phases must take **equal shares** of producing this rotation. Each phase contributes $120┬░$ of rotation sequentially. - **How it rotates:** At any instant, one phase produces a peak MMF. After $60┬░$ in time, the next phase peaks, and so on. The resultant MMF vector "jumps" smoothly, tracing a **circle of constant radius** equal to $\dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$. - **The "Constant Magnitude" Property:** Although individual phase fluxes oscillate, the vector sum of all three is **time-invariant in magnitude** ŌĆö only its **direction** changes. This is what distinguishes a true RMF from a simple pulsating field. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Unit / Value | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Synchronous Speed (RPM) | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | $\text{RPM}$ | | 2 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | | 3 | Electrical to Mechanical Angle | $\theta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{2}{P}\,\theta_{\text{elec}}$ | $\text{rad}$ | | 4 | Phase A Instantaneous Current | $i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $\text{A}$ | | 5 | Phase B Instantaneous Current | $i_b = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 120┬░\right)$ | $\text{A}$ | | 6 | Phase C Instantaneous Current | $i_c = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 240┬░\right)$ | $\text{A}$ | | 7 | Resultant Flux Magnitude | $\vert\Phi_R\vert = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m$ | $\text{Wb}$ | | 8 | Slip (used in induction motors) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per unit (p.u.) | | 9 | Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | $\text{RPM}$ | | 10 | Slip Frequency | $f_r = s\,f$ | $\text{Hz}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for table entries:** For absolute values like magnitude of flux, the symbol $\vert\,\cdot\,\vert$ is used in the formula column without breaking the markdown table structure. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Industrial Induction Motors:** Every 3-phase squirrel-cage or slip-ring induction motor ŌĆö from those driving conveyor belts in factories to those in large pumping stations ŌĆö operates **solely on the principle of RMF**. - **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Modern EV traction drives (e.g., Tesla Model 3 drivetrain) use **three-phase PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors)** whose stator is excited by inverter-generated RMF. - **HVAC and Compressors:** Centrifugal chillers and large air-conditioning compressors rely on RMF-based motors. - **Wind Turbine Generators:** Doubly-fed induction generators (DFIG) used in wind energy conversion systems operate on RMF principles for both motoring and generating modes. - **Maglev Trains:** Linear versions of RMF (called "traveling magnetic fields") are used to levitate and propel high-speed maglev systems like the Shanghai Transrapid. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Setup of the Mathematical Model Let the three-phase stator windings be physically oriented such that: - Phase **A** axis is along the **reference x-axis** ($\theta = 0┬░$) - Phase **B** axis is at $\theta = +120┬░$ from x-axis - Phase **C** axis is at $\theta = +240┬░$ from x-axis The three-phase balanced currents are: $$ i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t) $$ $$ i_b = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 120┬░\right) $$ $$ i_c = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 240┬░\right) $$ Each phase produces a flux that is **proportional to its instantaneous current** (assuming a linear magnetic circuit). Let $\Phi_m = k\,I_m$ be the peak flux per phase. ### 3.2 Flux Components Along Each Phase Axis The flux phasors at time $t$ are: $$ \Phi_a = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(along } 0┬░ \text{)} $$ $$ \Phi_b = \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 120┬░\right) \quad \text{(along } 120┬░ \text{)} $$ $$ \Phi_c = \Phi_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - 240┬░\right) \quad \text{(along } 240┬░ \text{)} $$ ### 3.3 Resolving into Orthogonal (X and Y) Components We resolve each flux phasor into its **x-component** and **y-component** using standard trigonometry. **X-axis component** (horizontal): $$ \Phi_x = \Phi_a \cos(0┬░) + \Phi_b \cos(120┬░) + \Phi_c \cos(240┬░) $$ **Y-axis component** (vertical): $$ \Phi_y = \Phi_a \sin(0┬░) + \Phi_b \sin(120┬░) + \Phi_c \sin(240┬░) $$ ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Expansion of $\Phi_x$ Expanding the x-component term-by-term: $$ \Phi_x = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)(1) + \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) $$ Factoring out $\Phi_m$: $$ \Phi_x = \Phi_m\!\left[\sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t - 240┬░)\right] $$ Applying the **sum-to-product identity** $\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$: $$ \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) + \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = 2\sin(\omega t - 180┬░)\cos(60┬░) $$ $$ = 2\!\left(-\sin(\omega t)\right)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\sin(\omega t) $$ Substituting back: $$ \Phi_x = \Phi_m\!\left[\sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(-\sin(\omega t)\right)\right] = \Phi_m\!\left[\sin(\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t)\right] $$ $$ \boxed{\;\Phi_x = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\;} $$ ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Expansion of $\Phi_y$ Expanding the y-component term-by-term: $$ \Phi_y = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)(0) + \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)\!\left(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) $$ Factoring out $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m$: $$ \Phi_y = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\Phi_m\!\left[\sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)\right] $$ Applying the **difference identity** $\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$: $$ \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = 2\cos(\omega t - 180┬░)\sin(60┬░) $$ $$ = 2\!\left(-\cos(\omega t)\right)\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -\sqrt{3}\,\cos(\omega t) $$ Substituting back: $$ \Phi_y = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\Phi_m\!\left[-\sqrt{3}\,\cos(\omega t)\right] = -\dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m \cos(\omega t) $$ $$ \boxed{\;\Phi_y = -\dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)\;} $$ ### 3.6 Magnitude of the Resultant Flux The magnitude is: $$ \vert\Phi_R\vert = \sqrt{\Phi_x^{\,2} + \Phi_y^{\,2}} $$ $$ = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\right)^{\!2} + \left(-\dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)\right)^{\!2}} $$ $$ = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m \sqrt{\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t)} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m \sqrt{1} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\vert\Phi_R\vert = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m = \text{constant}\;} $$ ### 3.7 Angular Position (Direction) of the Resultant The angle that $\Phi_R$ makes with the x-axis is: $$ \tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\Phi_y}{\Phi_x} = \dfrac{-\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)}{\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)} = -\cot(\omega t) $$ Equivalent expression using sine/cosine inversion: $$ \theta = -90┬░ - \omega t $$ Differentiating with respect to time: $$ \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\omega $$ The negative sign simply indicates the direction of rotation (clockwise with the assumed phase sequence A-B-C). The **angular velocity is constant and equal to $\omega$**. ### 3.8 Summary of Derived Resultant Flux Vector $$ \mathbf{\Phi}_R(t) = \Phi_x\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \Phi_y\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} $$ $$ \mathbf{\Phi}_R(t) = \dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} - \dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The resultant flux has a **constant magnitude of $\dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$** and **rotates at synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s**. This proves mathematically that a balanced three-phase supply applied to a balanced three-phase winding produces a **perfect Rotating Magnetic Field**. ### 3.9 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation) **Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole induction motor is supplied from a 50 Hz, 3-phase AC source. Calculate the synchronous speed, synchronous angular velocity, and the resultant flux magnitude if the peak flux per phase is $\Phi_m = 0.05\ \text{Wb}$. **Solution:** Using the standard formula: $$ N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{RPM} $$ Angular velocity in mechanical rad/s: $$ \omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s} $$ Resultant flux magnitude: $$ \vert\Phi_R\vert = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_m = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 0.05 = 0.075\ \text{Wb} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Numerical Answers:** $N_s = 1500\ \text{RPM}$, $\omega_s \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$, and $\vert\Phi_R\vert = 0.075\ \text{Wb}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RMF Generation Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **signal and power flow** from the three-phase source through the spatially-displaced windings to the resultant rotating magnetic field in the air gap. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SOURCE
Three Phase 50 Hz AC Supply] --> B[PHASE A WINDING
Spatially at 0 degrees] A --> C[PHASE B WINDING
Spatially at 120 degrees] A --> D[PHASE C WINDING
Spatially at 240 degrees] B --> E[AIR GAP FLUX PHI A
Peaks at wt equals 90 deg] C --> F[AIR GAP FLUX PHI B
Peaks at wt equals 210 deg] D --> G[AIR GAP FLUX PHI C
Peaks at wt equals 330 deg] E --> H[VECTOR SUMMATION
Resolving into X and Y axes] F --> H G --> H H --> I[RESULTANT FLUX PHI R
Magnitude equals 1.5 Phi m] I --> J[ROTATING MAGNETIC FIELD
Rotates at Ns equals 120f by P RPM] J --> K[ROTOR RESPONSE
Induction Motor Torque Produced] ``` ### 4.2 Phasor-Combination Logic at Six Key Time Instants ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 0 deg] --> P0[Phase A equals 0
Phase B equals Negative Peak
Phase C equals Positive Peak] T60[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 60 deg] --> P60[Phase A equals Positive
Phase B equals Negative Half
Phase C equals Positive Half] T120[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 120 deg] --> P120[Phase A equals Positive Peak
Phase B equals 0
Phase C equals Negative Peak] T180[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 180 deg] --> P180[Phase A equals 0
Phase B equals Positive
Phase C equals Negative Half] T240[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 240 deg] --> P240[Phase A equals Negative Peak
Phase B equals Positive Half
Phase C equals 0] T300[TIME INSTANT
wt equals 300 deg] --> P300[Phase A equals Negative Half
Phase B equals Positive Peak
Phase C equals Positive Half] P0 --> SUM[VECTOR SUMMATION AT EACH INSTANT] P60 --> SUM P120 --> SUM P180 --> SUM P240 --> SUM P300 --> SUM SUM --> RES[RESULTANT VECTOR TIPS TRACE A CIRCLE OF RADIUS 1.5 PHI M] ``` ### 4.3 Direction of Rotation Convention ```mermaid flowchart TD SEQ{Phase Sequence} SEQ -- ABC Positive Sequence --> CW[Anticlockwise Rotation
Standard Convention] SEQ -- ACB Negative Sequence --> CCW[Clockwise Rotation
Reverse Direction] CW --> APPL[APPLICATION
Pumps Fans Compressors Conveyor Motors] CCW --> APPL2[APPLICATION
Phase Interchange Used to Reverse Motor Direction] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** To reverse the direction of rotation of any three-phase induction motor, simply **interchange any two of the three supply lines** (e.g., swap B and C). This reverses the phase sequence from ABC to ACB, reversing the RMF. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Define Rotating Magnetic Field. State the formula for synchronous speed of a three-phase induction motor. **Model Answer:** A Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) is a magnetic field of **constant magnitude** that rotates continuously in space at a uniform angular velocity called the **synchronous speed** ($N_s$). It is produced when a balanced three-phase AC supply is fed to three spatially-displaced (120┬░ apart) stator windings. The synchronous speed is given by: $$ N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}\ \text{RPM} $$ where $f$ is the supply frequency in Hz and $P$ is the total number of poles. *(3 marks ŌĆö full credit requires both the definition and the formula with all variables defined.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]** **Q2.** State any three essential conditions for the production of a rotating magnetic field in a three-phase system. **Model Answer:** The three essential conditions are: 1. The system must be supplied with a **balanced three-phase AC source** (equal magnitudes, displaced by 120┬░ electrical in time). 2. The three stator windings must be **physically displaced by 120┬░ electrical in space** around the air-gap. 3. The three windings must be **identical** in terms of turns, resistance, and geometric distribution so that equal MMF magnitudes are produced. *(3 marks ŌĆö one mark per correct condition.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Paper]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat phasor diagram, explain the production of a rotating magnetic field in a three-phase induction motor. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Consider three identical stator windings **A, B, and C**, displaced **120┬░ in space** around the inner periphery of the stator. They are fed by a balanced 3-phase supply: $$ i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t),\quad i_b = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░),\quad i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) $$ **At $\omega t = 0┬░$:** $i_a = 0$, $i_b = -I_m \sin(120┬░) = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}I_m$, $i_c = +\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}I_m$. The resultant flux points along the **negative y-axis** (call it the $-90┬░$ direction). **At $\omega t = 60┬░$:** $i_a = +\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}I_m$, $i_b = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}I_m$, $i_c = 0$. The resultant flux now points along the **$-30┬░$ direction** ŌĆö it has rotated by **60┬░ in 60┬░ of time**, i.e., it rotates at synchronous speed. **At $\omega t = 120┬░$:** Resultant flux rotates to **+30┬░** direction. Continuing this analysis for six instants ($\omega t = 0┬░, 60┬░, 120┬░, 180┬░, 240┬░, 300┬░$), the tip of the resultant flux vector traces a **perfect circle of radius $\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$**, completing one full revolution in one electrical cycle ($T = 1/f$ seconds). This confirms the **rotating nature** of the magnetic field, which drags the rotor along in the same direction. **(b)** Derive the expression for synchronous speed of a rotating magnetic field. A 6-pole, 50 Hz three-phase induction motor runs at 960 RPM. Calculate its slip and rotor frequency. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Let $f$ be the supply frequency and $P$ be the number of poles. In one cycle, the RMF advances by **two pole-pitches** (i.e., 2 pole-pairs). Therefore, the number of revolutions per second is: $$ \dfrac{N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2f}{P} $$ $$ \boxed{\;N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}\ \text{RPM}\;} $$ **Numerical calculation:** Given: $P = 6$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $N_r = 960\ \text{RPM}$. Synchronous speed: $$ N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\ \text{RPM} $$ Slip (per unit): $$ s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1000 - 960}{1000} = 0.04\ \text{p.u.} $$ Rotor (slip) frequency: $$ f_r = s \times f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2\ \text{Hz} $$ **Final Answers:** $s = 0.04\ \text{p.u.}$, $f_r = 2\ \text{Hz}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **(a)** Starting from the instantaneous phase currents, derive the expression for the resultant flux in a three-phase winding and show that its magnitude is constant. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3** of these notes. The key steps are: 1. Define spatially-displaced phase axes: A at $0┬░$, B at $120┬░$, C at $240┬░$. 2. Write instantaneous fluxes: $\Phi_a = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$, etc. 3. Resolve into x and y components. 4. Show that $\Phi_x = \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ and $\Phi_y = -\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$. 5. Compute magnitude: $\vert\Phi_R\vert = \sqrt{\Phi_x^2 + \Phi_y^2} = \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m = \text{constant}$. **The magnitude is constant at $\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$**, independent of time $t$. Hence proved. **(b)** A three-phase, 8-pole induction motor is supplied from a 60 Hz source. Calculate the synchronous speed, synchronous angular velocity in rad/s, and the mechanical angle (in degrees) rotated by the RMF in $1/60$ th of a second. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Given: $P = 8$, $f = 60\ \text{Hz}$. Synchronous speed: $$ N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 60}{8} = 900\ \text{RPM} $$ Synchronous angular velocity in rad/s: $$ \omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 30\pi \approx 94.25\ \text{rad/s} $$ Time interval: $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{60}\ \text{s}$. Mechanical angle rotated: $$ \Delta\theta = \omega_s \times \Delta t = 30\pi \times \dfrac{1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 90┬░\ \text{(mechanical)} $$ **Final Answers:** $N_s = 900\ \text{RPM}$, $\omega_s \approx 94.25\ \text{rad/s}$, $\Delta\theta = 90┬░$ (mechanical). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls ŌĆö Avoid These!** > > 1. **Forgetting the constant $\tfrac{3}{2}$ factor:** Many students write the resultant flux as just $\Phi_m$ instead of $\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$. This factor arises from the spatial $120┬░$ displacement and is **frequently asked** in KTU exams. Lose **2 marks** if missing. > 2. **Mixing up electrical vs. mechanical angle:** The $120┬░$ in time-domain currents is **electrical**; the $120┬░$ spatial displacement of windings is also **electrical**. They become mechanical angles via $\theta_{\text{mech}} = \tfrac{2}{P}\theta_{\text{elec}}$. Marks are lost when this distinction is ignored. > 3. **Skipping the constant-magnitude proof:** KTU examiners specifically award **2ŌĆō3 marks** for showing $\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t) = 1$ leading to constant magnitude. Do not omit this step. > 4. **Synchronous speed formula units:** $N_s$ must be expressed in **RPM**, not RPS. The factor of 120 comes from converting from RPS to RPM. > 5. **Sign convention in the y-component:** The minus sign in $\Phi_y = -\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ indicates the **direction of rotation**. Do not drop it. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **RMF Definition:** Constant magnitude + uniform angular velocity = Rotating Magnetic Field. - **Three Mandatory Conditions:** Balanced 3-phase supply, spatially 120┬░ displaced windings, identical windings. - **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ ŌĆö this is the speed of the RMF, **not** the rotor. - **Rotor Speed Relation:** $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$, where $s$ is slip (always between 0 and 1 for motoring). - **Resultant Flux Magnitude:** $\vert\Phi_R\vert = \dfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m$ ŌĆö the $\tfrac{3}{2}$ factor is the hallmark of a balanced 3-phase system. - **Direction of Rotation:** Determined by the phase sequence (ABC = anticlockwise, ACB = clockwise in standard convention). - **Reversal Method:** Interchange any two of the three stator supply leads to reverse the RMF. - **Slip Frequency:** $f_r = s \times f$ ŌĆö this is the frequency of induced EMF and current in the rotor. - **Industrial Relevance:** Every three-phase induction motor, PMSM, and BLDC drive operates on the RMF principle ŌĆö the most important single concept in AC electric drives. - **Examination Hot-Spots:** Numerical problems on $N_s$, $s$, $f_r$, $\omega_s$, and derivations of $\Phi_x$, $\Phi_y$, and $\vert\Phi_R\vert$ are **guaranteed questions** in every KTU ESE.

topics.title: Single Phase and Three Phase Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Phase and Three Phase Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Induction Motor ŌĆö A Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> An **induction motor** is a singly-excited, self-starting (in 3-phase form) AC machine that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy through electromagnetic induction. The stator carries the only DC-free AC supply; the rotor receives its excitation purely by transformer action from the stator's rotating magnetic field ŌĆö hence the name *induction* motor.

The two principal families treated in Module 1 are:

| Variant | Supply | Self-Starting? | Construction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Three-Phase Induction Motor (3-Žå IM)** | 3-phase, balanced | Yes (inherently) | Squirrel-cage or Wound (Slip-ring) rotor |
| **Single-Phase Induction Motor (1-Žå IM)** | 1-phase | No (auxiliary winding needed) | Squirrel-cage rotor + auxiliary start winding |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Why a Magnetic Field *Rotates*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (The Spinning Magnet Analogy)**
> Imagine three horses pulling a merry-go-round, each horse tied at 120┬░ apart, and they each pull hardest in turn ŌĆö Horse-A pulls, then Horse-B 1/3 of a second later, then Horse-C 1/3 second after that. To a child sitting on the merry-go-round, the *net* pull is not pulsing ŌĆö it feels like a single, smooth, constant-strength force *chasing him around the circle*. That is exactly what the three stator windings of a 3-Žå IM do with magnetic flux: the *resultant* field is constant in magnitude and **rotates** at synchronous speed.

For a 1-Žå supply, only one winding is excited ŌĆö it produces an **oscillating (pulsating) field**, not a rotating one. Hence the rotor is *not* self-starting. The pulsating field is mathematically equivalent to two counter-rotating fields of equal magnitude ŌĆö this is **Double Revolving Field Theory**, and a 1-Žå IM must be given a synthetic rotating component to start.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise these standard reference values (used in all KTU numericals):**
> - **Line voltage, India LV:** $415 \text{ V}$, 3-phase, **$50 \text{ Hz}$**
> - **Phase voltage:** $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3} \approx 239.6 \text{ V}$
> - **Synchronous speed (50 Hz):** $N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}$ (4-pole), $1000 \text{ rpm}$ (6-pole), $750 \text{ rpm}$ (8-pole)
> - **Full-load slip range (industrial 3-Žå IM):** $s_{fl} \approx 0.02 - 0.05$ (i.e., **2 % to 5 %**)
> - **Rotor conductor material:** Copper or Aluminium (cage)

---

## 1.4 Why the Induction Motor Dominates Industry

> [!NOTE]
> Nearly **85ŌĆō90 %** of all industrial AC motors in the world are 3-phase induction motors. Reasons: rugged construction, no brushes/commutator, low cost, direct-on-line start capability, and excellent speed regulation under load.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a 3-Phase Induction Motor

Two mechanical assemblies separated by a narrow **air-gap** (typically $0.4 - 1.0 \text{ mm}$):

### A. Stator (Stationary Part)
- **Core:** Laminated silicon-steel stamping stacks (thickness $0.35 - 0.5 \text{ mm}$) to suppress eddy currents.
- **Slots:** Inner periphery has semi-closed slots housing a **3-phase distributed winding**.
- **Windings:** The three phases $A-A'$, $B-B'$, $C-C'$ are **spatially displaced by 120┬░ electrical**; the *return* paths are displaced by 180┬░ from their *go* paths.

### B. Rotor (Rotating Part)
1. **Squirrel-Cage Rotor** ŌĆö Copper/Aluminium bars shorted at both ends by end-rings. No external electrical connection. Cheapest, most rugged, almost maintenance-free.
2. **Wound (Slip-Ring) Rotor** ŌĆö Has a 3-phase insulated winding similar to the stator, with terminals brought out to three slip rings for external resistance control.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle ŌĆö Step by Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three "Golden Steps" of an Induction Motor (must reproduce verbatim in KTU answers):**
> 1. A 3-phase stator supply produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** of constant magnitude $1.5\,\phi_m$ rotating at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f / P$.
> 2. The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, **inducing an EMF** in them by Faraday's law (relative velocity is the slip speed).
> 3. Since the rotor bars are short-circuited, the induced EMF drives a **rotor current**, which interacts with the RMF to produce a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ on each conductor, yielding a **torque** in the direction of the RMF.

---

## 2.3 The Concept of **Slip ($s$)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Slip):** Slip is the fractional difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed.
> $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}, \quad \text{or} \quad s = \frac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s}$$

- At **standstill** (just switched on): $N_r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 1$
- At **no-load** (ideal): $N_r \approx N_s \Rightarrow s \approx 0$
- At **full-load**: $s \approx 0.02 - 0.05$
- For reverse rotation, $N_r$ is negative, making $s > 1$.

---

## 2.4 Rotor Circuit Frequency & Effective Resistance

When rotor rotates at $N_r$, the rotor conductors *see* the RMF at a *relative* speed of $(N_s - N_r) = sN_s$. Therefore:

- **Rotor frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$
- **Rotor EMF (running):** $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ (where $E_2$ is standstill rotor EMF/phase)
- **Rotor reactance (running):** $X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2$
- **Equivalent rotor circuit resistance at slip $s$:** $\frac{R_2}{s} = R_2 + R_2\left(\frac{1-s}{s}\right)$ ŌĆö the extra term $\frac{R_2(1-s)}{s}$ represents the **mechanical load** (power converted to shaft work).

---

## 2.5 Torque Equation of a 3-Phase Induction Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The master torque formula (KEEP for all KTU derivations):**
> $$\boxed{T_d = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^{\,2} \, R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}}}$$
> where $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ rad/s (electrical angular velocity of the RMF).

An equivalent and more practical version uses the **Thevenin equivalent** of the stator:

$$T_d = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^{2} \cdot (R_2 / s)}{(R_{th} + R_2/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}}$$

---

## 2.6 Maximum Torque & Slip at Max Torque

Setting $\frac{dT_d}{ds} = 0$ yields:

- **Slip at maximum torque:** $\displaystyle s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$
- **Maximum torque:** $\displaystyle T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{\,2}}{2 X_2}$

> [!TIP]
> **Key engineering insight:** $T_{max}$ is *independent of rotor resistance $R_2$*, but $s_{mT}$ is *directly proportional* to $R_2$. This is precisely why adding external rotor resistance (in slip-ring motors) shifts the maximum-torque point towards higher slip ŌĆö improving **starting torque** without reducing the maximum torque magnitude.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table ŌĆö every KTU numerical or derivation question will draw from it.**

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | $P$ = total poles (always even) |
| Rotor (actual) speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | rpm |
| Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu (0 to 1, normally) |
| Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \, f$ | Hz |
| Rotor EMF (running) | $E_{2r} = s \, E_2$ | V/phase |
| Rotor reactance (running) | $X_{2r} = s \, X_2$ | ╬®/phase |
| Rotor current | $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}}}$ | A/phase |
| Air-gap power | $P_g = 3 I_2^{\,2} \dfrac{R_2}{s}$ | W |
| Mechanical (gross) power | $P_m = (1 - s) P_g = 3 I_2^{\,2} R_2 \dfrac{1-s}{s}$ | W |
| Rotor Cu-loss | $P_{cu2} = s \, P_g$ | W |
| Developed torque | $T_d = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_r}$ | N┬Ęm |
| Slip at $T_{max}$ | $s_{mT} = R_2 / X_2$ | pu |
| Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{\,2}}{2 X_2}$ | N┬Ęm |
| Starting torque ($s = 1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + X_2^{\,2}}$ | N┬Ęm |
| Starting current (approx.) | $I_{st} \approx 5 \text{ to } 7$ times full-load current | A |

---

## 2.8 TorqueŌĆōSlip Characteristics ŌĆö What the Curve Tells You

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The torque-slip curve has **three regions** ŌĆö every KTU answer on characteristics MUST mention them:
> 1. **Low-slip region ($0 < s < s_{mT}$):** $T \propto s$ (linear, "stable operating region").
> 2. **Maximum-torque point ($s = s_{mT}$):** Pull-out torque ŌĆö operation beyond this is unstable.
> 3. **High-slip region ($s_{mT} < s < 1$):** $T \propto 1/s$ (motoring ŌåÆ braking/plugging zone).

For a 1-Žå IM, the curve is *symmetric about $s = 0$* (because forward and backward rotating fields produce equal-and-opposite torques at standstill), so the **net starting torque is zero**.

---

## 2.9 Single-Phase Induction Motor ŌĆö Double Revolving Field Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** A single-phase alternating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be **mathematically resolved** into two rotating fluxes of equal magnitude $\phi_m / 2$, rotating in opposite directions at synchronous speed.
> $$\phi(t) = \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)}_{\text{Forward (F)}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)}_{\text{Backward (B)}}$$

- The forward field produces torque $T_f$ in the direction of rotation.
- The backward field produces torque $T_b$ in the opposite direction.
- **At standstill ($s_f = s_b = 1$):** $T_f = T_b \Rightarrow T_{net} = 0$ ŌĆö hence no self-starting.
- **Once spun manually** (say clockwise), $s_f \to 0$, $T_f$ rises; $s_b \to 2$, $T_b$ falls ŌåÆ net torque builds in the spin direction.

---

## 2.10 Starting Methods for 1-Phase Induction Motors

| Method | Auxiliary Phase Shift | Typical Application | Start Capacitor Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Split-Phase (Resistance Start)** | 25┬░ ŌĆō 30┬░ | Fans, blowers, small pumps | None ŌĆö uses high-R auxiliary winding |
| **Capacitor-Start** | ~80┬░ ŌĆō 90┬░ | Compressors, conveyors (high starting torque) | $30 - 400 \text{ ╬╝F}$, electrolytic, short-rated |
| **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run** | ~90┬░ start + ~90┬░ run | Pumps, machine tools | Two capacitors ŌĆö start (C electrolytic) + run (C paper) |
| **Shaded-Pole** | Mechanical flux delay | Small fans, hair dryers, clocks | None ŌĆö copper ring creates delay |
| **Permanent Capacitor (Capacitor-Run)** | ~90┬░ continuously | Ceiling fans | $2 - 25 \text{ ╬╝F}$, paper-type, continuous duty |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **3-Žå IM** drives: pumps, compressors, conveyor belts, elevators, CNC spindles, rolling mills, electric trains, cement kilns, paper machines.
- **1-Žå IM** drives: ceiling fans, washing machines (capacitor-run), refrigerator compressors (capacitor-start), mixer-grinders, domestic water pumps.
- **EV traction context:** Modern EVs use 3-Žå squirrel-cage IMs (e.g., Tesla Model S, Tata Nexon EV) as high-efficiency traction drives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rotating Magnetic Field from a 3-Phase Winding

### Setup
Let the three stator phase axes be **displaced by 120┬░ in space** along the inner surface of the stator. Let the three balanced currents be **displaced by 120┬░ in time**:

$$i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$i_b = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$
$$i_c = I_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$$

Each winding produces a flux along its own axis, proportional to the instantaneous current. If the spatial angle from the $a$-axis is $\theta$ (in electrical radians), the **resultant flux density** at any point in the air-gap is:

$$B(\theta, t) = B_a(\theta, t) + B_b(\theta, t) + B_c(\theta, t)$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

$$B(\theta, t) = B_m \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta) + B_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + B_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$$

Using the product-to-sum identity $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$:

$$B(\theta, t) = \tfrac{B_m}{2}\big[\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\big]$$
$$+ \tfrac{B_m}{2}\big[\sin(\omega t + \theta - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\big]$$
$$+ \tfrac{B_m}{2}\big[\sin(\omega t + \theta - \tfrac{8\pi}{3}) + \sin(\omega t - \theta)\big]$$

Collecting the $\sin(\omega t - \theta)$ terms (there are three identical ones):

$$B(\theta, t) = \tfrac{B_m}{2}\big[\sin(\omega t + \theta) + \sin(\omega t + \theta - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}) + \sin(\omega t + \theta - \tfrac{8\pi}{3})\big] + \tfrac{3 B_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)$$

The first bracket is the sum of three unit phasors displaced by 120┬░ ŌĆö a classical trigonometric identity gives this sum as **zero**:

$$\sin(\alpha) + \sin\!\left(\alpha - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right) + \sin\!\left(\alpha - \tfrac{8\pi}{3}\right) = 0 \quad \text{(for any } \alpha\text{)}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{B(\theta, t) = \tfrac{3 B_m}{2}\sin(\omega t - \theta)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (the heart of the KTU answer):**
> - The amplitude of the field is **constant** at $1.5\,B_m$ (independent of $\theta$ or $t$).
> - The phase of the field is $(\omega t - \theta)$ ŌĆö it depends on *both* time and space.
> - To track a point of constant phase (e.g., $\omega t - \theta = \pi/2$), $\theta$ must increase as $\omega t$ increases ŌĆö i.e., the field **rotates** in space at angular speed $\omega$ rad/s.
> - The **direction of rotation** is $A \to B \to C$ (the *phase sequence* of the supply).

The mechanical synchronous speed in rpm:

$$N_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{P/2} = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Torque Equation of a 3-Phase IM

### Step 1 ŌĆö Equivalent Rotor Circuit at Slip $s$

The standstill rotor circuit (resistance $R_2$, leakage reactance $X_2$, standstill EMF $E_2$) operates at rotor frequency $f_r = s f$ when running. All rotor quantities at slip are: EMF $\to s E_2$, reactance $\to s X_2$.

Rotor current magnitude:

$$I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}}}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Air-Gap Power

The power crossing the air-gap from stator to rotor (3 phases):

$$P_g = 3 \, I_2^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}$$

> This step is *critical*: we divide the rotor copper loss ($3 I_2^{\,2} R_2$) by $s$ to get total air-gap power. The remaining fraction $(1-s)$ of $P_g$ is converted to mechanical power.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Mechanical (Gross) Power Developed

$$P_m = (1 - s) \, P_g = 3 \, I_2^{\,2} \, R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1 - s}{s}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Developed Torque

The mechanical angular speed of the rotor in mechanical rad/s:

$$\omega_r = \omega_s (1 - s), \quad \text{where } \omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$$

Using the powerŌĆōtorque relationship $P_m = T_d \cdot \omega_r$:

$$T_d = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s}$$

Substituting $P_g$ and $I_2$:

$$T_d = \dfrac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot 3 \cdot \dfrac{(s E_2)^{2}}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_d = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_2^{\,2} \, R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Slip at Maximum Torque

To find $s_{mT}$, differentiate $T_d$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero. Let $K = 3 E_2^{\,2} / \omega_s$ (constant). Then:

$$T_d = K \cdot \dfrac{s R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + s^{2} X_2^{\,2}}$$

Let $N(s) = s R_2$ and $D(s) = R_2^{\,2} + s^{2} X_2^{\,2}$. Then:

$$\dfrac{dT_d}{ds} = K \cdot \dfrac{N'(s) D(s) - N(s) D'(s)}{D(s)^{2}} = 0$$

Compute the derivatives:

$$N'(s) = R_2, \qquad D'(s) = 2 s X_2^{\,2}$$

Set the numerator to zero:

$$R_2 (R_2^{\,2} + s^{2} X_2^{\,2}) - s R_2 (2 s X_2^{\,2}) = 0$$

$$R_2^{\,3} + s^{2} R_2 X_2^{\,2} - 2 s^{2} R_2 X_2^{\,2} = 0$$

$$R_2^{\,3} - s^{2} R_2 X_2^{\,2} = 0$$

$$R_2^{\,2} = s^{2} X_2^{\,2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{\,2}}{2 X_2}$$

(independent of $R_2$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Double Revolving Field Theory (1-Phase IM)

A single-phase pulsating flux along the $\theta = 0$ axis:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

Express using the identity $\cos A = \tfrac{1}{2}[e^{jA} + e^{-jA}]$ in phasor form. The pulsating field is the sum of two equal counter-rotating phasors:

$$\phi(t) = \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{j(\omega t - \theta)}}_{\text{Forward, rotates at } +\omega} + \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{-j(\omega t - \theta)}}_{\text{Backward, rotates at } -\omega}$$

Each rotating field induces its own rotor current, and the **net torque** is the algebraic sum:

$$T_{net}(\omega_r) = T_{forward}(s_f) - T_{backward}(s_b)$$

where the forward slip $s_f = s$ and the backward slip $s_b = 2 - s$. The torque-slip curve of a 1-Žå IM is the **sum of two 3-Žå-like curves mirrored about $s = 1$** ŌĆö yielding zero net starting torque.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at 1440 rpm on full load. The standstill rotor EMF/phase is 100 V, rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.5 \;\Omega$, rotor standstill reactance $X_2 = 2.0 \;\Omega$. Find: (i) slip, (ii) rotor current, (iii) developed torque, (iv) mechanical power developed, (v) rotor Cu loss.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slip:**

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \quad (4\%)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rotor frequency & EMF:**

$$f_r = s f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}, \quad E_{2r} = s E_2 = 0.04 \times 100 = 4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rotor current:**

$$I_2 = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{0.5^{2} + (0.04 \cdot 2.0)^{2}}} = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{0.25 + 0.0064}} = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{0.2564}} = \dfrac{4}{0.5064} = 7.899 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Developed torque:**

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \cdot \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 100^{2} \cdot 0.5}{0.5^{2} + (0.04 \cdot 2.0)^{2}} = \dfrac{3}{157.08} \cdot \dfrac{200}{0.2564} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 780.0}{157.08} \approx 14.90 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Mechanical power developed:**

$$P_m = T_d \cdot \omega_r = 14.90 \cdot \left(157.08 \cdot 0.96\right) = 14.90 \cdot 150.80 \approx 2246.9 \text{ W} \approx 2.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Rotor Cu loss:**

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g = \dfrac{s}{1-s} P_m = \dfrac{0.04}{0.96} \cdot 2246.9 \approx 93.6 \text{ W}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture ŌĆö 3-Phase Induction Motor System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["THREE PHASE SUPPLY 415V 50HZ"] --> B1["MAIN CONTACTOR DOL STARTER"]
    B1 --> C1["STATOR WINDING A B C 120 DEG SPATIAL SHIFT"]
    C1 --> D1["ROTATING MAGNETIC FIELD RMF 1.5 PHI M AT NS"]
    D1 --> E1["RELATIVE MOTION CUTS ROTOR BARS AT SLIP SPEED SN S"]
    E1 --> F1["INDUCED ROTOR EMF S E2 PER PHASE AT FR SF"]
    F1 --> G1["SHORTED ROTOR CURRENTS IN CAGE BARS"]
    G1 --> H1["LORENTZ FORCE BIL ON EACH ROTOR BAR"]
    H1 --> I1["NET DEVELOPED TORQUE TD IN RMF DIRECTION"]
    I1 --> J1["MECHANICAL OUTPUT SHAFT NR NS 1 MINUS S"]
    J1 --> K1["INDUSTRIAL LOAD PUMP FAN COMPRESSOR"]
    K1 --> L1["FEEDBACK TACHOMETER FOR VFD SPEED CONTROL"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow ŌĆö Selection of Starting Method for 1-Phase IM

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2["1 PHASE SUPPLY 230V 50HZ"] --> B2["REQUIREMENT LOW STARTING TORQUE LESS THAN 50 PERCENT FLT"]
    A2 --> C2["REQUIREMENT HIGH STARTING TORQUE GREATER THAN 100 PERCENT FLT"]
    A2 --> D2["REQUIREMENT VERY LOW POWER LESS THAN 50W"]
    B2 --> E2["SPLIT PHASE RESISTANCE START MOTOR"]
    B2 --> F2["PERMANENT CAPACITOR RUN MOTOR CEILING FAN"]
    C2 --> G2["CAPACITOR START MOTOR COMPRESSOR"]
    C2 --> H2["CAPACITOR START CAPACITOR RUN MOTOR"]
    D2 --> I2["SHADED POLE MOTOR CLOCKS TINY FANS"]
    E2 --> J2["CENTRIFUGAL SWITCH OPENS AT 70 PERCENT NS"]
    F2 --> K2["RUN CAPACITOR 2 TO 25 MICRO FARAD CONTINUOUS DUTY"]
    G2 --> L2["START CAPACITOR 30 TO 400 MICRO FARAD SHORT RATED"]
    H2 --> M2["TWO CAPACITORS START ELECTROLYTIC PLUS RUN PAPER"]
    I2 --> N2["COPPER SHADING RING DELAYS FLUX LOBE"]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow ŌĆö RMF Generation Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["INSTANT T0 IA PEAK IB NEGATIVE IC NEGATIVE"] --> P2["RMF AXIS POINTS ALONG PHASE A"]
    P2 --> P3["INSTANT T0 PLUS 60 DEG IB PEAK"]
    P3 --> P4["RMF AXIS SHIFTS TO PHASE B DIRECTION"]
    P4 --> P5["INSTANT T0 PLUS 120 DEG IC PEAK"]
    P5 --> P6["RMF AXIS SHIFTS TO PHASE C DIRECTION"]
    P6 --> P7["ONE FULL CYCLE T 1 F 0.02S FOR 50HZ"]
    P7 --> P8["RMF COMPLETED ONE ELECTRICAL REVOLUTION"]
    P8 --> P9["IF P POLES MECHANICAL REVOLUTIONS PER CYCLE P 2"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block ŌĆö 1-Phase IM Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    X1["1 PHASE STATOR V1 F 50HZ"] --> X2["STATOR IMPEDANCE R1 JX1"]
    X2 --> X3["MUTUAL INDUCTANCE XM J"]
    X3 --> X4["ROTOR SIDE R2 BY S JX2"]
    X4 --> X5["MECHANICAL LOAD TERM R2 TIMES 1 MINUS S DIVIDED BY S"]
    X5 --> X6["DOUBLE REVOLVING FIELD THEORY SPLIT INTO FORWARD AND BACKWARD"]
    X6 --> X7["FORWARD ROTOR CIRCUIT SLIP S"]
    X6 --> X8["BACKWARD ROTOR CIRCUIT SLIP 2 MINUS S"]
    X7 --> X9["NET TORQUE TF MINUS TB"]
    X8 --> X9
    X9 --> X10["ZERO NET STARTING TORQUE AT S EQUALS 1"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] ŌĆö 3 Marks

**Define "slip" of a three-phase induction motor. What is its value at standstill and at no-load? Why is it never exactly zero at no-load?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Slip is defined as the **relative speed** between the rotating magnetic field (synchronous speed $N_s$) and the actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$:
> $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
>
> - At **standstill** (switch-on instant): $N_r = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s = 1$
> - At **ideal no-load**: $N_r \to N_s \;\Rightarrow\; s \to 0$
>
> **Why $s \ne 0$ at no-load:**
> At $s = 0$, the rotor would be moving at synchronous speed, meaning *no relative motion* between rotor and RMF, so *no induced EMF, no rotor current, and hence no torque*. A real motor must develop *some* torque to overcome friction and windage, so the rotor slips back slightly, giving a small but non-zero $s$ (typically 0.001 to 0.005 at no-load). The slip at full load is normally between 0.02 and 0.05 for standard industrial machines.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024] ŌĆö 3 Marks

**Explain the Double Revolving Field Theory for a single-phase induction motor.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A single-phase AC winding produces a **pulsating (oscillating) flux**, not a rotating one. By Double Revolving Field Theory, this pulsating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ can be **mathematically resolved** into two equal rotating fluxes of magnitude $\phi_m / 2$, rotating in *opposite* directions at synchronous speed:
> $$\phi(t) = \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t - \theta)}_{\text{Forward, } +\omega_s} + \underbrace{\tfrac{\phi_m}{2}\cos(\omega t + \theta)}_{\text{Backward, } -\omega_s}$$
>
> The forward flux produces a forward torque $T_f$ and the backward flux a reverse torque $T_b$ on the rotor. **At standstill** ($s = 1$), the two torques are **equal and opposite**, yielding **zero net starting torque** ŌĆö therefore a 1-Žå IM is **not self-starting**. Once spun by an auxiliary means, the forward torque rises (low slip region) and the backward torque falls (high slip), so net torque develops in the spin direction.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö INTERNAL CHOICE

### Q1. (A) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022] ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive an expression for the rotating magnetic field (RMF) produced by a balanced 3-phase winding fed from a 3-phase supply. Show that the resultant field has constant magnitude equal to $1.5 \phi_m$ and rotates at synchronous speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has a full-load slip of 3%. The rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase are $0.2 \;\Omega$ and $1.0 \;\Omega$ respectively. The standstill rotor EMF per phase is 80 V. Calculate:
   (i) Synchronous speed and actual rotor speed.
   (ii) Rotor current at full load.
   (iii) Developed torque.
   (iv) Mechanical power developed and rotor Cu loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (A)

**(a) Derivation of RMF:** (Full derivation already provided in Section 3.1; key result to reproduce in exam:)

- Set up 3-phase windings $A$, $B$, $C$ displaced by $120┬░$ in space; 3-phase currents $i_a = I_m \sin\omega t$, $i_b = I_m \sin(\omega t - 2\pi/3)$, $i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t - 4\pi/3)$.
- Write the resultant flux density as $B(\theta,t) = B_a + B_b + B_c$, expand using product-to-sum identity.
- After algebraic simplification, the three rotating-phasor terms cancel, leaving:
  $$B(\theta, t) = \dfrac{3 B_m}{2} \sin(\omega t - \theta)$$
- **Constant amplitude** $= 1.5 B_m$.
- **Rotating at $\omega$ rad/s** (electrical); mechanical $N_s = 120 f / P$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Synchronous speed:
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

Actual speed:
$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 (1 - 0.03) = 970 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii)** Rotor current at full load ($s = 0.03$):
$$I_2 = \dfrac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^{2}}} = \dfrac{0.03 \times 80}{\sqrt{0.2^{2} + (0.03 \times 1.0)^{2}}} = \dfrac{2.4}{\sqrt{0.04 + 0.0009}} = \dfrac{2.4}{\sqrt{0.0409}} = \dfrac{2.4}{0.2022} = 11.87 \text{ A}$$

**(iii)** Developed torque:
$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$T_d = \dfrac{3}{104.72} \cdot \dfrac{0.03 \times 80^{2} \times 0.2}{0.04 + 0.0009} = \dfrac{3}{104.72} \cdot \dfrac{38.4}{0.0409} = \dfrac{3 \times 938.88}{104.72} \approx 26.90 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**(iv)** Air-gap power: $P_g = T_d \times \omega_s = 26.90 \times 104.72 = 2816.97 \text{ W} \approx 2.817 \text{ kW}$

Mechanical power: $P_m = (1 - s) P_g = 0.97 \times 2816.97 = 2732.46 \text{ W} \approx 2.73 \text{ kW}$

Rotor Cu loss: $P_{cu2} = s P_g = 0.03 \times 2816.97 = 84.51 \text{ W}$

---

### Q1. (B) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023] ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the torque-slip characteristics of a 3-phase induction motor. Indicate the stable operating region, maximum torque point, and the effect of increasing rotor resistance on the characteristic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (B)

**(a) Construction and Working:**

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-phase squirrel-cage IM consists of two main parts: **stator** and **rotor**, separated by a small uniform air-gap.

**Stator:**
- Stationary outer frame.
- Laminated silicon-steel core with semi-closed slots on the inner periphery.
- A 3-phase distributed winding (one for each phase $A$, $B$, $C$) is placed in the slots, with the three phases **displaced by 120┬░ electrical in space**.

**Rotor (Squirrel-Cage type):**
- Laminated cylindrical iron core mounted on the shaft.
- Slots on the outer surface contain **un-insulated copper/aluminium bars**.
- All bars are **short-circuited at both ends** by conducting end-rings.
- No brushes, no slip rings ŌĆö extremely robust.

**Working (3 Golden Steps):**
1. 3-Žå stator supply ŌåÆ **RMF** at $N_s = 120f/P$ in the air-gap.
2. RMF cuts the *stationary* rotor bars ŌåÆ **EMF induced** (Faraday's law).
3. Induced current in shorted bars interacts with the RMF ŌåÆ **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ on each bar ŌåÆ unidirectional **torque** rotates the rotor in the direction of the RMF (Lenz's law).

The rotor can never reach $N_s$ ŌĆö it must always slip to maintain relative motion, EMF, current, and torque.

**(b) Torque-Slip Characteristics:**

- **X-axis:** slip $s$ (0 to 1 in the motoring region, extended to 2 for braking).
- **Y-axis:** developed torque $T_d$ (positive = motoring, negative = generating/braking).
- **Three regions:**
  1. $0 < s < s_{mT}$: $T \propto s$ ŌĆö *stable* running region.
  2. $s = s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$: maximum (pull-out) torque $T_{max} = 3 E_2^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2)$.
  3. $s_{mT} < s < 1$: $T \propto 1/s$ ŌĆö unstable; high-slip / start-up region.
- **Effect of increasing rotor resistance** (in slip-ring motors by adding external $R$):
  - $T_{max}$ remains **unchanged** (independent of $R_2$).
  - $s_{mT}$ shifts to **higher values** (the peak slides towards $s = 1$).
  - **Starting torque increases** significantly ŌĆö useful for heavy-load starting.
- **Braking region** ($1 < s < 2$): achieved by reversing any two stator phases; rotor opposes motion, dissipates energy as heat (plugging).
- **Generating region** ($s < 0$): when driven by an external prime mover faster than $N_s$ (rare in IM practice).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks ŌĆö Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\omega_s$ in the denominator** of the torque equation ŌĆö many students compute $T_d = P_m / \omega_r$ and forget $P_m / \omega_s$ relationship. Both are equivalent *if used correctly*; mixing them gives the wrong answer.
> 2. **Confusing $X_2$ and $s X_2$**: $X_2$ is the *standstill* rotor reactance. At any slip $s$, the *running* reactance is $s X_2$. Watch this in the formula.
> 3. **In 1-Žå IM theory, students write $T_b$ as a forward torque** ŌĆö it is *backward* (opposite to rotor motion). Always write $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in 3-phase formulae when applying per-phase analysis ŌĆö every power and torque expression for a 3-phase IM must carry a factor of 3.
> 5. **Wrong units:** $N_s$ in *rpm*, $f$ in *Hz*, $P$ in *number of poles* (not pole-pairs), $\omega_s$ in *rad/s* (not rpm).
> 6. **Thevenin equivalent in KTU exams:** When $V_1$ and stator impedance are given, always reduce to Thevenin $V_{th}$ and $Z_{th}$ *before* computing torque ŌĆö many students plug $V_1$ directly into the simple formula.
> 7. **Capacitor-start vs. Capacitor-run:** Examiner may give a trick question ŌĆö a ceiling fan uses a *run* capacitor, a refrigerator compressor uses a *start* capacitor. Know the difference cold.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorise Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

- **3-Žå IM is self-starting** because the 3-phase supply *inherently* produces a *rotating* magnetic field. 1-Žå IM is *not* self-starting.
- **RMF magnitude** $= 1.5\,\phi_m$ (constant); **RMF speed** $= N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Direction of rotation** in a 3-Žå IM is determined by the **phase sequence** of the supply (swap any two phases ŌåÆ reverse rotation).
- **Slip** at standstill $= 1$, at synchronous speed $= 0$, at full load $\approx 0.02 - 0.05$. Rotor always runs *below* $N_s$.
- **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s f$ ŌĆö *not* the supply frequency.
- **Rotor EMF and reactance at slip $s$** are $sE_2$ and $sX_2$ respectively (linear with slip).
- **Maximum torque $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$**; **slip at $T_{max}$ is directly proportional to $R_2$**.
- **Power flow chain:** $P_{in} \to P_{stator\,Cu} \to P_g \to P_{cu2} \to P_{mech} \to P_{shaft}$ where $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$ and $P_{mech} = (1 - s) P_g$.
- **Efficiency of rotor conversion** $= (1 - s)$ ŌĆö higher slip = more rotor loss.
- **1-Žå IM ŌĆö Double Revolving Field Theory:** pulsating flux = two equal counter-rotating fluxes of half magnitude.
- **Net starting torque of 1-Žå IM = 0** without auxiliary means. Starting methods: split-phase, capacitor-start, capacitor-run, capacitor-start-capacitor-run, shaded-pole.
- **Slip-ring / wound rotor motors** allow external rotor resistance insertion for **high starting torque** at low starting current ŌĆö used in cranes, hoists, elevators.
- **Squirrel-cage** is the most common industrial rotor ŌĆö rugged, cheap, low maintenance.
- **Braking methods for IM:** plugging (reverse stator), dynamic braking (DC injection), regenerative (if $s < 0$).
- **Standard formulae to write at the top of your answer sheet** (saves time and signals confidence): $N_s = 120f/P$, $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, $f_r = sf$, $E_{2r} = sE_2$, $T_d = (3/\omega_s) \cdot sE_2^2 R_2 / (R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2)$, $s_{mT} = R_2/X_2$, $T_{max} = (3/\omega_s) \cdot E_2^2 / (2X_2)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Devices and Controlled Rectifiers: Power Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Devices and Controlled Rectifiers: Power Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Electronic Devices ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Power electronic devices* are solid-state semiconductor switches capable of controlling or converting electrical power (typically from a few watts to megawatts) by switching between high-impedance (OFF) and low-impedance (ON) states. They form the foundational building blocks of **controlled rectifiers**, **inverters**, **choppers**, and **AC voltage controllers** used in modern electric drive systems.

In the context of **PCAET601 (Electric Drives and Control)**, Module 2 specifically targets the **Power Devices** that constitute the *front-end* of every power electronic converter feeding an electric motor. Without these switches, neither speed control nor regenerative braking of DC/AC drives would be feasible.

### 1.1.1 Ideal Switch Characteristics

| Property | Ideal Switch | Practical Power Device |
|---|---|---|
| **OFF-state** current | $0 \text{ A}$ | $\mu$A to mA (leakage) |
| **ON-state** voltage drop | $0 \text{ V}$ | $0.7$ to $3.0 \text{ V}$ |
| **Switching time** | $0 \text{ s}$ | $\mu$s range |
| **Power handling** | Infinite | kW to MW |
| **Control signal** | Zero power | mW (gate drive) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Smart Water Tap"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Power Device = Smart Water Tap**
>
> Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (your DC bus / AC mains). A normal tap is either fully **OPEN** (water gushes out) or fully **CLOSED** (no water). A *power electronic device* is a **"smart tap"** that:
>
> 1. Can be **opened or closed by a tiny electrical pulse** (the *gate trigger*) ŌĆö not a big mechanical handle.
> 2. Can switch ON/OFF **thousands of times per second** (high-frequency switching for PWM).
> 3. Handles **huge currents and voltages** (the water pressure) while the *control signal* is *tiny* (the finger flick).
>
> - **Diode** = One-way tap (water flows only one direction, no control knob).
> - **Thyristor (SCR)** = Smart tap that needs a *push-button* (gate pulse) to open, but **cannot be closed by a button** ŌĆö it closes only when the water (current) naturally stops.
> - **MOSFET / IGBT** = Fully smart tap ŌĆö opens AND closes by button-push, in any direction (when properly configured).
> - **TRIAC** = Bi-directional smart tap for AC water lines.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Power Devices (KTU Hierarchy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three families ŌĆö examiners love this classification tree:**

### A. Uncontrolled Devices
- **Power Diode** ŌĆö conducts when forward-biased above $\sim 0.7$ V; no gate control.

### B. Semi-Controlled Devices
- **SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier / Thyristor)** ŌĆö turned ON by gate pulse, OFF by natural/current commutation.
- **TRIAC** ŌĆö bi-directional SCR equivalent for AC.
- **DIAC** ŌĆö bi-directional trigger device.
- **GTO (Gate Turn-Off thyristor)** ŌĆö turn-OFF via gate (bridge between semi- and fully-controlled).

### C. Fully-Controlled Devices
- **Power BJT** (now obsolete in drives) ŌĆö continuous base current control.
- **Power MOSFET** ŌĆö voltage-controlled, fast switching.
- **IGBT** ŌĆö combines BJT + MOSFET; workhorse of modern drives.
- **IGCT / IEGT** ŌĆö modern high-power variants.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
> - **Bandgap of Silicon:** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$
> - **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = 25.85 \text{ mV}$
> - **Duty cycle range:** $D = 0$ to $1$ (dimensionless)
> - **Switching frequencies (typical):** MOSFET ŌĆö $100$ kHz to $1$ MHz; IGBT ŌĆö $1$ to $20$ kHz; SCR ŌĆö $50/60$ Hz line frequency.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Electric Drives

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Perspective:** A student who cannot identify the *right device* for a *given drive application* will lose marks in application-based questions. Always remember:
>
> - **DC motor drives (chopper / rectifier):** SCR (high power) or IGBT (medium power, fast response).
> - **AC induction motor (VFD):** IGBT (PWM inverter, 1ŌĆō20 kHz).
> - **Servo drives / BLDC:** MOSFET (high frequency).
> - **HVDC / Ultra-high power:** Thyristor (line-commutated).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Diode ŌĆö Working & Characteristics

A power diode is a two-terminal, three-layer ($P$ŌĆō$N$ŌĆō$N^+$) device. The lightly doped $N^-$ drift region is what distinguishes it from a signal diode ŌĆö it provides the **high breakdown voltage** capability.

### 2.1.1 Forward Conduction

When $V_{AK} > V_0$ (cut-in voltage, typically $0.7$ V for Si):

$$V_{AK} = V_0 + I_F \cdot R_{on}$$

Where:
- $V_0$ = threshold / cut-in voltage (constant, $0.7$ V)
- $R_{on}$ = on-state slope resistance
- $I_F$ = forward current

### 2.1.2 Reverse Recovery ŌĆö A Critical Dynamic Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$):** When a conducting diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it does NOT stop conducting instantly. Stored minority carriers in the drift region must be swept out.

$$t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$$

Where:
- $t_a$ = storage time (delay before reverse current peaks)
- $t_b$ = transition time (reverse current falls to zero)

**Softness factor:** $S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a}$ ŌĆö *higher S = softer recovery = better for drives.*

### 2.1.3 Power Loss in a Diode

$$P_{loss} = V_0 \cdot I_{F(avg)} + R_{on} \cdot I_{F(rms)}^{2}$$

---

## 2.2 The SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) ŌĆö The Heart of Controlled Rectifiers

The SCR is a **four-layer ($P$ŌĆō$N$ŌĆō$P$ŌĆō$N$), three-terminal** device with terminals: **Anode (A), Cathode (K), Gate (G)**.

### 2.2.1 Two-Transistor Analogy (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SCR can be modeled as a **complementary pair of BJTs**: a PNP ($Q_1$) feeding an NPN ($Q_2$).

- $\alpha_1$ = common-base current gain of $Q_1$ (PNP)
- $\alpha_2$ = common-base current gain of $Q_2$ (NPN)

The total anode current:

$$I_A = \frac{I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2} + \alpha_2 \cdot I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Latching condition** (SCR turns ON permanently once triggered):

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$$

When this sum **reaches unity**, regenerative action drives both transistors into saturation ŌĆö the SCR latches.

### 2.2.2 Static V-I Characteristics

The SCR V-I curve has four regions:

| Region | Condition | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Blocking** | $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$, $I_G = 0$ | High impedance, small leakage |
| **Forward Conduction** | After triggering | Low $V_T \approx 1$ŌĆō$2$ V |
| **Reverse Blocking** | $V_{AK} < 0$, $|V_{AK}| < V_{BR}$ | High impedance |
| **Reverse Breakdown** | $V_{AK} < -V_{BR}$ | Avalanche ŌĆö destructive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key parameter:** **Holding current ($I_H$)** is the *minimum* anode current below which the SCR reverts to OFF state. **Latching current ($I_L$)** is the *minimum* anode current that *must be reached* during turn-on to keep the SCR ON after the gate pulse is removed. **Always: $I_L > I_H$.**

### 2.2.3 Turn-ON Methods (Frequently asked)

1. **Forward voltage triggering** ŌĆö exceed $V_{BO}$.
2. **Gate triggering** ŌĆö apply $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ (most common).
3. **dv/dt triggering** ŌĆö unwanted; can cause false turn-on.
4. **Light triggering** (LASCR) ŌĆö used in HVDC.
5. **Temperature triggering** ŌĆö thermal run-away.

### 2.2.4 Turn-OFF (Commutation) ŌĆö Why SCR is "Semi-Controlled"

> [!WARNING]
> Once latched, the SCR **CANNOT be turned off by the gate.** This is the *single most important fact* a KTU examiner tests. Methods to turn OFF:
>
> 1. **Natural (line) commutation** ŌĆö AC supply naturally reverses.
> 2. **Class A (Load commutation)** ŌĆö load is resonant / leading PF.
> 3. **Class B (Resonant commutation)** ŌĆö LC tank forces current zero.
> 4. **Class C (Complementary commutation)** ŌĆö auxiliary charged capacitor.
> 5. **Class D (External pulse commutation)** ŌĆö rare, high-power.

**Circuit Turn-Off Time:**

$$t_c = t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$$

Where:
- $t_{rr}$ = reverse recovery time
- $t_{gr}$ = gate recovery time
- $t_c$ = turn-off time available in the circuit ŌĆö must be **Ōēź** device's $t_q$ (specified turn-off time).

### 2.2.5 Gate Characteristics (KTU Standard Graph)

Three critical limits of the gate drive:

$$V_G \in [V_{GT(min)}, V_{G(max)}], \quad I_G \in [I_{GT}, I_{G(max)}]$$

- **$I_{GT}$** = minimum gate current to trigger all SCRs of a family.
- **$V_{GT}$** = corresponding minimum gate voltage.
- **$P_{G(avg)}$** = average gate power dissipation limit (don't exceed).
- **$V_{GD}$, $I_{GD}$** = maximum values that *must not* trigger the SCR (noise immunity).

---

## 2.3 Power MOSFET ŌĆö The High-Frequency Workhorse

### 2.3.1 Construction
- **N-channel enhancement mode** is most common.
- **V-MOSFET** (V-groove) and **D-MOSFET** (double-diffused) are the two dominant geometries.

### 2.3.2 Transfer Characteristic

$$I_D = K \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

For $V_{GS} > V_{th}$; else $I_D = 0$.

### 2.3.3 ON-State Resistance

$$R_{DS(on)} \propto \dfrac{V_{BD}^{2.5}}{I_D} \quad \text{(empirically)}$$

This is why MOSFETs become *inefficient* above $\sim 200$ V ratings.

### 2.3.4 Switching Times
- $t_{d(on)}$ ŌĆö turn-on delay
- $t_r$ ŌĆö rise time
- $t_{d(off)}$ ŌĆö turn-off delay
- $t_f$ ŌĆö fall time

**Total switching time** = $t_{d(on)} + t_r + t_{d(off)} + t_f$ ŌĆö typically **$<$ 100 ns**, allowing MHz operation.

---

## 2.4 IGBT ŌĆö The Modern Choice for Motor Drives

### 2.4.1 Symbol & Equivalent Circuit

The IGBT is essentially a **Darlington of a Power MOSFET (input) driving a BJT (output)**.

- **Input:** MOSFET gate (voltage-controlled) ŌåÆ high input impedance.
- **Output:** BJT collector-emitter ŌåÆ low on-state voltage drop.

### 2.4.2 Static Characteristic (Output)

$$V_{CE(sat)} = V_{BE(on)} + I_C \cdot R_{channel}$$

Typical $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$ V ŌĆö lower than MOSFET at high currents, hence **preferred for medium-to-high power drives**.

### 2.4.3 Switching Frequency Limit
IGBTs switch efficiently up to $\sim 20$ kHz ŌĆö perfectly suited for **PWM inverter-fed induction motor drives** in the $1$ŌĆō$20$ kW range.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (PRINT-READY)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. Every KTU paper draws at least 2 questions directly from this list.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ | Thermal voltage at 300 K | V |
| 2 | $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | SCR anode current (two-transistor) | A |
| 3 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | SCR latching condition | ŌĆö |
| 4 | $I_L > I_H$ | Latching $>$ Holding current (always) | A |
| 5 | $t_c = t_q$ constraint | $t_{c,circuit} \geq t_{q,device}$ | s |
| 6 | $I_D = K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | MOSFET transfer | A |
| 7 | $R_{DS(on)} \propto \dfrac{V_{BD}^{2.5}}{I_D}$ | MOSFET on-resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $V_{CE(sat)} = V_{BE} + I_C R_{ch}$ | IGBT on-state drop | V |
| 9 | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_s I_s (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching power loss | W |
| 10 | $P_{cond} = V_{on} I_{rms}$ | Conduction power loss | W |
| 11 | $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ | Diode reverse recovery | s |
| 12 | $S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a}$ | Recovery softness factor | ŌĆö |
