# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 ŌĆö SCR Two-Transistor Anode Current Equation

**Starting principle:** The SCR is replaced by an **interconnected PNP ($Q_1$) and NPN ($Q_2$)** sharing collector-base junctions.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define leakage currents
The collector currents in CB configuration:

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_{E1} + I_{CBO1}$$
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_{E2} + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply KCL at the emitter nodes
For $Q_1$ (PNP), emitter current is anode current:

$$I_{E1} = I_A + I_G$$

For $Q_2$ (NPN), emitter current is the gate-side current; gate is the only external node, so:

$$I_{E2} = I_A - I_G$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute back

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO1}$$
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 (I_A - I_G) + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Total anode current = sum of collector currents (KCL at anode)
The anode is fed by the collector of $Q_1$ *and* the emitter current of $Q_2$ recirculating back. By careful KCL at the anode node:

$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$$

Substituting:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO1} + \alpha_2 (I_A - I_G) + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Collect terms in $I_A$ and $I_G$

$$I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$$

$$I_A - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$$

$$I_A \left[ 1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \right] = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Final expression

$$\boxed{I_A = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Latching condition (numerical logic)
If $I_G = 0$ and $I_{CBO1}, I_{CBO2}$ are negligible, $I_A = 0$ as long as $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) < 1$.
As $I_A$ rises (or temperature raises $\alpha$'s), the denominator shrinks.
At the *critical* point $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$, the denominator ŌåÆ $0$, and $I_A \rightarrow \infty$ ŌĆö this is the **latching / breakover** condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical example (KTU-style):**
> An SCR has $\alpha_1 = 0.32$, $\alpha_2 = 0.42$, leakage currents = $0$. Find the gate current $I_G$ required to deliver $I_A = 20$ A. Assume the device just latches.

Apply latching $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 0.74$ and rewrite the equation at the verge of conduction:

$$I_A = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + 0}{1 - 0.74} = \frac{(0.32 - 0.42) I_G}{0.26} = \frac{-0.10 I_G}{0.26}$$

For a *positive* $I_A = 20$ A from a *negative* numerator, we must have negative $I_G$ which is unphysical ŌĆö meaning the **device has not latched yet**. We need to wait until $\alpha$'s grow with $I_A$.

A more practical design uses: $I_{GT} \approx$ (catalogue value) and checks $I_L$ is reached. This is the *latching current* check.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 ŌĆö Switching Power Loss in a Power Device

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define voltage and current waveforms during a transition
During turn-OFF (and turn-ON), the device voltage rises (or falls) linearly while current falls (or rises) ŌĆö approximated as a **linear crossover** region.

During a transition of duration $t_{sw}$ (say $t_{on} + t_{off}$):

$$v(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{t_{sw}} t, \quad i(t) = I_s \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{t_{sw}}\right)$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Instantaneous power

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} t \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{t_{sw}}\right) = \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} \left( t - \dfrac{t^2}{t_{sw}} \right)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Energy lost per switching event

$$E_{sw} = \int_{0}^{t_{sw}} p(t) \, dt = \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} \int_{0}^{t_{sw}} \left( t - \dfrac{t^2}{t_{sw}} \right) dt$$

$$= \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} - \dfrac{t^3}{3 t_{sw}} \right]_{0}^{t_{sw}}$$

$$= \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} \left( \dfrac{t_{sw}^2}{2} - \dfrac{t_{sw}^2}{3} \right) = \dfrac{V_s I_s}{t_{sw}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{sw}^2}{6}$$

$$\boxed{E_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{6} V_s I_s t_{sw}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Power loss at switching frequency $f_{sw}$

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{6} V_s I_s t_{sw} f_{sw}}$$

A common engineering approximation (used in KTU textbooks) is:

$$P_{sw} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} V_s I_s (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical example
A $600$ V / $50$ A IGBT has $t_{on} + t_{off} = 600$ ns, switching at $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz.

$$P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 50 \cdot 600 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10^4$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot 30000 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \cdot 30000 \cdot 0.006 = 90 \text{ W}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with sub-microsecond switching, $90$ W of loss is generated. This is why IGBT modules need **heatsinks** and why engineers minimise $f_{sw}$ when high currents are involved.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 ŌĆö Diode Reverse Recovery Softness Factor

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define recovery phases
- **Phase A** ($0$ to $t_a$): Reverse current rises to peak $I_{RM}$. Stored charge $Q_1$ is removed.
- **Phase B** ($t_a$ to $t_{rr}$): Reverse current decays to $\approx 0.1 I_{RM}$. Stored charge $Q_2$ removed by recombination.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Charge relationships

$$Q_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} I_{RM} t_a, \quad Q_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I_{RM} t_b$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Softness factor

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a} = \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Design rule
For a "**soft recovery**" diode (preferred in motor drives to limit EMI and voltage spikes):

$$S \geq 1 \quad \text{(soft),} \quad S < 1 \quad \text{(abrupt / snappy)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Device Selection for Drive Applications

| Drive Type | Power Range | Switching Freq. | Best Device | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DC motor (chopper) | $<$ 5 kW | $1$ŌĆō$5$ kHz | MOSFET / IGBT | Fast response, easy PWM |
| DC motor (phase-controlled rectifier) | $5$ŌĆō$500$ kW | $50/60$ Hz | SCR | High current, low cost |
| Induction motor (VFD) | $1$ŌĆō$500$ kW | $2$ŌĆō$16$ kHz | IGBT | Optimal trade-off |
| Servo / BLDC | $0.1$ŌĆō$5$ kW | $20$ŌĆō$100$ kHz | MOSFET | Highest switching speed |
| HVDC / SMPS-grade | $>$ 1 MW | Line freq. | SCR / IGCT | Only thyristors survive voltage |
| Solar PV inverter | $5$ŌĆō$100$ kW | $20$ŌĆō$100$ kHz | IGBT / SiC MOSFET | Efficiency critical |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SCR ŌĆö Symbol, Layer Diagram & Two-Transistor Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCR_Internal["SCR Four Layer P-N-P-N Structure"]
        A1["P Layer Anode"]
        A2["N Layer"]
        A3["P Layer"]
        A4["N+ Layer Cathode"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    subgraph TwoTransistorModel["Two Transistor Analogy"]
        Q1["Q1 PNP Transistor"]
        Q2["Q2 NPN Transistor"]
        Q1Emitter["Emitter of Q1 = Anode"]
        Q2Emitter["Emitter of Q2 = Cathode"]
        Q1Base["Base of Q1 = Collector of Q2"]
        Q2Base["Base of Q2 = Collector of Q1"]
        Q1GateInput["Gate pulse injected at Q2 base"]

        Q1 --- Q1Emitter
        Q1 --- Q1Base
        Q2 --- Q2Emitter
        Q2 --- Q2Base
        Q1Base --- Q2Base
        Q2Base --- Q1GateInput
    end

    SCR_Internal --> TwoTransistorModel
```

**Description:** The four layers $P_1$ŌĆō$N_1$ŌĆō$P_2$ŌĆō$N_2$ of the SCR are split into two complementary BJTs. The middle $N_1$ŌĆō$P_2$ junction is shared; a gate pulse into the $P_2$ layer acts as the base of $Q_2$ (NPN), initiating the regenerative loop.

---

## 4.2 IGBT ŌĆö Equivalent Circuit & Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IGBT_Block["IGBT Internal Block Diagram"]
        M["Power MOSFET Input Stage"]
        B["BJT Output Stage Darlington"]
        G["Gate Terminal Voltage Controlled"]
        C["Collector Terminal"]
        E["Emitter Terminal"]

        G --> M
        M --> B
        B --> C
        B --> E
    end

    subgraph DriveApp["Typical Drive Application"]
        DC["DC Bus 600 V"]
        LOAD["Induction Motor Load"]
        DC --> IGBT_Block
        IGBT_Block --> LOAD
    end
```

**Description:** Gate signal modulates the MOSFET channel, which in turn drives the base of the BJT. The BJT handles the high current; the MOSFET provides the high input impedance ŌĆö combining the best of both worlds.

---

## 4.3 SCR V-I Characteristic Curve (Functional Flow Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Device under Forward Bias VAK > 0 and IG = 0"] --> ForwardBlock{"VAK less than VBO?"}
    ForwardBlock -- YES --> Region1["FORWARD BLOCKING REGION\n Small leakage current IDA"]
    ForwardBlock -- NO --> Breakover["FORWARD BREAKOVER at VBO\n Self triggering avalanche"]

    Gate["Apply Gate Pulse IG greater than IGT"] --> Region3
    Breakover --> Region3
    Region1 --> Gate
    Region3["FORWARD CONDUCTION REGION\n Low VT approx 1 to 2 V"]

    Reverse["Apply Reverse VAK less than 0"] --> ReverseBlock{"|VAK| less than VBR?"}
    ReverseBlock -- YES --> Region4["REVERSE BLOCKING REGION"]
    ReverseBlock -- NO --> Region5["REVERSE BREAKDOWN - DESTRUCTIVE"]
```

---

## 4.4 Power Loss Distribution in a Switching Device (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LossBlocks["Total Power Loss in a Switching Device"]
        P_cond["Conduction Loss Pcond = Von x I_rms"]
        P_sw["Switching Loss Psw = 0.5 x Vs x Is x t_sw x f_sw"]
        P_gate["Gate Drive Loss Pgate = Vg x Ig"]
        P_leak["Leakage Loss Pleak = Vak x Ileak during OFF state"]
    end

    P_total["Total Device Loss Ptotal"] --> P_cond
    P_total --> P_sw
    P_total --> P_gate
    P_total --> P_leak

    Output["Heat to Heatsink"] --> P_total
```

**Description:** Engineers use this functional map to design thermal management. For SCRs at line frequency, $P_{sw} \approx 0$ and $P_{cond}$ dominates. For IGBTs at $10$ kHz, $P_{sw}$ becomes comparable to $P_{cond}$ ŌĆö hence the push to use **wide-bandgap devices (SiC, GaN)** to cut $P_{sw}$.

---

## 4.5 Gate Drive Circuit Block (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL["Controller DSP or Microcontroller 3.3 V or 5 V logic"] --> ISO["Isolator Optocoupler or Digital Isolator"]
    ISO --> DRV["Gate Driver IC eg TLP250 HCPL3120"]
    DRV --> RG["Gate Resistor RG limits di/dt"]
    RG --> GATE["Gate of Power Device IGBT or MOSFET"]
    GATE --> SINK["Negative bias during turn off to prevent spurious triggering"]
```

**Description:** A practical gate drive is far more than "just apply a voltage." Isolation prevents ground loops, $R_G$ controls switching speed, and negative turn-off bias improves noise immunity in motor-drive environments with high $dv/dt$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With the help of a two-transistor analogy, explain the working of an SCR. Hence derive the latching condition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SCR consists of **four layers $P_1$ŌĆō$N_1$ŌĆō$P_2$ŌĆō$N_2$** which can be split into a **PNP transistor $Q_1$** and an **NPN transistor $Q_2$** with their collector-base junctions shared.

Applying KCL at the anode:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}$$

$$\Rightarrow I_A = \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

The denominator must remain positive for finite $I_A$. As $I_A$ increases, $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ both increase. When $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \rightarrow 1$, the denominator $\rightarrow 0$ and $I_A \rightarrow \infty$ ŌĆö the **regenerative feedback** latches the device ON.

$$\boxed{\text{Latching condition: } \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1}$$

The gate pulse simply initiates this avalanche; once the loop is self-sustaining, removing $I_G$ does not turn the device OFF.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, S6 B.Tech EEE]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SCR | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching Frequency** | $50/60$ Hz (limited by $t_q$) | Up to $1$ MHz | $1$ŌĆō$20$ kHz |
| **Voltage Rating** | Up to $10$ kV | Up to $1000$ V (typical) | Up to $6.5$ kV |
| **Current Rating** | Up to $5000$ A | Up to $100$ A | Up to $3600$ A |
| **Control** | Semi (gate ON only) | Fully (voltage) | Fully (voltage) |
| **Conduction drop** | $\sim 1.5$ŌĆō$2.5$ V | Higher ($R_{DS} \cdot I$) | Low ($V_{CE(sat)} \sim 1.5$ŌĆō$3$ V) |
| **Typical Drive Application** | Phase-controlled DC motor rectifier | SMPS, low-power servo | VFD, EV traction inverter |

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Draw the static V-I characteristics of an SCR and label all critical parameters. Explain the **turn-ON** and **turn-OFF** mechanisms in detail. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** An SCR in a DC chopper circuit has the following data:
> $V_s = 400$ V, $I_s = 100$ A, $t_{on} = 40\ \mu s$, $t_{off} = 30\ \mu s$, $f_{sw} = 2$ kHz.
> Calculate (i) the switching power loss, (ii) the conduction power loss if $V_T = 1.8$ V and $I_{rms} = 60$ A. (7 marks)

#### Part A(a) ŌĆö Model Solution

The **SCR V-I characteristics** are divided into four regions:

1. **Forward Blocking Region (OFF state, $I_G = 0$):** Anode positive, cathode negative. Forward leakage current $I_D \approx \mu$A flows until $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$ (Forward Breakover Voltage).
2. **Forward Conduction Region (ON state):** Once triggered, $V_{AK}$ drops to $V_T$ (typically $1$ŌĆō$2$ V) and $I_A$ can be very large.
3. **Reverse Blocking Region:** Anode negative, cathode positive. Small reverse leakage $I_R$ flows until $|V_{AK}| = V_{BR}$ (Reverse Breakdown Voltage).
4. **Reverse Breakdown Region:** Avalanche multiplication ŌĆö destructive.

**Critical parameters to label:**
- $V_{BO}$ ŌĆö Forward Breakover Voltage
- $I_L$ ŌĆö Latching current
- $I_H$ ŌĆö Holding current ($I_H < I_L$)
- $V_{BR}$ ŌĆö Reverse Breakdown Voltage
- $V_T$ ŌĆö On-state voltage drop
- $V_{GD}$, $V_{GT}$ ŌĆö Gate non-trigger / trigger voltages

**Turn-ON mechanism:**
A positive gate current $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ causes $\alpha_2$ to rise. Once $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$, regenerative action snaps the device into conduction. The anode current must reach $I_L$ before $I_G$ is removed; otherwise, the SCR returns to the OFF state.

**Turn-OFF mechanism:**
Once ON, the gate loses control. The anode current must be reduced below $I_H$ for a duration $\geq t_q$ (circuit turn-off time). Methods:
- **Natural / Line commutation** (AC supply reverses).
- **Forced commutation** via resonant / class-C circuits in DC choppers.

#### Part A(b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Given:**
$V_s = 400$ V, $I_s = 100$ A, $t_{on} = 40\ \mu s$, $t_{off} = 30\ \mu s$, $f_{sw} = 2$ kHz, $V_T = 1.8$ V, $I_{rms} = 60$ A.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Switching power loss:**

$$P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_s I_s (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$$

$$= 0.5 \times 400 \times 100 \times (40 + 30) \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{3}$$

$$= 0.5 \times 400 \times 100 \times 70 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000$$

$$= 0.5 \times 400 \times 100 \times 0.14$$

$$= 0.5 \times 400 \times 14 = 2800 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Conduction power loss:**

$$P_{cond} = V_T \cdot I_{rms} = 1.8 \times 60 = 108 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total power loss:**

$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{cond} = 2800 + 108 = 2908 \text{ W}$$

**Inference:** Even a small $t_{on} + t_{off} = 70\ \mu s$ at $2$ kHz creates nearly $2.9$ kW of loss. This dictates **heatsink sizing** and motivates use of *faster* devices (IGBT, MOSFET) when possible.

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **power MOSFET** with a neat sketch of its equivalent circuit. Derive the relationship between drain current and gate-source voltage. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With the help of a circuit and waveform, explain **resonant commutation (Class B)** of an SCR. State the necessary condition for successful commutation. (7 marks)

#### Part B(a) ŌĆö Model Solution

**Construction:**
A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier** device. The **N-channel enhancement-mode** V-MOSFET has:
- $N^+$ source and $N^-$ drift region (for high breakdown).
- **Vertical** current flow through the drift region.
- **V-shaped** or **trench** gate to control the channel.

**Working:**
- When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$: no inversion layer; drain current $I_D = 0$ (OFF).
- When $V_{GS} > V_{th}$: an n-type channel forms at the surface; electrons flow from source ŌåÆ channel ŌåÆ $N^-$ drift ŌåÆ drain.

**Equivalent Circuit:**
A MOSFET is essentially a **voltage-controlled resistor** in the triode region, with parasitic capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{ds}$ that govern switching speed.

**Drain current derivation:**
In saturation, the channel behaves as a square-law device:

$$\boxed{I_D = K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}$$

where the transconductance parameter is:

$$K = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$$

- $\mu_n$ = electron mobility in channel
- $C_{ox}$ = gate oxide capacitance per unit area
- $W/L$ = aspect ratio

The transconductance (small-signal gain):

$$g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = 2 K (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{2 K I_D}$$

#### Part B(b) ŌĆö Model Solution

**Resonant (Class B) Commutation:**

A series **LC tank** is connected across the SCR. The capacitor is pre-charged to a polarity that *opposes* the SCR current. When the auxiliary thyristor $T_2$ is fired, the LC circuit produces a **resonant current** that flows in the *opposite* direction through the main SCR, forcing its current to zero.

**Circuit elements:**
- Main SCR ($T_1$) carrying load current $I_L$.
- Commutation capacitor $C$ charged to $V_C$.
- Commutation inductor $L$.
- Auxiliary SCR $T_2$.

**Waveform sequence:**
1. $T_1$ is conducting, $C$ charged to $V_0$.
2. $T_2$ is fired at $t = 0$.
3. Resonant current $i_C = V_0 \sqrt{C/L} \sin(\omega_0 t)$ where $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ flows through $T_1$ in the *reverse* direction.
4. At $t = \pi/\omega_0$, the resonant current returns to zero ŌĆö $T_1$ sees net anode current = $I_L - i_C = 0$ and turns OFF.
5. $T_1$ must remain OFF for at least $t_q$ to regain forward blocking.

**Necessary condition for successful commutation:**

$$i_{C,peak} = V_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} > I_L$$

$$\boxed{V_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} \geq I_L}$$

If the peak resonant current is less than the load current, the SCR will *re-ignite* and commutation fails.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Avoid These Mark-Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $0.5$ in switching loss** ŌĆö writing $P_{sw} = V_s I_s (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2} V_s I_s (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 2. **Saying SCR can be turned off by gate** ŌĆö this is the #1 myth. Use the words *"natural / forced commutation"* and *"anode current below holding current."*
> 3. **Confusing latching and holding currents** ŌĆö remember $I_L > I_H$. Examiners ask *"which is higher?"* in Part A.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** ŌĆö always write W, kW, A, V explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting the condition $i_{C,peak} > I_L$** in commutation derivations ŌĆö this *is* the answer to "necessary condition."
> 6. **Drawing IGBT with wrong symbol** ŌĆö the IGBT has *three terminals* (G, C, E) and the body diode is part of the symbol; do not draw it like a BJT.
> 7. **Ignoring safety factor in dv/dt triggering** ŌĆö students forget to mention *snubber circuits* when discussing false triggering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam**

- ŌśÉ A **Power Diode** conducts only when forward-biased; has stored charge ŌåÆ reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$.
- ŌśÉ **SCR** is a 4-layer, 3-terminal ($A$, $K$, $G$) **semi-controlled** device ŌĆö latches ON, turned OFF by commutation.
- ŌśÉ **Latching current $I_L$** is the *minimum anode current at turn-on*; **holding current $I_H$** is the *minimum to stay ON*; always $I_L > I_H$.
- ŌśÉ **Two-transistor analogy** gives $I_A = \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$.
- ŌśÉ **Latching condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.
- ŌśÉ **Turn-OFF methods:** Natural, Class A (load), Class B (resonant), Class C (capacitor), Class D (external pulse).
- ŌśÉ **MOSFET** = voltage-controlled, majority carrier, *fast* ($>$ 100 kHz), high $R_{DS(on)}$ at high voltage.
- ŌśÉ **IGBT** = MOSFET-input + BJT-output, dominant in motor drives ($1$ŌĆō$20$ kHz), low $V_{CE(sat)}$.
- ŌśÉ **Switching power loss** $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_s I_s (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$.
- ŌśÉ **Conduction power loss** $P_{cond} = V_T \cdot I_{rms}$.
- ŌśÉ For **Class B resonant commutation**, peak resonant current $>$ load current is the *key* condition.
- ŌśÉ **dv/dt triggering** is *unwanted* ŌĆö use **snubber circuits** ($R$ŌĆō$C$ across SCR) to suppress.
- ŌśÉ **Device selection rule of thumb:** SCR for high-power low-frequency; IGBT for medium-power VFDs; MOSFET for low-power high-frequency.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES ŌĆö POWER DEVICES**
> *These notes align with KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1ŌĆōL4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse), and Course Outcomes CO2 / CO3 of PCAET601 ŌĆö Electric Drives and Control.*

topics.title: Power BJT, Power MOSFET and IGBT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power BJT, Power MOSFET and IGBT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Power BJT is a three-layer, two-junction, **current-controlled** bipolar semiconductor switching device designed to handle high voltage (up to **1200 V**) and high current (up to **500 A**). It operates in the **saturation region** (ON state) and **cut-off region** (OFF state) when used in power electronic converters. The device requires a continuous base drive current $I_B$ to remain in the ON state, making it fundamentally different from voltage-controlled devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A single Power BJT cell has a low current gain ($h_{FE} \approx 5$ to $10$) at high collector currents. Hence, practical power BJTs are always constructed as a **Darlington pair** (also called a *Sziklai pair* or *composite transistor*) to boost the effective current gain to **$h_{FE(\text{eff})} \approx 50$ to $500$**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Power BJT like a **heavy industrial water tap** (the collector-emitter path) that requires a small hand-pump (the base current) to keep it open. The moment you stop pumping (remove $I_B$), the tap closes. The catch? You must keep pumping continuously for as long as you want the tap open ŌĆö you cannot simply "latch" it open. Also, the tap is *heavy and slow to close* because the water already inside must drain out (this represents the **charge storage** problem of minority carriers in the base region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants/Metrics:**
> - Storage time $t_s$ in BJT: typically **$0.5\ \mu s$ to $5\ \mu s$**
> - Fall time $t_f$: **$0.1\ \mu s$ to $1\ \mu s$**
> - Maximum junction temperature: **$150\ ^{\circ}C$**
> - Current gain $h_{FE}$ at rated current: **$5$ to $10$ (single cell)**

---

### 1.2 Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier** unipolar switching device with a **MOS (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) gate structure**. It is the dominant choice for high-frequency switching applications (up to **$1\ MHz$**) at low-to-medium voltage levels (up to **$1000\ V$**) and moderate currents (up to **$200\ A$**). The device is OFF when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (threshold voltage) and ON when $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.

**Structural Variants (as per KTU syllabus):**
1. **VDMOS (Vertical Double-diffused MOS)** ŌĆö Most common in power electronics
2. **LDMOS (Lateral Double-diffused MOS)** ŌĆö Used in RF and integrated applications
3. **TMOS / HEXFET / SIPMOS** ŌĆö Trade-name equivalents of VDMOS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Because Power MOSFET is a **majority-carrier device**, there is **no minority-carrier storage** in the drift region. This is the fundamental reason MOSFETs switch **100 to 1000 times faster** than BJTs of equivalent rating.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a Power MOSFET as a **modern sensor-activated automatic door**. The door (drain-source channel) opens the instant a person places their hand on the sensor pad (the gate), and there is **no mechanical latch** to release ŌĆö as soon as the hand is removed, the door closes almost instantly. Critically, the sensor needs only a tiny *puff of voltage* (not a continuous current) to operate it, and there's no "memory" of the previous state ŌĆö extremely clean, fast, and efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants/Metrics:**
> - Threshold voltage $V_{th}$: typically **$2\ V$ to $4\ V$**
> - Typical ON-state gate drive: **$+15\ V$**
> - ON-state resistance $R_{DS(\text{on})}$: **milli-ohms** range
> - Maximum junction temperature: **$150\ ^{\circ}C$ to $175\ ^{\circ}C$**

---

### 1.3 IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The IGBT is a **voltage-controlled, three-terminal, four-layer (p$^+$-n$^-$-p-n$^+$)** hybrid semiconductor device that combines the **high input impedance and voltage-driven gate of a MOSFET** with the **low on-state voltage drop and high current-carrying capability of a BJT**. It is the workhorse of medium-to-high power converters (operating from **$600\ V$ to $6500\ V$** and currents up to **$3500\ A$**) and switching frequencies in the range **$1\ kHz$ to $100\ kHz$**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the IGBT as a **"best of both worlds" device** ŌĆö like a **hybrid car** that uses an electric motor (the MOSFET input stage) to silently control a powerful gasoline engine (the BJT output stage). The driver only presses a small electric pedal (voltage on the gate), but the heavy machinery (high current) is handled by the rugged internal combustion side. The trade-off? The "engine" (BJT section) has a slight **tail-current memory** when shutting off, so it is not quite as instantaneous as a pure electric car (MOSFET).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants/Metrics:**
> - Gate-emitter threshold $V_{GE(\text{th})}$: **$4\ V$ to $6\ V$**
> - Recommended ON-state gate drive: **$+15\ V$** (with **$-5\ V$ to $-15\ V$** OFF bias)
> - Tail current time $t_{\text{tail}}$: **$0.1\ \mu s$ to $1\ \mu s$**
> - Maximum junction temperature: **$150\ ^{\circ}C$**
> - Forward voltage drop $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ at rated current: **$1.5\ V$ to $3.5\ V$**

---

### 1.4 Comparative Snapshot Table (KTU 2024 Module-2 Perview)

| **Parameter** | **Power BJT** | **Power MOSFET** | **IGBT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drive Type | Current | Voltage | Voltage |
| Carrier Type | Bipolar (minority) | Unipolar (majority) | Bipolar (minority in drift) |
| Input Impedance | Low | Very High | Very High |
| Switching Frequency | $< 10\ kHz$ | Up to $1\ MHz$ | $1$ to $100\ kHz$ |
| Voltage Rating | Up to $1200\ V$ | Up to $1000\ V$ | Up to $6500\ V$ |
| On-state Drop | Moderate | High ($I^2 R_{DS}$) | Low |
| Anti-parallel Diode | External | Body diode (intrinsic) | External (or co-pack) |
| Secondary Breakdown | Yes (critical issue) | No (absent) | Has latch-up equivalent |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Power BJT ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Construction (Vertical npn Structure)
Unlike a signal-level BJT, a power BJT uses a **vertical geometry** to maximize the cross-sectional area for current flow. The structure consists of:
- A heavily-doped **n$^+$** emitter at the bottom
- A thick, lightly-doped **n$^-$\ drift region** for voltage blocking
- A **p-type base** diffused from the top
- **n$^+$** emitter fingers at the top surface for interdigitated contacts

> [!NOTE]
> The thick **n$^-$\ drift region** is what gives the BJT its high blocking voltage, but it also causes the high on-state voltage drop $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$.

#### 2.1.2 Static Characteristics (Output $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$)
The three operating regions of importance are:
- **Cut-off region** ŌåÆ $I_B = 0$, $I_C \approx 0$ (OFF state)
- **Active region** ŌåÆ $I_C = h_{FE} I_B$ (used only during switching transients)
- **Saturation region** ŌåÆ $V_{CE} = V_{CE(\text{sat})}$, $I_C < h_{FE} I_B$ (ON state)

The device must be **driven into hard saturation** to minimize conduction loss, but **hard saturation increases storage time** ŌĆö a fundamental trade-off in BJT design.

#### 2.1.3 Dynamic Characteristics (Switching Transients)
The four switching intervals are critical for KTU:

- **Turn-ON delay time $t_{d(\text{on})}$**: Time for $I_C$ to rise to $10\%$ of final value. Depends on charging of **base-emitter capacitance**.
- **Rise time $t_r$**: Time for $I_C$ to rise from $10\%$ to $90\%$.
- **Storage time $t_s$**: Time for $I_C$ to fall from $100\%$ to $90\%$ during turn-OFF. **Most dominant** ŌĆö caused by stored minority carriers in the base.
- **Fall time $t_f$**: Time for $I_C$ to fall from $90\%$ to $10\%$.

#### 2.1.4 Switching Loss in Power BJT
Total switching loss per cycle is the area under the $V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ product during turn-ON and turn-OFF transients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Darlington Configuration (KTU High-Priority Topic):**
> A Darlington pair is formed by cascading two BJTs such that the emitter of the driver transistor feeds the base of the output transistor. The effective current gain is **multiplied**:
>
> $$h_{FE(\text{eff})} = h_{FE1} \cdot h_{FE2} + h_{FE1} + h_{FE2} \approx h_{FE1} \cdot h_{FE2}$$
>
> The penalty: increased $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ (sum of both $V_{BE}$ drops, $\approx 2.5\ V$ instead of $1.5\ V$).

---

### 2.2 Power MOSFET ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Construction (VDMOS)
The vertical double-diffused MOSFET has:
- **Source** (n$^+$) and **Body** (p) shorted together at the top
- **Channel** (n) induced under the gate oxide
- **Drift region** (n$^-$) for voltage blocking
- **Drain** (n$^+$) at the bottom

The **gate**, **source**, and **drain** terminals correspond to **G**, **S**, **D** respectively. The gate is **electrically isolated** from the channel by a thin SiO$_2$ layer ŌĆö hence the **extremely high input impedance** ($> 10^9\ \Omega$).

#### 2.2.2 Static Characteristics (Transfer and Output)
- **Transfer characteristic**: For $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the device is OFF ($I_D = 0$).
- For $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, in the **ohmic (triode) region**:
  $$I_D = k \left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$
- In the **active (saturation) region** (used only for linear amplifiers, not in switching converters):
  $$I_D = \frac{k}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$
- In the **ohmic/fully-ON region** (used in power switching):
  $$V_{DS(\text{on})} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(\text{on})}$$
  where $R_{DS(\text{on})}$ is the key figure of merit ŌĆö it is **temperature-dependent** with a **positive temperature coefficient** (PTC), enabling safe paralleling of MOSFETs.

#### 2.2.3 Parasitic Capacitances (KTU Critical)
The MOSFET's three inter-terminal capacitances determine the **gate-drive power** and **switching loss**:

- $C_{GS}$: gate-to-source capacitance (essentially constant)
- $C_{GD}$: gate-to-drain capacitance (**Miller capacitance** ŌĆö nonlinear, voltage-dependent)
- $C_{DS}$: drain-to-source capacitance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Miller effect** during turn-ON/turn-OFF causes a plateau in the $V_{GS}$ waveform at the $V_{GP}$ (Miller plateau) level, where the gate current is used to charge/discharge $C_{GD}$.

#### 2.2.4 Switching Times
- **Turn-ON delay $t_{d(\text{on})}$**: Time for $V_{GS}$ to rise from $0$ to $V_{th}$
- **Rise time $t_r$**: $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_{th}$ to Miller plateau; $V_{DS}$ falls
- **Turn-OFF delay $t_{d(\text{off})}$**: $V_{GS}$ falls from steady-state to Miller plateau
- **Fall time $t_f$**: $V_{DS}$ rises from saturation to $V_{DD}$

#### 2.2.5 Body Diode
Every power MOSFET has an **intrinsic body diode** (also called *anti-parallel* or *commutating diode*) formed by the p-body / n$^-$$-drift junction. This diode can conduct reverse current, making MOSFETs suitable for **bidirectional switches** and **half-bridge** topologies without external anti-parallel diodes.

---

### 2.3 IGBT ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Construction
The IGBT structure is essentially a **VDMOS with an additional p$^+$ substrate layer** at the drain (now called the **collector**). The terminal naming also changes:

- **Gate** (G) ŌĆö same as MOSFET
- **Emitter** (E) ŌĆö corresponds to MOSFET source
- **Collector** (C) ŌĆö corresponds to MOSFET drain (with added p$^+$ layer)

This added p$^+$ layer injects minority carriers (holes) into the n$^-$$-drift region during the ON state, which **conductivity-modulates** the drift region, dramatically reducing its resistance. This is the key reason IGBTs have a much lower on-state drop than MOSFETs of equivalent voltage rating.

#### 2.3.2 Operating Regions
- **Forward blocking**: $V_{CE} > 0$, $V_{GE} < V_{th}$ ŌåÆ OFF state, blocks positive voltage
- **Forward conduction**: $V_{GE} > V_{th}$, $V_{CE} > 0$ ŌåÆ ON state, $V_{CE} \approx V_{CE(\text{sat})}$
- **Reverse blocking**: IGBTs have **limited reverse blocking capability** (unlike MOSFETs). An **anti-parallel diode** is always required in practical circuits.

#### 2.3.3 Static $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ Characteristic
At low collector currents, the $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{GE}$ transfer curve follows a square-law (MOSFET-like), but at high currents, the curve shows a **bending** due to the BJT section's current gain characteristics.

#### 2.3.4 Dynamic Switching Behavior ŌĆö The Tail Current
During turn-OFF, even though the gate is turned off, the **minority carriers stored in the n$^-$\ drift region** (injected by the p$^+$ collector) must recombine or be swept out. This causes a characteristic **tail current** in the $I_C$ waveform:

$$I_C(t) = I_{C(\text{tail})} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{hl}}$$

where $\tau_{hl}$ is the **high-level lifetime** of minority carriers in the drift region. The tail current is the **primary source of turn-OFF switching loss** in IGBTs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latch-up (KTU Examiner's Pet Topic):**
> If the collector current exceeds a critical value $I_{C(\text{latch})}$, the parasitic thyristor formed by the four-layer IGBT structure latches ON, and the gate loses control. The IGBT is destroyed if latch-up is not prevented. Modern IGBTs include **buffer layers** and **lifetime-killing** processes to suppress latch-up.

#### 2.3.5 Safe Operating Area (SOA)
The IGBT SOA is a **rectangular boundary** in the $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ plane, defined by:
- Maximum continuous collector current $I_{C(\text{max})}$
- Maximum collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE(\text{max})}$
- Maximum power dissipation $P_{D(\text{max})}$
- Maximum junction temperature $T_{J(\text{max})}$

Unlike BJT, the IGBT SOA is **approximately rectangular** (no secondary breakdown), making it more predictable.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module-2 Essential)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description & Units** | **Applicable Device** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective current gain (Darlington) | $h_{FE(\text{eff})} = h_{FE1} h_{FE2}$ | Effective $\beta$ of Darlington pair | BJT |
| BJT saturation voltage | $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\text{ŌĆō}2.5\ V$ | ON-state drop | BJT |
| MOSFET ON-resistance drop | $V_{DS(\text{on})} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(\text{on})}$ | Conduction loss per device | MOSFET |
| MOSFET conduction loss | $P_{\text{cond}} = I_{D(\text{rms})}^2 \cdot R_{DS(\text{on})}$ | Watts, switching converter | MOSFET |
| MOSFET switching loss | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{r} + t_{f}) f_{sw}$ | Watts per device | MOSFET |
| Gate charge energy | $E_g = Q_g \cdot V_{GS}$ | Joules, drive energy per cycle | MOSFET / IGBT |
| Miller plateau voltage | $V_{GP} \approx V_{th} + \frac{I_D}{g_m}$ | Volts, gate plateau | MOSFET / IGBT |
| IGBT ON-state drop | $V_{CE(\text{sat})} = V_{BE} + I_C R_{\text{drift(mod)}}$ | Modulated drift resistance | IGBT |
| IGBT tail-current decay | $I_C(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau_{hl}}$ | Exponential tail | IGBT |
| BJT storage time | $t_s = \tau_{p} \ln \left( \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{I_{C}/h_{FE} + I_{B2}} \right)$ | Turn-OFF storage | BJT |
| IGBT switching loss | $E_{\text{off}} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CE} I_C t_{\text{tail}}$ | Tail-loss energy | IGBT |
| Thermal resistance | $T_J = T_A + P_D (R_{\theta JA})$ | $R_{\theta JA}$ in $^\circ C/W$ | All |
| Junction temp rise | $\Delta T_J = P_D \cdot R_{\theta JC}$ | $R_{\theta JC}$ in $^\circ C/W$ | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Power BJT** is used in older UPS systems, audio amplifiers, and very-high-current low-voltage regulators.
> - **Power MOSFET** dominates SMPS, DC-DC converters, synchronous rectifiers, low-voltage motor drives, and Class-D audio.
> - **IGBT** is the **industry standard** for VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives), solar inverters, traction drives, HVDC, induction heating, and electric vehicle powertrains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power BJT Switching Losses

We derive the total switching loss per cycle for a BJT used in a hard-switched chopper circuit. The chopper switches a DC bus voltage $V_{CC}$ and carries a load current $I_L$ (assumed constant due to a highly inductive load).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the switching waveforms.**
During turn-ON, $V_{CE}$ falls from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ while $I_C$ rises from $0$ to $I_L$. The transition is assumed linear. Define:
- $V_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_{r}} \right)$ for $0 \le t \le t_r$
- $I_C(t) = I_L \cdot \frac{t}{t_{r}}$ for $0 \le t \le t_r$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute turn-ON switching energy.**
The instantaneous power dissipated is $p(t) = V_{CE}(t) \cdot I_C(t)$.

$$p(t) = V_{CC} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_{r}} \right) \cdot I_L \cdot \frac{t}{t_{r}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{t_{r}} \left( t - \frac{t^2}{t_r} \right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate over the rise interval $0$ to $t_r$.**

$$E_{\text{on}} = \int_{0}^{t_r} p(t)\, dt = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{t_{r}} \int_{0}^{t_r} \left( t - \frac{t^2}{t_r} \right) dt$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{t_r} t\, dt = \frac{t_r^2}{2}, \qquad \int_{0}^{t_r} \frac{t^2}{t_r} dt = \frac{t_r^2}{3}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute and simplify.**

$$E_{\text{on}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{t_{r}} \left( \frac{t_r^2}{2} - \frac{t_r^2}{3} \right) = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{t_{r}} \cdot \frac{t_r^2}{6} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L t_r}{6}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the same logic to the turn-OFF phase (using $t_f$).**

$$E_{\text{off}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L t_f}{6}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Total switching energy per cycle.**

$$E_{\text{sw,total}} = E_{\text{on}} + E_{\text{off}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{6} (t_r + t_f)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Switching power loss (multiply by switching frequency $f_{sw}$).**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{sw}} = f_{sw} \cdot \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{6} (t_r + t_f)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (KTU application):** The result shows that switching loss scales **linearly with frequency** but **linearly with the product $V_{CC} \cdot I_L$** and rise/fall times. Reducing $t_r$ and $t_f$ is the most direct way to cut losses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: IGBT Tail-Current Switching Loss

The unique feature of an IGBT's turn-OFF is the **tail current**. The total turn-OFF energy consists of two parts: (1) the *fall-time* component and (2) the *tail-decay* component.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Tail current exponential model.**
Immediately after the Miller plateau, the collector current decays as:
$$I_C(t) = I_{C0} \, e^{-t/\tau_{hl}}$$
where $I_{C0}$ is the current at the start of the tail ($t = 0$) and $\tau_{hl}$ is the high-level carrier lifetime.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage approximation.**
During the tail, the collector-emitter voltage is approximately equal to the DC bus $V_{CC}$ (the device is already OFF, blocking the full supply).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Instantaneous power during tail.**

$$p(t) = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C0} \, e^{-t/\tau_{hl}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = \infty$ (practical integration to $5\tau_{hl}$ suffices).**

$$E_{\text{tail}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{CC} I_{C0} e^{-t/\tau_{hl}} dt = V_{CC} I_{C0} \cdot \tau_{hl}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total IGBT turn-OFF energy (fall-time component $E_f$ plus tail component).**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{off,IGBT}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{C0} t_f}{6} + V_{CC} I_{C0} \tau_{hl}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö IGBT total switching loss.**

$$P_{\text{sw,IGBT}} = f_{sw} \left( \frac{V_{CC} I_{C0} t_f}{6} + V_{CC} I_{C0} \tau_{hl} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tail-Current Penalty (KTU Insight):** Even with a perfect *zero* fall time ($t_f = 0$), the IGBT would still dissipate a switching loss proportional to $\tau_{hl}$. This is **why IGBTs cannot switch at MHz frequencies** like MOSFETs, and why the IGBT industry invests heavily in *lifetime control* and *thin-wafer technology* to shrink $\tau_{hl}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Power MOSFET Drive Power

**Step 1 ŌĆö Gate charge equation.**
The total charge delivered to the gate per turn-ON cycle is the gate charge $Q_g$, supplied by the gate driver at voltage $V_{GS(\text{on})}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Energy per charge transfer.**

$$E_g = Q_g \cdot V_{GS(\text{on})}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Drive power per cycle (also requires $Q_g$ on turn-OFF, assuming $V_{GS(\text{off})} \approx 0$ or negative).**

$$E_{g,\text{cycle}} = Q_g (V_{GS(\text{on})} - V_{GS(\text{off})})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Average drive power.**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{drive}} = Q_g \, (V_{GS(\text{on})} - V_{GS(\text{off})}) \cdot f_{sw}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** At high $f_{sw}$ (say $500\ kHz$) with a large MOSFET ($Q_g = 100\ nC$, $V_{GS} = 12\ V$), the drive power alone is $P_{\text{drive}} = 100 \times 10^{-9} \times 12 \times 500 \times 10^3 = 0.6\ W$. This must be supplied by the gate-drive IC.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When to Choose Which Device?

**Selection logic (following the KTU Module-2 design heuristics):**

Let us define the design requirement space using two parameters:
- **Voltage rating $V_{\text{rated}}$** (DC bus voltage level)
- **Switching frequency $f_{sw}$** (kHz)

| **Application Domain** | **$V_{\text{rated}}$** | **$f_{sw}$** | **Best Device** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-voltage SMPS ($< 100\ V$) | Low | High (100 kHz ŌĆō 1 MHz) | **MOSFET** | $R_{DS(\text{on})}$ is low at low $V$ |
| Synchronous rectification ($< 50\ V$) | Very Low | Very High | **MOSFET** | Body diode + ultra-low $R_{DS(\text{on})}$ |
| Three-phase VFD ($400\ V$ ŌĆō $690\ V$) | Medium | Medium ($2$ ŌĆō $20\ kHz$) | **IGBT** | Better $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ than MOSFET $I^2 R$ |
| Solar PV inverter (string) | Medium ($600$ ŌĆō $1500\ V$) | Medium ($16$ ŌĆō $50\ kHz$) | **IGBT or SiC MOSFET** | IGBT standard, SiC emerging |
| Traction / locomotive | High ($1.5$ ŌĆō $4\ kV$) | Low ($1$ ŌĆō $5\ kHz$) | **IGBT** | High voltage, current capability |
| HVDC / STATCOM | Very High ($> 4.5\ kV$) | Low | **IGBT (press-pack)** | Series connection feasible |
| Audio amplifier (Class-D) | Low | Very High ($> 250\ kHz$) | **MOSFET** | Linear region, low distortion |
| Induction heating | Medium | Medium-High | **IGBT** | High current, rugged SOA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb taught in KTU 2024 syllabus:** If the product $V \cdot f_{sw}$ exceeds **$10^{10}\ V \cdot Hz$**, MOSFET becomes less efficient than IGBT due to the $I^2 R_{DS}$ loss; below this boundary, MOSFET is preferred. Beyond **$6500\ V$**, IGBT dominates as MOSFET voltage ratings plateau.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A BJT is used in a $200\ V$ DC chopper circuit, switching a constant load current of $50\ A$. The rise time $t_r = 1.5\ \mu s$ and fall time $t_f = 2\ \mu s$. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 5\ kHz$. Compute the switching loss.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute total switching energy per cycle using the derived formula.**

$$E_{\text{sw,total}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{6} (t_r + t_f)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$E_{\text{sw,total}} = \frac{200 \times 50}{6} \times (1.5 + 2) \times 10^{-6}\ J$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify numerator.**

$$\frac{200 \times 50}{6} = \frac{10000}{6} \approx 1666.67\ VA$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Add rise and fall times.**

$$t_r + t_f = 3.5\ \mu s = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\ s$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute energy.**

$$E_{\text{sw,total}} = 1666.67 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-6} = 5.833 \times 10^{-3}\ J = 5.83\ mJ$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute average power loss.**

$$P_{\text{sw}} = E_{\text{sw,total}} \times f_{sw} = 5.83 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^3 = 29.17\ W$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{sw}} \approx 29.2\ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The BJT dissipates $\approx 29\ W$ in switching alone. The thermal designer must ensure the heatsink's $R_{\theta SA}$ keeps $T_J$ below $150\ ^{\circ}C$ when this loss is added to conduction loss.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **What is a Darlington pair? Why is it used in power BJTs? Mention its main drawback.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Darlington pair is a composite configuration of two BJTs connected such that the emitter of the first (driver) transistor feeds the base of the second (output) transistor, and their collectors are tied together. It is used in power BJTs because a single power BJT cell has a low current gain ($h_{FE} \approx 5$ to $10$) at high collector currents, and the Darlington configuration multiplies the effective current gain to $h_{FE(\text{eff})} = h_{FE1} \cdot h_{FE2}$ (often **$500$ to $1000$**), reducing the base drive current requirement. The main drawback is the **higher on-state saturation voltage** $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 2.5\ V$ (sum of two $V_{BE}$ drops), leading to greater conduction loss.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **List any three advantages of IGBT over Power MOSFET for high-power applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lower on-state voltage drop** ŌĆö IGBTs have a conductivity-modulated drift region, so $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ is much lower than the equivalent MOSFET's $I^2 R_{DS(\text{on})}$ drop, especially at voltages above **$500\ V$**.
2. **Higher voltage and current ratings** ŌĆö IGBTs are commercially available up to **$6500\ V$** and **$3500\ A$**, far exceeding MOSFETs (which top out around **$1000\ V$**).
3. **Better thermal performance at high power** ŌĆö The BJT output section handles the current with lower conduction loss, resulting in a smaller die size for the same power rating.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module-2 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and operating principle of a **Power MOSFET (VDMOS)**. Compare its switching characteristics with that of a **Power BJT**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **switching power loss in a hard-switched power BJT** with a constant load current. A power BJT is used in a $400\ V$ chopper carrying a load current of $100\ A$. The rise time is $1\ \mu s$ and fall time is $1.5\ \mu s$. If the switching frequency is $10\ kHz$, calculate the switching loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Power MOSFET vs Power BJT (7 marks):**

The Power MOSFET (VDMOS) has a **vertical four-layer structure** consisting of: (1) a heavily doped **n$^+$** drain substrate at the bottom, (2) a lightly doped **n$^-$** drift region that supports the high blocking voltage, (3) **p-body** and **n$^+$ source** regions diffused from the top, and (4) a **polysilicon gate** isolated from the channel by a thin SiO$_2$ layer.

**Operating Principle:** When a positive voltage $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ is applied between gate and source, an n-type **inversion layer (channel)** is induced under the gate oxide, connecting the **n$^+$ source** to the **n$^-$ drift region** and hence to the **n$^+$ drain**. Current flows vertically from drain to source. Removing $V_{GS}$ removes the channel, and the device turns OFF. Because the gate is electrically isolated, **no continuous gate current** is required ŌĆö only charge to charge/discharge the input capacitance $C_{iss}$.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Power BJT** | **Power MOSFET** |
|---|---|---|
| Control parameter | Base current $I_B$ | Gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Input impedance | Low | Extremely high ($> 10^9\ \Omega$) |
| Switching speed | Slow (storage time) | Very fast (no minority storage) |
| Frequency | $< 10\ kHz$ | Up to $1\ MHz$ |
| Drive power | High | Negligible |
| On-state drop | $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2$ to $2.5\ V$ | $I_D \cdot R_{DS(\text{on})}$ |
| Secondary breakdown | Yes | No |
| SOA | Limited | Rectangular |

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Switching Loss Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

From the derivation in **Section 3.1**, the total switching power loss in a hard-switched BJT is:

$$P_{\text{sw}} = f_{sw} \cdot \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{6} (t_r + t_f)$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- $V_{CC} = 400\ V$
- $I_L = 100\ A$
- $t_r = 1\ \mu s = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ s$
- $t_f = 1.5\ \mu s = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\ s$
- $f_{sw} = 10\ kHz = 10^4\ Hz$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sum rise and fall times.**

$$t_r + t_f = 2.5\ \mu s = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\ s$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute switching energy per cycle.**

$$E_{\text{sw}} = \frac{400 \times 100}{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{40000}{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \approx 6666.67 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{\text{sw}} \approx 1.667 \times 10^{-2}\ J = 16.67\ mJ$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply by switching frequency.**

$$P_{\text{sw}} = 16.67 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4 = 166.67\ W$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{sw}} \approx 166.7\ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor **$1/6$** (incorrectly using $1/2$) and obtain $500\ W$, which is a 3├Ś error. The factor of 6 arises from integrating the linear product of two linearly-varying waveforms (V and I) over the transition interval ŌĆö remember this key integration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **construction, operating principle, and switching characteristics of an IGBT**. Discuss the **tail-current phenomenon** and how it limits the switching frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-phase voltage source inverter uses **IGBTs** rated at $1200\ V$, $300\ A$ for driving a $400\ V$ induction motor. The DC bus voltage is $600\ V$, the load current per switch is $80\ A$ (constant), the fall time is $0.3\ \mu s$, and the tail-current time constant is $\tau_{hl} = 0.4\ \mu s$. The switching frequency is $8\ kHz$. Compute the **turn-OFF switching loss per IGBT** and comment on the thermal design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö IGBT Construction, Principle, Tail Current (7 marks):**

The IGBT is a **four-layer (p$^+$-n$^-$-p-n$^+$) hybrid device**. Its structure is essentially identical to a VDMOS, with **one critical addition** ŌĆö a **p$^+$ substrate layer** at the collector (drain) end.

**Operating Principle:** When $V_{GE} > V_{GE(\text{th})}$, an n-channel is induced in the p-body region, exactly as in a MOSFET. Electrons flow from the n$^+$ emitter through the channel into the n$^-$ drift region. These electrons cause the **p$^+$ collector to inject holes** (minority carriers) into the n$^-$ drift region. This hole injection **conductivity-modulates** the n$^-$ drift region, drastically reducing its resistance. As a result, the IGBT exhibits the **low on-state voltage drop of a BJT** with the **easy voltage gate-drive of a MOSFET**.

**Tail-Current Phenomenon:**
During turn-OFF, the gate voltage is removed and the channel pinches off, but the **holes stored in the n$^-$ drift region** must either recombine (slow process) or be collected by the p$^+$ collector. The current does not fall to zero abruptly ŌĆö it decays exponentially:

$$I_C(t) = I_{C0} \, e^{-t/\tau_{hl}}$$

This **tail current** is the primary source of turn-OFF switching loss and **limits the maximum switching frequency** of IGBTs to about $20$ to $50\ kHz$ for most commercial modules. Higher frequencies are achievable with newer SiC and GaN-based IGBTs.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö IGBT Turn-OFF Loss Numerical (7 marks):**

From the derivation in **Section 3.2**, the total turn-OFF energy per IGBT is:

$$E_{\text{off,IGBT}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{C0} t_f}{6} + V_{CC} I_{C0} \tau_{hl}$$

**Given data:**
- $V_{CC} = 600\ V$ (DC bus)
- $I_{C0} = 80\ A$ (load current)
- $t_f = 0.3\ \mu s = 0.3 \times 10^{-6}\ s$
- $\tau_{hl} = 0.4\ \mu s = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}\ s$
- $f_{sw} = 8\ kHz = 8 \times 10^3\ Hz$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute fall-time component.**

$$E_f = \frac{600 \times 80 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-6}}{6} = \frac{48000 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-6}}{6} = \frac{14.4 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 2.4\ mJ$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute tail-current component.**

$$E_{\text{tail}} = 600 \times 80 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6} = 48000 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6} = 19.2\ mJ$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total turn-OFF energy per cycle.**

$$E_{\text{off,total}} = 2.4 + 19.2 = 21.6\ mJ$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total turn-OFF power loss.**

$$P_{\text{off}} = E_{\text{off,total}} \times f_{sw} = 21.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^3 = 172.8\ W$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{off}} \approx 172.8\ W \text{ per IGBT}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Thermal design comment.**
The thermal designer must verify that $P_{\text{off}}$ plus the conduction loss ($V_{CE(\text{sat})} \cdot I_C \cdot D$, where $D$ is duty) does not raise the junction temperature above $150\ ^{\circ}C$. A typical TO-247 IGBT has $R_{\theta JC} \approx 0.4\ ^{\circ}C/W$, so the temperature rise across the die-to-case interface would be $\Delta T_{JC} = 0.4 \times (172.8 + 60) \approx 93\ ^{\circ}C$. A high-performance heatsink with $R_{\theta SA} < 0.3\ ^{\circ}C/W$ is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** Students often **ignore the tail-current component** and compute only $E_f$, obtaining an answer around $19.2\ W$ instead of the correct $172.8\ W$. The **tail current is the dominant loss mechanism** in IGBTs and is the single most important concept in Module 2. Always include both terms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 ŌĆö Power BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT**

- [ ] **Power BJT** is a **current-controlled**, **bipolar** device; single-cell $h_{FE}$ is low ($\approx 5$ to $10$).
- [ ] **Darlington pair** multiplies current gain but doubles the $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ drop.
- [ ] **Storage time $t_s$** is the dominant switching interval in BJTs; it limits frequency to $< 10\ kHz$.
- [ ] **Power MOSFET** is a **voltage-controlled**, **unipolar** device; extremely high input impedance.
- [ ] **Majority-carrier conduction** in MOSFETs means **no storage time** ŌåÆ switching is **100├Ś to 1000├Ś faster** than BJT.
- [ ] **Miller plateau** in $V_{GS}$ waveform is caused by $C_{GD}$ charging/discharging through the gate resistor.
- [ ] **$R_{DS(\text{on})}$ has a positive temperature coefficient** ŌåÆ MOSFETs can be safely paralleled.
- [ ] **Body diode** of a MOSFET is an intrinsic feature; it acts as a freewheeling diode in half-bridge circuits.
- [ ] **IGBT** combines MOSFET input + BJT output; the **p$^+$ collector layer** enables **conductivity modulation**.
- [ ] **Tail current** during IGBT turn-OFF is the **dominant switching loss**; it limits IGBT frequency to $\approx 20$ŌĆō$50\ kHz$.
- [ ] **Latch-up** is the IGBT's worst-case failure mode; caused by the **parasitic thyristor** structure.
- [ ] **IGBT SOA is rectangular** (no secondary breakdown), making it more predictable than BJT.
- [ ] **BJT switching loss formula**: $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot \frac{V_{CC} I_L}{6} (t_r + t_f)$ ŌĆö remember the factor **$1/6$**.
- [ ] **IGBT turn-OFF energy formula**: $E_{\text{off}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_C t_f}{6} + V_{CC} I_C \tau_{hl}$ ŌĆö always include **both terms**.
- [ ] **Device selection rule of thumb** (Module 2): MOSFET preferred for $V < 500\ V$ and $f_{sw} > 200\ kHz$; IGBT preferred for $V > 500\ V$ and $f_{sw} < 50\ kHz$.
- [ ] **Gate drive requirements**: BJT needs continuous $I_B$ (Baker clamp / proportional driver); MOSFET and IGBT need $+15\ V$ ON (and $-5$ to $-15\ V$ OFF for IGBT to prevent spurious turn-on).
- [ ] **Thermal design** must consider $P_{\text{cond}} + P_{\text{sw}} = P_{\text{total}}$ with $R_{\theta JA}$ chosen so that $T_J < 150\ ^{\circ}C$.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Steady State Characteristics of Power Semiconductor Devices

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady State Characteristics** refer to the behavior of a power semiconductor device (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT) when it operates in a fully **ON** (saturation) or fully **OFF** (cut-off) condition for a sustained period, with no transient switching losses considered. These characteristics define the **on-state voltage drop (V<sub>CE(sat)</sub>, V<sub>DS(on)</sub>, V<sub>CE(on)</sub>)**, **on-state current (I<sub>C</sub>, I<sub>D</sub>)**, and **blocking voltage** capabilities under continuous operating conditions.

The three key power devices in Module 2 have distinct steady-state signatures:

| Device | ON-State Parameter | OFF-State Parameter | Controlling Terminal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power BJT** | V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> Ōēł 1 ŌĆō 2 V | V<sub>CEO</sub> (collector-emitter breakdown) | Base current I<sub>B</sub> |
| **Power MOSFET** | R<sub>DS(on)</sub> (m╬® range) | V<sub>DSS</sub> (drain-source breakdown) | Gate-source voltage V<sub>GS</sub> |
| **IGBT** | V<sub>CE(on)</sub> Ōēł 2 ŌĆō 3 V | V<sub>CES</sub> (collector-emitter breakdown) | Gate-emitter voltage V<sub>GE</sub> |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In exam questions, students are frequently asked to *compare* the steady-state V-I curves of BJT, MOSFET, and IGBT. Memorize that MOSFET behaves as a **resistive device** in conduction (V ŌłØ I), while BJT and IGBT exhibit a **constant voltage drop** characteristic.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Tap" Model
Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline controlled by a tap:
- The **fully open tap** represents the device in **steady-state ON** ŌĆö water flows freely, but the tap still offers a small mechanical resistance (analogous to V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> or R<sub>DS(on)</sub>).
- The **fully closed tap** represents **steady-state OFF** ŌĆö the pipe holds back the full pressure (analogous to the device blocking the supply voltage V<sub>DC</sub>).
- The **partial opening** of the tap is **forbidden** for these power devices because it causes massive power dissipation ŌĆö this is exactly why we switch them rapidly between ON and OFF instead of operating them in the linear (active) region.

---

## 1.2 Switching Characteristics

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switching Characteristics** describe the **transient behavior** of a power device during the transition between OFF and ON states (and vice versa). They are quantified by four key time intervals: **turn-on delay (t<sub>d(on)</sub>)**, **rise time (t<sub>r</sub>)**, **turn-off delay (t<sub>d(off)</sub>)**, and **fall time (t<sub>f</sub>)**. These times determine the device's **switching losses** and **maximum operating frequency**.

### Switching Waveform (Generic Power Switch)
During each switching transition, both **voltage and current** change simultaneously, creating a region of overlap where instantaneous power dissipation P = v(t)┬Ęi(t) can be very large.

**Turn-ON sequence:** t<sub>d(on)</sub> ŌåÆ t<sub>r</sub> ŌåÆ Device fully ON
**Turn-OFF sequence:** t<sub>d(off)</sub> ŌåÆ t<sub>f</sub> ŌåÆ Device fully OFF

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Elevator" Model
Think of an elevator moving between floors:
- **Turn-on delay (t<sub>d(on)</sub>)** is the time the elevator door takes to *unlock* before it starts moving (control signal applied, but no movement yet).
- **Rise time (t<sub>r</sub>)** is how long the elevator actually *accelerates* from ground floor to top floor.
- **Turn-off delay (t<sub>d(off)</sub>)** is the time the elevator *coasts* before brakes engage.
- **Fall time (t<sub>f</sub>)** is the actual *deceleration* to a complete stop.

The faster the elevator moves (shorter times), the less energy is wasted during transitions.

---

## 1.3 Drive Requirements for Power Devices

> [!NOTE]
> **Drive Circuit** is the interface circuitry between a low-power logic signal (from a microcontroller/digital controller) and the power device's controlling terminal (Base for BJT, Gate for MOSFET/IGBT). The drive circuit must provide sufficient **voltage and current** levels with proper **isolation** and **protection**.

### 1.3.1 Power BJT Base Drive Requirements
- Continuous base current I<sub>B</sub> Ōēź I<sub>C</sub>/╬▓<sub>forced</sub> for saturation
- A **negative base current** of magnitude **1.5 ├Ś I<sub>B(sat)</sub>** is required during turn-off to remove stored minority carriers quickly
- **Anti-saturation** (Baker's clamp) may be needed to reduce t<sub>off</sub>

### 1.3.2 Power MOSFET Gate Drive Requirements
- Gate-source voltage V<sub>GS</sub> = **+15 V** (typical) to fully enhance the channel
- Gate is **capacitively coupled** ŌĆö peak gate current i<sub>G</sub> = C<sub>iss</sub> ┬Ę dV<sub>GS</sub>/dt
- A **pull-down resistor** of 10 k╬® to 100 k╬® is essential to prevent spurious turn-on from dV/dt

### 1.3.3 IGBT Gate Drive Requirements
- Forward V<sub>GE</sub> = **+15 V** for full conduction
- Reverse V<sub>GE</sub> = **ŌłÆ5 V to ŌłÆ15 V** during turn-off to ensure fast removal of gate charge
- Gate resistance R<sub>G</sub> (typically **10 ╬® to 100 ╬®**) controls the dV/dt and dI/dt

---

## 1.4 Principle of DC Motor Control

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Motor Speed Control** is the technique of regulating the rotational speed (N) of a DC motor by varying either the **armature voltage (V<sub>a</sub>)**, the **field flux (╬”)**, or both. The fundamental speed equation is: **N ŌłØ (V<sub>a</sub> ŌłÆ I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub>) / ╬”**

### Two Primary Methods
1. **Armature Voltage Control** (constant field): Used below base speed. Provides good speed regulation.
2. **Field Flux Control** (constant armature voltage): Used above base speed. Weakening the field increases speed but reduces torque.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bicycle" Model
- **Armature control** is like changing the *gear ratio* (effort applied at the pedals) while the road slope remains constant.
- **Field control** is like changing the *road slope itself* ŌĆö easier to pedal (higher speed) but with less pushing force (lower torque).

---

## 1.5 Principle of PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Switching Control

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM Control** is a switching technique where a power semiconductor is rapidly turned ON and OFF at a fixed switching frequency (f<sub>s</sub>), and the **duty cycle (D)** is varied to control the average voltage or current delivered to the load.

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

The average output voltage for a DC-DC chopper is:

$$V_{o(avg)} = D \cdot V_s$$

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Faucet Pulsing" Model
Imagine a faucet that you open and close very rapidly. By adjusting *how long* you keep it open per cycle (duty cycle), you control the **average water flow** without actually changing the water pressure. PWM works the same way ŌĆö it controls average power without dissipating energy in a linear element.

---

## 1.6 Two-Quadrant and Four-Quadrant Converter Circuits

### Operating Quadrants of a Drive
A motor drive operates in four quadrants defined by the **signs of speed (N)** and **torque (T)**:

| Quadrant | Speed | Torque | Operation Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | + (Forward) | + (Motoring) | Forward Motoring |
| **II** | + (Forward) | ŌłÆ (Generating) | Forward Braking |
| **III** | ŌłÆ (Reverse) | ŌłÆ (Motoring) | Reverse Motoring |
| **IV** | ŌłÆ (Reverse) | + (Generating) | Reverse Braking |

- **Two-Quadrant Chopper**: Operates in Quadrants I and II (or III and IV) ŌĆö uses a **half-bridge** topology with one switch and one diode per quadrant pair.
- **Four-Quadrant Chopper (H-Bridge)**: Operates in all four quadrants ŌĆö uses **four switches** arranged in an H-pattern.

---

## 1.7 Controlled Rectifiers

> [!NOTE]
> **Controlled Rectifiers** are AC-to-DC converter circuits using **thyristors (SCRs)** as switching elements. By adjusting the **firing angle (╬▒)**, the average DC output voltage can be smoothly varied from zero to the maximum possible value.

| Topology | Output Voltage Equation | Conduction Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Phase Half-Wave** | V<sub>o(avg)</sub> = (V<sub>m</sub>/2ŽĆ)(1 + cos ╬▒) | Discontinuous |
| **Single-Phase Full-Wave (Semi-Converter)** | V<sub>o(avg)</sub> = (V<sub>m</sub>/ŽĆ)(1 + cos ╬▒) | Continuous (with FWD) |
| **Single-Phase Full-Wave (Full Converter)** | V<sub>o(avg)</sub> = (2V<sub>m</sub>/ŽĆ) cos ╬▒ | Continuous |

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steady-State V-I Characteristics ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)
**Why these characteristics matter:** A BJT is a **current-controlled device**. The collector current I<sub>C</sub> depends on the base current I<sub>B</sub> through the current gain ╬▓. In saturation, the collector-emitter voltage drops to **V<sub>CE(sat)</sub>**, and the device behaves almost like a closed switch.

**Key steady-state points:**
- **Cut-off region:** V<sub>CE</sub> Ōēź V<sub>CEO</sub> (breakdown), I<sub>C</sub> Ōēł 0
- **Active region:** I<sub>C</sub> = ╬▓┬ĘI<sub>B</sub>, V<sub>CE</sub> > V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> (used only for linear amplifiers, NOT in drives)
- **Saturation region:** V<sub>CE</sub> = V<sub>CE(sat)</sub> Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 V, I<sub>C</sub> is limited by external load

**Why BJT is rarely used in modern drives:** It has a **tail current** during turn-off due to stored minority carriers in the base, leading to longer switching times and higher losses.

### 2.1.2 Power MOSFET
**Why these characteristics matter:** A MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled device** with majority carrier conduction (no minority carrier storage). It exhibits:
- An extremely fast switching speed (nanoseconds)
- A resistive conduction characteristic: V<sub>DS</sub> = I<sub>D</sub> ┬Ę R<sub>DS(on)</sub>
- A **body diode** (anti-parallel) inherently present, useful as a freewheeling path

**Key steady-state points:**
- **Cut-off:** V<sub>GS</sub> < V<sub>th</sub> (threshold Ōēł 2ŌĆō4 V), I<sub>D</sub> = 0
- **Ohmic/Triode region:** V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>th</sub> and V<sub>DS</sub> small ŌåÆ R<sub>DS(on)</sub> controls current
- **Active/Pinch-off:** V<sub>DS</sub> > V<sub>GS</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>th</sub> ŌåÆ constant current region

**Limitation:** Higher conduction loss at high currents due to R<sub>DS(on)</sub> rising with breakdown voltage rating.

### 2.1.3 IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)
**Why this device dominates modern drives:** The IGBT combines the **easy gate drive of a MOSFET** (voltage-controlled, high input impedance) with the **low conduction drop of a BJT** (minority carrier conduction at the output). It is the workhorse of medium-to-high power drives (1 kW to several MW).

**Key steady-state points:**
- **Cut-off:** V<sub>GE</sub> < V<sub>GE(th)</sub> (Ōēł 5ŌĆō6 V)
- **Active region:** I<sub>C</sub> = g<sub>m</sub>(V<sub>GE</sub> ŌłÆ V<sub>GE(th)</sub>)
- **Saturation region:** V<sub>CE</sub> = V<sub>CE(on)</sub> Ōēł 2ŌĆō3 V, current limited by external load

---

## 2.2 Switching Characteristics ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Turn-ON Process (Generic)
The four sub-intervals during turn-ON are:

1. **t<sub>d(on)</sub> (turn-on delay):** Control signal applied, but device current has not yet started rising. The device's internal capacitances (C<sub>BE</sub>, C<sub>BC</sub> for BJT; C<sub>GS</sub>, C<sub>GD</sub> for MOSFET/IGBT) are being charged.
2. **t<sub>r</sub> (rise time):** Device current rises from 10% to 90% of its final value, while voltage falls from 90% to 10%.
3. **Voltage fall time t<sub>fv</sub>:** Voltage continues to fall to its on-state value.
4. **Current tail (IGBT only):** Minority carrier removal from the drift region.

### 2.2.2 Turn-OFF Process (Generic)
1. **t<sub>d(off)</sub> (turn-off delay):** Control signal removed, but device still conducting. Capacitances begin to discharge.
2. **t<sub>f</sub> (fall time):** Current falls from 90% to 10% of its initial value.
3. **Voltage rise time t<sub>rv</sub>:** Voltage rises to the blocking value.
4. **Current tail (IGBT/BJT):** Stored charge removal ŌĆö the most critical loss-producing phase.

### 2.2.3 Why Switching Losses Occur
During the brief overlap of high voltage and high current, instantaneous power P(t) = v(t)┬Ęi(t) is large. The energy lost per switching event is:

$$E_{sw} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

For a linearized approximation, both BJT and IGBT have similar switching loss expressions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master every row of this table** ŌĆö these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU Module 2 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Duty Cycle (PWM/Chopper)** | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_s}$ | $T_s = T_{on} + T_{off}$ |
| 2 | **Chopper Avg Output Voltage** | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | Step-down chopper |
| 3 | **Chopper Output Current Ripple** | $\Delta I_o = \dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{f_s L}$ | For continuous conduction |
| 4 | **DC Motor Speed Equation** | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | SI units, $k\Phi$ is back-emf constant |
| 5 | **DC Motor Armature EMF** | $E_b = k \Phi N$ | V per rpm (or rad/s) |
| 6 | **DC Motor Torque Equation** | $T = k \Phi I_a$ | Newton-meters |
| 7 | **Power Loss in Armature** | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Copper (I┬▓R) loss |
| 8 | **Switching Loss per Cycle** | $E_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Linearized overlap |
| 9 | **Average Switching Power Loss** | $P_{sw} = f_s \cdot E_{sw}$ | Total across all switches |
| 10 | **Single-Phase Full Converter Output** | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$ | For $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 11 | **Single-Phase Semi-Converter Output** | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$ | With free-wheeling diode |
| 12 | **Three-Phase Full Converter Output** | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi/6$ (continuous) |
| 13 | **Power MOSFET Conduction Loss** | $P_{cond} = I_{D,rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Resistive model |
| 14 | **IGBT Conduction Loss** | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(on)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_d I_{C,rms}^2$ | Two-term model |
| 15 | **BJT Forced Beta** | $\beta_{forced} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | $I_B$ in saturation region |
| 16 | **H-Bridge Quadrant (Diagonal Switch ON)** | $V_{out} = +V_s$ or $-V_s$ | Forward/Reverse motoring |
| 17 | **H-Bridge Quadrant (Adjacent Switch ON)** | $V_{out} = 0$ | Freewheeling |
| 18 | **Regenerative Braking Power** | $P_{regen} = T \cdot \omega$ (negative) | Returns energy to source |
| 19 | **Base Drive Negative Current** | $I_{B2} = 1.5 \cdot I_{B1}$ | For fast BJT turn-off |
| 20 | **IGBT Tail Loss Approximation** | $E_{tail} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_L t_{tail}$ | $t_{tail}$ Ōēł 0.1ŌĆō0.3 ┬Ąs |

## 2.5 Analytical Reasoning ŌĆö Why These Equations Have This Form

**Speed Equation Derivation (Physical Reasoning):**
The induced back-EMF in a DC motor armature is E<sub>b</sub> = k╬”N. Kirchhoff's voltage law around the armature circuit gives V<sub>a</sub> = E<sub>b</sub> + I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub>. Solving for N:

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$$

The minus sign reveals the *load regulation* ŌĆö as torque demand increases, I<sub>a</sub> increases, drop across R<sub>a</sub> increases, and speed decreases. This is why **armature control** has inferior speed regulation at low speeds.

**PWM Output Voltage (Physical Reasoning):**
During T<sub>on</sub>, the load is connected to V<sub>s</sub>. During T<sub>off</sub>, the load is isolated (or shorted through a freewheeling diode). The average is simply the time-weighted average:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_0^{T_s} v(t) \, dt = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off}}{T_s} = D \cdot V_s$$

**Controlled Rectifier Output (Physical Reasoning):**
For a full converter, the output is the average of a phase-controlled sinusoid over its conduction interval. With firing angle ╬▒, the integral evaluates to:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

The cosine dependence on ╬▒ gives a smooth linear-like control (for small ╬▒) and reverses polarity at ╬▒ = 90┬░ ŌĆö a key feature of full converters for **two-quadrant DC motor drives**.
