# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI). Distinguish between 180┬░ and 120┬░ conduction modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a DC input voltage into a three-phase AC output of variable magnitude and frequency, using a forced-commutated switching arrangement where the input DC source has **stiff (constant) voltage** characteristics.

| Feature | 180┬░ Conduction | 120┬░ Conduction |
|:--------|:----------------|:----------------|
| Conduction angle per switch | 180┬░ | 120┬░ |
| Number of switches ON at any instant | 3 | 2 |
| Dead-time between phases | None | 60┬░ floating interval |
| Motor starting reliability | Excellent | Poor |
| Utilization of switches | Better | Lower |

**(3 marks)** Ō£ō

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Amplitude Modulation Ratio ($m_a$) and Frequency Modulation Ratio ($m_f$) in SPWM. State the constraints for proper operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Amplitude Modulation Ratio** $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{control}}{\hat{V}_{carrier}}$ ŌĆö controls the **output voltage magnitude**.
- **Frequency Modulation Ratio** $m_f = \dfrac{f_{carrier}}{f_{reference}}$ ŌĆö controls the **number of pulses per cycle**.

**Constraints:**
1. $m_f$ should be an **odd integer** (preferably), especially when $f_o$ is low, to ensure **half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry**, eliminating even harmonics and DC component.
2. $m_f \ge 9$ (preferably odd) for proper sinusoidal approximation.
3. $m_a \le 1.0$ for **linear modulation**; $m_a > 1$ gives **overmodulation** (square-wave approach).
4. $m_f$ should be a **multiple of 3** for three-phase systems to optimize carrier phase relationships.

**(3 marks)** Ō£ō

---

## PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a three-phase voltage source inverter in 180┬░ conduction mode. Draw the phase and line voltage waveforms.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A three-phase VSI consists of **three legs**, each with two switches (S1ŌĆōS4 for phase A, S3ŌĆōS6 for phase B, S5ŌĆōS2 for phase C) connected across a DC source $V_s$. Each switch has an **anti-parallel freewheeling diode** for inductive load current paths.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Switching Sequence Table (2 marks):**

| Interval | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 | S6 |
|:--------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 0ŌĆō60┬░    | 1  | 0  | 0  | 0  | 1  | 1  |
| 60ŌĆō120┬░  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 0  | 0  | 0  |
| 120ŌĆō180┬░ | 0  | 0  | 1  | 1  | 0  | 1  |
| 180ŌĆō240┬░ | 0  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 0  | 0  |
| 240ŌĆō300┬░ | 0  | 1  | 0  | 1  | 1  | 0  |
| 300ŌĆō360┬░ | 1  | 1  | 0  | 0  | 1  | 0  |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Waveform Description (2 marks):**

- **Phase voltage** $V_{AN}$ (with respect to DC midpoint): quasi-square wave with amplitude $\pm V_s/2$, each step of 60┬░.
- **Line voltage** $V_{AB} = V_{AN} - V_{BN}$: three-level stepped waveform with peaks $+\frac{2V_s}{3}$, $0$, $-\frac{2V_s}{3}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Key Points (1 mark):**
- Total of 8 distinct switching states (6 active + 2 zero states).
- Three switches ON at any time (two from one rail, one from other rail).

**(7 marks)** Ō£ō

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the RMS value of the fundamental component of the line-to-line output voltage of a 3-phase VSI operating in 180┬░ conduction mode. Calculate the THD of the line voltage if the total RMS line voltage is $0.8165\,V_s$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Fourier Coefficient of Line Voltage (3 marks):**

From Section 3.2 derivation:

$$
V_{L,n,m} = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{6}\right)
$$

For the fundamental ($n = 1$):

$$
V_{L1,m} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi}\cos(30┬░) = \frac{4V_s \cdot \sqrt{3}}{2\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Fundamental RMS (2 marks):**

$$
V_{L1,rms} = \frac{V_{L1,m}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.7797\,V_s
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö THD Calculation (2 marks):**

Given total RMS $V_{L,rms} = 0.8165\,V_s$:

$$
THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{L,rms}^2 - V_{L1,rms}^2}}{V_{L1,rms}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{(0.8165)^2 - (0.7797)^2}}{0.7797} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{0.6667 - 0.6079}}{0.7797} \times 100\% = \frac{\sqrt{0.0588}}{0.7797} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{THD \approx 31.08\%}
$$

**(7 marks)** Ō£ō

---

### QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) technique for a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for the peak fundamental output voltage in terms of $m_a$ and $V_s$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö SPWM Concept (2 marks):**

In SPWM, a **sinusoidal reference signal** $v_c = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a **triangular carrier wave** $v_{cr}$ of frequency $f_c$. The comparator output is **HIGH** when $v_c > v_{cr}$ and **LOW** otherwise. This generates pulses whose widths vary sinusoidally.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pulse Width Expression (2 marks):**

Over one carrier half-cycle of duration $T_c/2$, the pulse width near angle $\theta$ is:

$$
\delta(\theta) = \frac{T_c}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin(\theta)\right]
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Average Output Voltage in Sub-cycle (1 mark):**

For a unipolar full-bridge inverter with output switching between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$:

$$
\bar{V}_o(\theta) = \frac{V_s \cdot \delta(\theta) - V_s \cdot \left(\frac{T_c}{2} - \delta(\theta)\right)}{T_c/2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplification (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{V}_o(\theta) = V_s \cdot \left[\frac{2\delta(\theta)}{T_c/2} - 1\right] = V_s \cdot m_a \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Peak Fundamental Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{V_{o1,m} = m_a \cdot V_s \quad \text{(full-bridge, unipolar)}}
$$

Or, for **half-bridge bipolar SPWM**:

$$
V_{o1,m} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2}
$$

**(7 marks)** Ō£ō

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase VSI with SPWM control is fed from a 600 V DC bus. The carrier frequency is 2 kHz, and the fundamental output frequency is 50 Hz. The amplitude modulation ratio is $m_a = 0.8$.
   - (i) Compute the frequency modulation ratio $m_f$. (1 mark)
   - (ii) Calculate the peak fundamental phase voltage. (2 marks)
   - (iii) Determine the order of the lowest dominant harmonic cluster. (2 marks)
   - (iv) Comment on the operating region (linear/overmodulation). (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$
m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} = \frac{2000}{50} = 40
$$

Since $m_f = 40$ is **even**, harmonics at $f_o, 2f_o, 3f_o, \ldots$ may exist. For a 3-phase inverter with shared carrier, the dominant harmonics cluster around $m_f \pm 2$, $m_f \pm 4$, etc.

**(1 mark)** Ō£ō

**(ii) Peak Fundamental Phase Voltage:**

For a **3-phase VSI with half-bridge legs**, the peak fundamental phase voltage is:

$$
V_{ph1,m} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{600}{2} = 0.8 \cdot 300 = 240\,\text{V}
$$

**(2 marks)** Ō£ō

**(iii) Lowest Dominant Harmonic Cluster:**

With $m_f = 40$, the lowest significant harmonic sidebands appear at:

$$
n = m_f \pm 2 = 40 \pm 2 \Rightarrow n = 38,\,42
$$

Frequencies: $38 \times 50 = 1900\,\text{Hz}$ and $42 \times 50 = 2100\,\text{Hz}$.

**(2 marks)** Ō£ō

**(iv) Operating Region:**

Since $m_a = 0.8 < 1.0$, the inverter operates in the **linear modulation region**. Output voltage varies linearly with $m_a$, and no overmodulation distortion occurs. The DC bus utilization is:

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{V_{ph1,m}}{V_s} = \frac{240}{600} = 40\% \text{ (phase); line voltage peak} = 240\sqrt{3} = 415.7\,\text{V}
$$

For **maximum linear** operation, $m_a$ could be increased to **1.0** ŌåÆ $V_{ph1,m} = 300\,\text{V}$, giving 50% DC utilization for phase (Ōēł 78.5% line utilization). $m_a = 0.8$ leaves **headroom for transient overvoltage** without entering overmodulation.

**(2 marks)** Ō£ō

**(7 marks)** Ō£ō

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing phase vs line voltage formulas** ŌĆö phase voltage uses $\sqrt{2/3} V_s$ formula, line uses $\sqrt{2} V_s$. Drawing the wrong waveform shape (square vs quasi-square) costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-parallel diodes** in circuit diagrams (ŌĆō1 mark minimum).
> 3. **Confusing 180┬░ and 120┬░ modes** ŌĆö board questions often say "with 180┬░ mode" but students describe 120┬░ mode.
> 4. **THD calculation errors** ŌĆö using total RMS as fundamental RMS (a very common mistake).
> 5. **Not drawing the gating signals** $S_1, S_3, S_5$ along with the voltage waveforms ŌĆö KTU demands complete timing diagrams.
> 6. **In SPWM, omitting the constraint that $m_f$ should be odd** for single-phase inverters, or a multiple of 3 for 3-phase inverters.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ŌÜĪ 3-Phase VSI ŌĆö Core Concepts
- Six switches arranged in **three legs**, fed from stiff DC source.
- **180┬░ mode** = each switch ON for half the cycle; **120┬░ mode** = one-third (less common).
- At any instant, **3 switches ON**: either (2 upper + 1 lower) or (1 upper + 2 lower).
- **Phase voltage** = 6-step quasi-square, amplitude $\pm V_s/2$.
- **Line voltage** = 3-level stepped, amplitude $\pm 2V_s/3$ with 0 in between.

### ŌÜĪ Key Voltage Formulas (Must Memorize)
- $V_{ph1,m} = \dfrac{2V_s}{\pi}$ (peak fundamental phase, 180┬░ mode)
- $V_{L1,m} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.1026 V_s$
- $V_{L1,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.7797 V_s$
- $V_{L,rms} = \sqrt{2}\,V_s \approx 1.414 V_s$ (total RMS, 180┬░ mode)
- $\text{THD}_{\text{line, 6-step}} \approx 31.08\%$

### ŌÜĪ Harmonic Spectrum Rules
- Harmonics present at $n = 6k \pm 1$ (i.e., 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, ...).
- **Triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15)** are absent in the **line voltage** (cancel in line-to-line).
- 5th harmonic has magnitude $\approx 20\%$ of fundamental.

### ŌÜĪ SPWM ŌĆö Critical Parameters
- $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_m}{\hat{V}_{cr}}$ ŌĆö controls voltage magnitude.
- $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_o}$ ŌĆö controls pulses per cycle.
- **Linear region:** $m_a \le 1$, $V_{o1,m} = m_a \cdot V_s/2$ (half-bridge).
- **Overmodulation:** $m_a > 1$, output approaches 6-step waveform.
- **DC bus utilization (linear):** up to 78.5%; **with 3rd harmonic injection:** up to 90.6%.

### ŌÜĪ KTU Exam-Favorite Comparisons
| Parameter | 6-Step (180┬░) | Linear SPWM |
|:----------|:--------------|:------------|
| THD | ~31% | Lower (energy at high freq) |
| Switching loss | Low | High (depends on $f_c$) |
| Voltage control | Fixed (only $f$) | Both $V$ and $f$ |
| Filter size | Large | Small |

### ŌÜĪ Last-Minute Memory Hooks
1. **"$2/\pi$ rule"** ŌĆö fundamental peak = $\dfrac{2V_{DC}}{\pi}$ for square-wave-derived inverters.
2. **"$\sqrt{6}/\pi$ rule"** ŌĆö fundamental RMS line voltage = $\dfrac{\sqrt{6}\,V_s}{\pi}$.
3. **"31% THD"** ŌĆö the 6-step line voltage has THD $\approx 31\%$.
4. **"Odd + Multiple of 3"** ŌĆö choose $m_f$ as **odd multiple of 3** for clean 3-phase SPWM (e.g., 15, 21, 27, 33).
5. **"78.5% Rule"** ŌĆö max linear SPWM DC utilization = $4/\pi \approx 78.5\%$ (relative to 6-step peak).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sine Triangle PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sine Triangle PWM # ŌÜĪ Sine Triangle PWM (SPWM) ŌĆö The Heartbeat of Modern Inverters ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** is the most fundamental and widely deployed carrier-based PWM technique used to synthesize a quasi-sinusoidal AC output voltage from a DC source by switching a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI). In SPWM, a low-frequency **modulating reference sine wave** ($v_c$, the *control signal*) is continuously compared against a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** ($v_{cr}$, the *switching signal*). The intersection points of these two waveforms generate the gating pulses that command the inverter's semiconductor switches (IGBTs/MOSFETs), thereby producing a train of variable-width rectangular pulses whose **fundamental component faithfully tracks** the reference sine wave in both magnitude and frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must understand the *modulation index*, *frequency ratio*, harmonic spectrum placement, and the derivation of the fundamental RMS output voltage. These are perennial high-weightage areas in ESE (End Semester Examination). ### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Shadow Puppet of Power" Imagine you are standing in front of a bright lamp (the DC bus) holding a cutout shape of a **sinusoidal curve** (the reference signal). Between the lamp and the screen, a hand waves a triangular cardboard (the carrier). As the triangular cardboard oscillates rapidly, the *shadow* it casts on the screen is broken into a stream of *pulse-width modulated* light. By carefully controlling how wide each sliver of shadow is (using the sine cutout), the **average brightness of the shadow appears as a smooth sine wave** to your eye, even though the actual light is chopped. In SPWM: - The **lamp** = DC bus voltage ($V_s$) - The **triangular cardboard** = High-frequency carrier wave ($v_{cr}$) - The **sine cutout** = Low-frequency reference sine wave ($v_c$) - The **average brightness of the shadow** = Fundamental component of inverter output ($V_{o1}$) > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Parameters:** > - Switching frequency ($f_s$): typically **2 kHz to 20 kHz** in industrial drives > - Reference frequency ($f_1$): **0 Hz to 50/60 Hz** (motor rated frequency) > - DC link voltage ($V_s$): **Standard drive ratings** 400 V, 600 V, 800 V DC --- # ­¤ōś Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of SPWM ŌĆö Step-by-Step The mechanism of SPWM can be broken down into a clean, deterministic sequence: 1. **Generation of Reference Signal:** A pure sine wave $v_c = A_s \sin(\omega_s t)$ is generated at the desired fundamental output frequency $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ (s subscript = signal/sinusoidal). 2. **Generation of Carrier Signal:** A high-frequency triangular wave $v_{cr}$ oscillates at carrier frequency $f_c$ with peak amplitude $A_c$. Common triangular waves are *bipolar* (touches both +$A_c$ and -$A_c$) or *unipolar* (touches 0 to $A_c$). 3. **Comparator Action:** A continuous analog/digital comparator evaluates $v_c > v_{cr}$ at every switching instant. - If $v_c > v_{cr}$ ŌåÆ upper switch of inverter leg is **ON**, lower switch is **OFF** ŌåÆ output = $+V_s$. - If $v_c < v_{cr}$ ŌåÆ upper switch is **OFF**, lower switch is **ON** ŌåÆ output = $-V_s$ (or 0 for unipolar). 4. **Pulse Train Formation:** The resulting output voltage is a **bipolar (or unipolar) PWM waveform** whose harmonic energy is pushed away from the low-frequency fundamental and concentrated at the high-frequency carrier multiple. 5. **Filtering (often natural, via load inductance):** The motor's stator inductance naturally integrates these high-frequency pulses, leaving a clean sinusoidal current. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Logic > [!NOTE] > **Why a triangular carrier?** Because a triangle has a **linear rising and falling slope**, which guarantees that the *width of the generated pulse* is **directly proportional to the instantaneous value of the reference sine**. This is the mathematical secret behind SPWM's linearity. > [!NOTE] > **Why compare and not multiply?** Comparator circuits are *orders of magnitude faster* than analog multipliers, and they produce clean digital logic outputs that can directly drive the opto-isolated gate drivers of IGBT modules. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Range/Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | Amplitude Modulation Ratio (Modulation Index) | $m_a$ | $m_a = \dfrac{A_s}{A_c}$ | Linear region: $0 \le m_a \le 1.0$
Overmodulation: $m_a > 1.0$ | | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f$ | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_s}$ | Integer (synchronous); typically $\ge 15$ for low harmonics | | Fundamental RMS Output Voltage (Linear, $m_a \le 1$) | $V_{o1,rms}$ | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot V_s}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Bipolar SPWM | | Fundamental Peak Output Voltage | $V_{o1,peak}$ | $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Maximum at $m_a = 1.0$: $V_s/2$ | | Maximum Fundamental RMS (Linear SPWM) | $V_{o1,\max}$ | $V_{o1,\max} = \dfrac{V_s}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.3536\,V_s$ | Only 70.7% of square-wave fundamental | | DC Bus Utilization | ŌĆö | $\dfrac{V_{o1,rms}}{V_s} = \dfrac{m_a}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Demonstrates poor DC bus usage | | Effective Output Voltage (per pulse $k$) | $V_{or,k}$ | $V_{or,k} = V_s \sqrt{d_k}$ | $d_k$ = duty ratio of $k^{th}$ pulse | | Total RMS Output Voltage | $V_{or}$ | $V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k}$ | Sum over N pulses per half cycle | | THD (Total Harmonic Distortion) | $THD$ | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{or}^2 - V_{o1}^2}}{V_{o1}}$ | Used for evaluating inverter quality | | Overmodulation Region Peak Output | $V_{o1,peak}$ | $V_{o1,peak} = \dfrac{4 \cdot V_s}{\pi} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{m_a - 1}{\pi}\cdot \text{correction}\right)$ | Approaches square wave at $m_a \to \infty$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Formula:** The peak of fundamental component for *linear SPWM* is always $V_{o1} = m_a V_s / 2$. Memorize this cold ŌĆö it appears in **nearly every ESE question** on this topic. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility SPWM is the **workhorse modulation strategy** used in: - ­¤ö¦ **Industrial VFDs** (Variable Frequency Drives) ŌĆö Allen-Bradley PowerFlex, Siemens SINAMICS, ABB ACS series - ŌÜĪ **Grid-tied solar inverters** ŌĆö String inverters in PV plants - ­¤ÜŚ **EV traction inverters** ŌĆö Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV powertrains - ­¤ÅĀ **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** ŌĆö Double-conversion online UPS - Ō£ł’ĖÅ **Aircraft power systems** ŌĆö 400 Hz variable-speed constant-frequency (VSCF) systems --- # ­¤¦« Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö RMS Value of the PWM Output Voltage Consider a half-cycle of the SPWM output containing $N$ pulses of varying widths. Each pulse $k$ has a **duty ratio** $d_k$ (ratio of pulse width to pulse period). **Step 1:** Define the instantaneous output voltage as a piecewise function. For the $k^{th}$ pulse within a switching period $T_{sw}$: $$ v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_s, & 0 \le t \le d_k T_{sw} \\ -V_s, & d_k T_{sw} \le t \le T_{sw} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2:** Express the RMS value using the standard definition for a periodic signal of period $T_1$ (fundamental period): $$ V_{or} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_1} \int_{0}^{T_1} v_o^2(t)\, dt} $$ **Step 3:** Since each pulse has amplitude $\pm V_s$, we have $v_o^2(t) = V_s^2$ whenever the switch is conducting. Thus, the squared integral reduces to summing the conducting durations across all pulses within $T_1$: $$ V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{\text{pulse }k} dt = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \delta_k $$ where $\delta_k$ is the *pulse width* of the $k^{th}$ pulse. **Step 4:** Introduce the duty ratio $d_k = \delta_k / T_{sw}$, and note that $T_{sw} = T_1 / N$. Substitute: $$ V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1} \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k \cdot T_{sw} = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{T_1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k $$ **Step 5:** Take the square root to obtain the final compact form: $$ \boxed{\,V_{or} = V_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k} = V_s \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{d_k}{N}}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The RMS output voltage is essentially $V_s$ scaled by the **root-mean-square of the duty ratios**. Because each $d_k$ varies sinusoidally with $k$ (wider near the peaks, narrower near the zero crossings), the sum converges elegantly. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Fundamental Component Amplitude (Peak) For SPWM in the **linear modulation region** ($m_a \le 1.0$), the fundamental output voltage has a remarkable *linear* relationship with the modulation index. The derivation uses Fourier analysis of the pulse train. **Step 1:** Write the output as a bipolar pulse train of amplitude $\pm V_s$. Its Fourier series is: $$ v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[\sin(n \alpha_{k2}) - \sin(n \alpha_{k1})\right] \sin(n \omega_s t + \phi_n) $$ where $\alpha_{k1}$ and $\alpha_{k2}$ are the **trailing and leading edge angles** of the $k^{th}$ pulse. **Step 2:** By the principle of **double-edge natural sampling**, when the sine reference intersects a linear triangle, the leading edge $\alpha_{k1}$ and trailing edge $\alpha_{k2}$ are symmetrically placed about the carrier peak, with **pulse center** located at the *phase angle* of the modulating sine, $\theta_k = \pi (2k - 1) / N$. **Step 3:** Define the *half-pulse width* (in radians) as $\delta_k$, which is governed by the intersection geometry: $$ \delta_k = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) $$ **Step 4:** For the **fundamental component** ($n = 1$), the inner sine difference simplifies using small-angle approximations because the pulse widths are narrow compared to the period. The expression becomes: $$ V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sin(\delta_k) \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) $$ **Step 5:** For **small $\delta_k$** (high $m_f$ regime, which is the practical operating range), $\sin(\delta_k) \approx \delta_k$. Substituting: $$ V_{o1,\text{peak}} \approx \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \delta_k \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) $$ **Step 6:** Plug in the expression for $\delta_k$: $$ V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot m_a \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) $$ $$ V_{o1,\text{peak}} = 2 V_s \cdot m_a \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) $$ **Step 7:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(x) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2}$ and use the orthogonality of sinusoids over a full cycle, which yields $\sum \sin^2(\cdot) = N/2$: $$ \sum_{k=1}^{N} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi(2k-1)}{N}\right) = \frac{N}{2} $$ **Step 8:** Substitute back: $$ V_{o1,\text{peak}} = 2 V_s \cdot m_a \cdot \frac{N}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{N} = m_a V_s \cdot \frac{1}{1} $$ > [!NOTE] > Wait ŌĆö re-evaluating carefully with the **proper bipolar SPWM averaging** over the carrier period: $$ \boxed{\,V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_s}{2}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Clean Result for KTU Exams:** For **bipolar linear SPWM**, the peak of the fundamental output voltage is exactly $V_{o1,peak} = m_a V_s / 2$. The corresponding RMS value is $V_{o1,rms} = m_a V_s / (2\sqrt{2})$. When $m_a = 1.0$, the maximum achievable fundamental RMS is $V_s / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.3536 \, V_s$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö THD of Linear SPWM (Square-Wave Reference Benchmark) **Step 1:** The RMS output of an SPWM inverter (with high $m_f$) approaches: $$ V_{or} \approx V_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} + \frac{m_a}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin(m_a)\right] + \frac{m_a}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - m_a^2}}, \quad m_a \le 1 $$ **Step 2:** The fundamental RMS is: $$ V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a V_s}{2\sqrt{2}} $$ **Step 3:** Plug into the THD definition: $$ THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{or}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}} \times 100\% $$ > [!NOTE] > For $m_a = 1.0$, the THD of a **bipolar SPWM** output is approximately **74.3%**, while **unipolar SPWM** achieves **44.7%** ŌĆö illustrating the superiority of unipolar PWM in reducing output harmonics. --- # ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö SPWM Signal Generation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR REF[Reference Sine Generator
v_control = A_s sin wt]:::signalGen CR[Triangular Carrier Generator
v_tri at frequency f_c]:::signalGen CMP[Analog Comparator
v_control vs v_tri]:::coreBlock LOGIC[Logic Level Shifter
and Dead-Time Insertion]:::coreBlock GATE[Isolated Gate Driver
Opto or Fiber Coupled]:::coreBlock VSI[Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter
IGBT Bridge Legs]:::powerBlock LOAD[AC Motor Load
Stator Inductance Filters Harmonics]:::load REF -->|low frequency sine| CMP CR -->|high frequency triangle| CMP CMP -->|set reset pulses| LOGIC LOGIC -->|complementary gating signals| GATE GATE -->|boosted gate pulses| VSI VSI -->|PWM voltage waveform| LOAD LOAD -->|filtered sinusoidal current| OUT[Clean AC Output]:::load classDef signalGen fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef coreBlock fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100 classDef powerBlock fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px,color:#B71C1C classDef load fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Operational Flow ŌĆö Comparator Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Start: Each Switching Cycle T_sw]) --> READSINE[Read instantaneous v_control] READSINE --> READTRI[Read instantaneous v_tri] READTRI --> DECIDE{v_control greater than v_tri?} DECIDE -->|YES| UPPERON[Turn ON Upper Switch S_plus
Turn OFF Lower Switch S_minus
Output equals plus V_s] DECIDE -->|NO| UPPEROFF[Turn OFF Upper Switch S_plus
Turn ON Lower Switch S_minus
Output equals minus V_s] UPPERON --> WAIT[Wait for next comparator tick] UPPEROFF --> WAIT WAIT --> NEXT{End of half cycle?} NEXT -->|NO| READSINE NEXT -->|YES| REVERSE[Reverse output polarity
Generate negative half cycle] REVERSE --> START style START fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C style DECIDE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#F57C00 style UPPERON fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828 style UPPEROFF fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2 style REVERSE fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#7B1FA2 ``` ## 4.3 SPWM Waveform Comparison ŌĆö Reference vs. Carrier vs. Output ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SINE[REFERENCE SIGNAL] S1[Pure Sine Wave v_control
Frequency f_1 = 50 Hz
Amplitude A_s] end subgraph TRI[CARRIER SIGNAL] S2[High Frequency Triangle v_tri
Frequency f_c = 2000 Hz
Amplitude A_c
m_f equals f_c divided by f_1 equals 40] end subgraph CMP[COMPARATOR OUTPUT LOGIC] S3[IF v_control over v_tri THEN high
ELSE low] end subgraph OUT[SPWM OUTPUT v_o] S4[Bipolar Pulse Train
plus V_s when sine above triangle
minus V_s when sine below triangle
Pulse widths vary sinusoidally] end subgraph SPECT[HARMONIC SPECTRUM] S5[Fundamental at f_1 with amplitude m_a times V_s over 2
Dominant harmonics at multiples of f_c
No low order harmonics in linear region] end S1 --> S3 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 style S1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style S2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style S3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style S4 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C style S5 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20 ``` ## 4.4 Modulation Index Operational Map ```mermaid flowchart LR MA0[m_a equals 0] -->|zero reference| ZERO[Output is zero
All switches alternate symmetrically
Net fundamental equals 0] MA01[m_a between 0 and 1] -->|linear region| LIN[Output fundamental scales linearly
V_o1 equals m_a times V_s over 2
Harmonics at carrier multiples] MA1[m_a equals 1] -->|maximum linear| MAX[Maximum linear output
V_o1 peak equals V_s over 2
V_o1 rms equals V_s over 2 sqrt 2] MAOVER[m_a greater than 1] -->|overmodulation| OVER[Nonlinear pulse dropping
Output saturates toward square wave
Maximum V_o1 peak equals 4 V_s over pi] MASQ[m_a approaches infinity] -->|extreme| SQW[Pure Square Wave
Full DC bus utilization
Highest harmonics including triplen] style MA0 fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F style MA01 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style MA1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style MAOVER fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style MASQ fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C ``` --- # ­¤Ä» KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM). Mention its key parameters.** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer:** **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** is a carrier-based PWM technique in which a low-frequency sinusoidal reference wave ($v_c$) is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier wave ($v_{cr}$) to generate the gating pulses for a voltage source inverter. The instantaneous intersection of these two waveforms determines the switching instants. The **two key parameters** governing SPWM are: 1. **Amplitude Modulation Ratio (Modulation Index):** $m_a = \dfrac{A_s}{A_c}$, where $A_s$ = peak amplitude of reference sine, $A_c$ = peak amplitude of carrier triangle. 2. **Frequency Modulation Ratio:** $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_s}$, where $f_c$ = carrier frequency, $f_s$ = reference sine frequency. For linear operation, $m_a$ is maintained in the range $0 \le m_a \le 1.0$. Beyond this, **overmodulation** occurs, distorting the output. --- ### **Question 2: What is the significance of choosing a triangular carrier over other waveforms in SPWM?** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Answer:** The triangular wave is chosen as the carrier in SPWM because: 1. **Linearity of Slope:** The rising and falling edges of a triangle are *linear with constant slope*. This ensures that the *width of the generated gating pulse* is *directly proportional* to the instantaneous value of the modulating sine wave, yielding a clean, linear transfer characteristic. 2. **Symmetry:** The triangular waveform is symmetric about its peak, which simplifies the **double-edge natural sampling** mechanism, giving equal leading and trailing edge intervals around the pulse center. 3. **Harmonic Predictability:** Because the carrier is a *deterministic linear function*, the harmonic spectrum of the resulting PWM is mathematically tractable ŌĆö harmonics appear at predictable multiples of $f_c$, making filtering straightforward. 4. **Ease of Generation:** Triangular waves can be generated with extreme precision using simple op-amp relaxation oscillators or digital counters (DDS ŌĆö Direct Digital Synthesis). --- ## ­¤ōÉ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **­¤ģ░’ĖÅ Question A (14 Marks)** ### **Question: With a neat block diagram and necessary waveforms, explain the generation of SPWM signals for a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for the RMS value of the output voltage.** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Solution:** #### **(a) Block Diagram and Working Principle (7 Marks)** A single-phase full-bridge VSI has four switches: $S_1, S_2$ (one leg) and $S_3, S_4$ (other leg). The gating signals are generated as follows: - A **reference sine wave** $v_c = A_s \sin(\omega t)$ is generated at the desired fundamental frequency (e.g., 50 Hz). - A **triangular carrier wave** $v_{cr}$ at carrier frequency $f_c$ (e.g., 2 kHz) with peak amplitude $A_c$ is generated. - A **comparator** continuously compares $v_c$ with $v_{cr}$. **Switching Logic:** | Condition | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | $v_o$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $v_c > v_{cr}$ | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $+V_s$ | | $v_c < v_{cr}$ | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $-V_s$ | > **Note:** $S_1$ and $S_2$ are complementary; $S_3$ and $S_4$ are complementary. $S_3$ is driven by the **inverted** reference ($180┬░$ phase-shifted) to produce bipolar output. #### **(b) RMS Output Voltage Derivation (7 Marks)** Consider $N$ pulses per half-cycle of the fundamental. Each pulse has a duty ratio $d_k$. The instantaneous output is: $$ v_o(t) = \pm V_s $$ The RMS value over a fundamental period $T_1$ is: $$ V_{or} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_1}\int_{0}^{T_1} v_o^2(t)\, dt} $$ Since $v_o^2 = V_s^2$ during conduction: $$ V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\delta_k $$ Substituting $\delta_k = d_k T_{sw} = d_k T_1/N$: $$ V_{or}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{T_1}\cdot \frac{T_1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k = \frac{V_s^2}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{or} = V_s\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_k}\,} $$ For linear SPWM, the fundamental RMS simplifies to: $$ \boxed{\,V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a V_s}{2\sqrt{2}}\,} $$ **Verification at $m_a = 1$:** Maximum linear output RMS = $\dfrac{V_s}{2\sqrt{2}} = 0.3536\, V_s$, which is only $70.7\%$ of the square-wave fundamental ($4V_s/\pi\sqrt{2} = 0.9 V_s$). This **DC bus under-utilization** is a known drawback of pure SPWM and motivates advanced techniques like **SVPWM**. --- ### **­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ Question B (14 Marks)** ### **Question: A single-phase full-bridge inverter employs SPWM with $V_s = 400$ V DC, modulation index $m_a = 0.8$, and frequency ratio $m_f = 21$.** **(a) Calculate the RMS value of the fundamental output voltage and the peak of the fundamental.** **(b) If the modulation index is increased to $m_a = 1.2$ (overmodulation), explain the effect on output waveform and harmonic content. Compare with linear operation.** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** #### **(a) Linear Region Calculations (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $V_s = 400$ V - $m_a = 0.8$ - $m_f = 21$ **Peak Fundamental Output Voltage:** $$ V_{o1,peak} = \frac{m_a V_s}{2} = \frac{0.8 \times 400}{2} = 160 \text{ V} $$ **RMS Fundamental Output Voltage:** $$ V_{o1,rms} = \frac{V_{o1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{160}{\sqrt{2}} = 113.14 \text{ V} $$ **Alternative direct formula:** $$ V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a V_s}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.8 \times 400}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{320}{2.828} = 113.14 \text{ V} \checkmark $$ #### **(b) Overmodulation Analysis (7 Marks)** When $m_a$ exceeds **1.0**, the peak of the reference sine exceeds the peak of the triangular carrier. Within the central region of the cycle, **the reference saturates the comparator**, meaning: - For a finite interval near the sine peak, $v_c > v_{cr}$ continuously ŌåÆ output **saturates at $+V_s$**. - The pulse is **dropped** (a pulse period is lost) ŌĆö the comparator never toggles within that saturated interval. - **Result:** The PWM waveform begins to resemble a **square wave** with stepped transitions. **Effect on Output Amplitude:** The fundamental amplitude no longer follows $m_a V_s/2$ linearly. Instead: $$ V_{o1,peak} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{m_a}\right)\left(\frac{1 - \cos\alpha}{\alpha}\right)\right], \quad \alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_a}\right) $$ For $m_a = 1.2$: $\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.833) = 33.56┬░ = 0.5857$ rad. $$ V_{o1,peak} = \frac{4 \times 400}{\pi}\left[1 - \frac{1}{1.2}\cdot \frac{1 - \cos(33.56┬░)}{0.5857}\right] $$ $$ = 509.3\left[1 - 0.833 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.833}{0.5857}\right] = 509.3\left[1 - 0.833 \cdot 0.285\right] $$ $$ = 509.3 \times [1 - 0.2375] = 509.3 \times 0.7625 \approx 388.3 \text{ V} $$ This is **closer to the square-wave peak** ($4V_s/\pi = 509.3$ V) than the linear maximum ($V_s/2 = 200$ V). **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Linear ($m_a = 0.8$) | Overmodulation ($m_a = 1.2$) | |---|---|---| | Peak Fundamental | 160 V | ~388 V | | Pulse Pattern | Uniform variation | Pulse dropping near peaks | | THD | Moderate | High (dominant low-order harmonics) | | DC Bus Utilization | Poor (~28%) | Better (~50%) | | Waveform Shape | Quasi-sinusoidal | Approaching square wave | > [!WARNING] > **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assume that **the linear formula $V_{o1,peak} = m_a V_s / 2$ holds for all $m_a$ values**. It does **NOT** apply for $m_a > 1.0$. In overmodulation, the relationship is **nonlinear**, and you MUST mention "pulse dropping" and "waveform saturation" explicitly to fetch full marks. --- ## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - Ō£ģ **SPWM = Sine vs. Triangle comparison** ŌåÆ simplest and most fundamental PWM strategy. - Ō£ģ **Two governing ratios:** $m_a = A_s/A_c$ (amplitude) and $m_f = f_c/f_s$ (frequency). - Ō£ģ **Linear region:** $m_a \in [0, 1.0]$ ŌåÆ fundamental amplitude scales linearly: $V_{o1,peak} = m_a V_s / 2$. - Ō£ģ **Overmodulation:** $m_a > 1.0$ ŌåÆ pulse dropping occurs; output approaches square wave; introduces low-order harmonics. - Ō£ģ **Maximum linear fundamental RMS:** $V_s / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.3536\, V_s$ ŌĆö only 70.7% of square-wave output. - Ō£ģ **DC bus under-utilization** is a known limitation ŌĆö solved by **SVPWM** (which reaches $V_s/\sqrt{3}$). - Ō£ģ **Carrier frequency choice:** Higher $f_c$ ŌåÆ better filtering, lower harmonics, but greater switching losses. Trade-off at ~2ŌĆō20 kHz. - Ō£ģ **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous:** $m_f$ should be an **odd integer** in three-phase systems to eliminate even harmonics and DC components. - Ō£ģ **Unipolar SPWM** has lower THD (~44.7%) than **bipolar SPWM** (~74.3%) at $m_a = 1$. - Ō£ģ **Harmonics appear as sidebands** centered at $m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f,...$ multiples of carrier frequency, *not* at low frequencies. - Ō£ģ **Application domains:** Industrial VFDs, solar inverters, UPS, EV traction, VSCF aircraft systems. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **­¤Äō Final KTU Pro-Tip:** When answering theory questions, **always draw the three waveforms** (sine, triangle, PWM output) and **label the pulse widths**. Examiners award *easy marks* for clear waveform sketches even if your derivations have minor arithmetic slips. In numerical problems, **state the formula before substituting** ŌĆö partial credit is granted even if the final number is wrong.

topics.title: Space Vector PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVE AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Space Vector PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Space Vector Pulse Width Modulation (SVPWM)** is an advanced, digital pulse-width modulation technique used to control the switching of a three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI). Instead of treating each phase independently, SVPWM represents the three-phase output voltages as a single rotating **reference space vector** $\vec{V}_{ref}$ in the stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ reference frame, and synthesizes this vector by time-averaging the discrete switching states of the inverter.

In a two-level three-phase VSI, there are $2^3 = 8$ permissible switching states: **6 active states** ($\vec{V}_1$ to $\vec{V}_6$) that generate non-zero output vectors, and **2 zero states** ($\vec{V}_0$ and $\vec{V}_7$) that produce zero voltage at the load. SVPWM uses these 8 vectors as a toolkit to approximate any desired $\vec{V}_{ref}$ inside the hexagonal boundary of the inverter's linear modulation range.

The defining attribute that distinguishes SVPWM from Sinusoidal PWM is its **DC bus utilization**:

- Sinusoidal PWM: Maximum fundamental phase voltage = $\frac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,V_{dc}$ (line-to-line: $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$)
- **Space Vector PWM**: Maximum fundamental phase voltage = $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,V_{dc}$ (line-to-line: $V_{dc}$)

This represents a **15.47% increase** in DC bus utilization, making SVPWM the de-facto standard for high-performance AC drives, grid-tied inverters, and industrial motor control.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Compass and Magnet Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are standing in the center of a hexagonal courtyard, and you need to point a powerful flashlight (your reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$) in any direction at any instant. The flashlight must always point in a specific direction with a specific brightness, and the brightness must be controlled smoothly.
>
> Around the edges of the courtyard stand **6 fixed spotlights** at the 6 vertices of the hexagon (these are the **6 active switching vectors**, $\vec{V}_1$ to $\vec{V}_6$). Each fixed spotlight can be turned ON or OFF, and has a fixed direction and fixed brightness.
>
> Your task: Make the combined beam (using 2 or 3 fixed spotlights plus a "dark" zero state) look exactly like your desired flashlight direction, but only by **time-averaging** ŌĆö turning lights on and off very fast.
>
> The brighter you want the beam in a particular direction, the longer you keep the two nearest fixed spotlights ON in that sector. When the beam should be weaker, you keep them ON for shorter durations and fill the remaining time with the **zero vectors** ($\vec{V}_0$ or $\vec{V}_7$ ŌĆö all lights OFF or all lights ON, which produces zero average).
>
> This time-averaged approximation, performed at a high switching frequency (e.g., 5 kHz to 20 kHz), creates the illusion of a continuously rotating vector of any magnitude up to the inscribed circle of the hexagon. The "inscribed circle" radius represents the maximum linear modulation range ŌĆö this is exactly why SVPWM achieves **higher DC utilization** than SPWM (which is bounded by the smaller apothem of the hexagon).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **DC Bus Voltage ($V_{dc}$)**: The fixed input DC link voltage (in Volts, **V**).
- **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$)**: Typically **2 kHz to 20 kHz** in industrial drives (limited by IGBT switching losses).
- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_1$)**: Output frequency of the inverter, typically **0 Hz to 50/60 Hz** (or up to several hundred Hz for high-speed drives).
- **Modulation Index ($m$)**: Ratio of reference vector magnitude to maximum available magnitude. Linear range: $0 \le m \le \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$.
- **Sampling Period ($T_s$)**: $T_s = \frac{1}{f_{sw}}$ (in seconds, **s**).
- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)**: Equals the switching frequency $f_{sw}$ (in **Hz**).
- **Per-unit base voltage**: $V_{base} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}$ (used for per-unit normalization in vector analysis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The course outcome mapping for this topic is:
> - **CO3**: Apply advanced modulation strategies for three-phase inverters used in AC drives.
> - **Bloom's Level**: Apply (L3) ŌåÆ Analyze (L4).
> - **Key takeaway**: Students must be able to (a) draw the hexagonal space vector diagram, (b) compute dwell times $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_0$ for any sector, and (c) explain why SVPWM gives 15.47% higher DC utilization than SPWM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Phase VSI Switching States

A two-level three-phase VSI has three legs (A, B, C). Each leg has two switches: one upper (1) and one lower (0). The switching state of the inverter is represented by a 3-bit code $(S_a, S_b, S_c)$.

| State Code $(S_a S_b S_c)$ | Vector Name | Type | Phase Voltages $(V_{an}, V_{bn}, V_{cn})$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 000 | $\vec{V}_0$ | Zero Vector | $(0, 0, 0)$ |
| 001 | $\vec{V}_5$ | Active Vector | $(-V_{dc}/3, -V_{dc}/3, 2V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 010 | $\vec{V}_3$ | Active Vector | $(-V_{dc}/3, 2V_{dc}/3, -V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 011 | $\vec{V}_4$ | Active Vector | $(-2V_{dc}/3, V_{dc}/3, V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 100 | $\vec{V}_1$ | Active Vector | $(2V_{dc}/3, -V_{dc}/3, -V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 101 | $\vec{V}_6$ | Active Vector | $(V_{dc}/3, -2V_{dc}/3, V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 110 | $\vec{V}_2$ | Active Vector | $(V_{dc}/3, V_{dc}/3, -2V_{dc}/3)$ |
| 111 | $\vec{V}_7$ | Zero Vector | $(0, 0, 0)$ |

## 2.2 The $\alpha$-$\beta$ Transformation (Clarke Transform)

The three-phase quantities are converted to a two-axis stationary frame using:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_\alpha &= \frac{2}{3}\left(V_{an} - \frac{1}{2}V_{bn} - \frac{1}{2}V_{cn}\right) \\
V_\beta &= \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}V_{bn} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}V_{cn}\right) \\
V_0 &= \frac{1}{3}(V_{an} + V_{bn} + V_{cn})
\end{aligned}$$

Each active state maps to a vector of magnitude $\frac{2V_{dc}}{3}$ in the $\alpha$-$\beta$ plane.

## 2.3 Six Active Vectors and the Hexagon

The 6 active vectors are positioned at the vertices of a regular hexagon, separated by **60┬░**, with magnitude:

$$|\vec{V}_k| = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}, \quad k = 1, 2, ..., 6$$

The 6 vectors divide the $\alpha$-$\beta$ plane into **6 sectors** (Sector I to Sector VI), each spanning 60┬░.

## 2.4 Reference Vector Synthesis (Ampere-Second / Volt-Second Balance)

The reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ in any sector is synthesized by time-averaging the two adjacent active vectors and one or both zero vectors within one sampling period $T_s$:

$$T_s \cdot \vec{V}_{ref} = T_1 \cdot \vec{V}_k + T_2 \cdot \vec{V}_{k+1} + T_0 \cdot \vec{V}_{zero}$$

where $T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = T_s$.

This is the **volt-second balance equation**, and it is the cornerstone of SVPWM.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Active vector magnitude | $\vert \vec{V}_k \vert = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$ | All 6 active states |
| 2 | Hexagon inscribed-circle radius | $V_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,V_{dc}$ | Maximum linear modulation (line-to-neutral) |
| 3 | Maximum line-to-line voltage | $V_{LL,max} = V_{dc}$ | At $m = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| 4 | Modulation Index (SVPWM) | $m = \frac{\sqrt{3} \vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert}{V_{dc}}$ | Range: $0 \le m \le \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
| 5 | Dwell time $T_1$ (active vector 1) | $T_1 = \frac{\sqrt{3} \vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert T_s}{V_{dc}} \sin(60┬░ - \theta)$ | $\theta$ = angle within sector |
| 6 | Dwell time $T_2$ (active vector 2) | $T_2 = \frac{\sqrt{3} \vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert T_s}{V_{dc}} \sin(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = angle within sector |
| 7 | Zero vector time $T_0$ | $T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$ | Always non-negative |
| 8 | Sampling period | $T_s = \frac{1}{f_{sw}}$ | In seconds |
| 9 | SPWM DC utilization | $0.577\,V_{dc}$ (phase) / $0.816\,V_{dc}$ (line) | Square-wave comparison |
| 10 | SVPWM DC utilization gain | $\frac{2/\sqrt{3}}{1} = 1.1547$ (i.e., +15.47%) | Over SPWM |
| 11 | Sector determination | $\theta_{ref} \in [0┬░, 60┬░) \Rightarrow$ Sector I, etc. | Map to nearest active pair |
| 12 | Overmodulation threshold | $\vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert > \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Non-linear region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipes**: In the table above, $\vert \vec{V}_k \vert$ uses `\vert` (vertical bar delimiter) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do **not** write `|V_k|` directly inside a table cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial AC Drives**: ABB, Siemens, Schneider, Yaskawa, and Mitsubishi drives all use SVPWM variants for induction motor (IM) and permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) control.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Tesla Model 3/Y drivetrain inverters, Tata Nexon EV ŌĆö all rely on SVPWM for high-torque-per-ampere operation.
- **Grid-Tied Solar Inverters**: SMA, Sungrow, and Huawei use SVPWM to maximize energy harvest by pushing DC utilization to the limit.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)**: For clean sine-wave output with minimum filter size.
- **Avionics and Naval Drives**: Where DC-link voltage is limited and every volt of utilization matters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 15.47% boost matters**: In a 400 V EV inverter, SPWM gives a peak line-to-line fundamental of ~326 V, while SVPWM delivers 400 V ŌĆö translating directly to higher motor torque and speed for the same DC bus.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Active Vector Magnitude

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Phase Voltages of State 100

For the switching state $(S_a, S_b, S_c) = (1, 0, 0)$, the upper switch of leg A is ON (connected to $+V_{dc}$), and the lower switches of legs B and C are ON (connected to ground). Assuming a balanced, wye-connected load with neutral point $N$:

$$V_{an} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}, \quad V_{bn} = -\frac{V_{dc}}{3}, \quad V_{cn} = -\frac{V_{dc}}{3}$$

This is derived from the constraint that the sum of phase voltages must equal zero (no neutral connection to source) and that two phases share the negative rail.

### 3.1.2 Applying the Clarke Transform

Substituting into the $\alpha$-$\beta$ equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_\alpha &= \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{2V_{dc}}{3} - \frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{3}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{3}\right)\right) \\
&= \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{2V_{dc}}{3} + \frac{V_{dc}}{6} + \frac{V_{dc}}{6}\right) \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4V_{dc} + V_{dc} + V_{dc}}{6} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{6V_{dc}}{6} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} V_{dc}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_\beta &= \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{3}\right) - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{3}\right)\right) \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 Vector Magnitude

$$\begin{aligned}
|\vec{V}_1| &= \sqrt{V_\alpha^2 + V_\beta^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{2V_{dc}}{3}\right)^2 + 0^2} \\
&= \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

By symmetry, the same magnitude applies to all 6 active vectors. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of Dwell Times $T_1$ and $T_2$ (Sector I)

### 3.2.1 Setting Up the Reference Vector

Let $\vec{V}_{ref}$ lie in **Sector I**, making an angle $\theta$ with the $\alpha$-axis, where $0 \le \theta \le 60┬░$. The two bounding active vectors are:

- $\vec{V}_1$ along the $\alpha$-axis: $\vec{V}_1 = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \angle 0┬░$
- $\vec{V}_2$ at 60┬░: $\vec{V}_2 = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \angle 60┬░$

### 3.2.2 Applying Volt-Second Balance

The volt-second balance equation is:

$$T_s \cdot \vec{V}_{ref} = T_1 \cdot \vec{V}_1 + T_2 \cdot \vec{V}_2$$

Writing the reference vector in Cartesian form:

$$\vec{V}_{ref} = |\vec{V}_{ref}|\cos\theta + j\,|\vec{V}_{ref}|\sin\theta$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s \left(|\vec{V}_{ref}|\cos\theta + j|\vec{V}_{ref}|\sin\theta\right) &= T_1 \cdot \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}(1 + j\cdot 0) \\
&\quad + T_2 \cdot \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}\left(\cos 60┬░ + j\sin 60┬░\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.3 Separating Real and Imaginary Parts

**Real part:**

$$T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cos\theta = T_1 \cdot \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} + T_2 \cdot \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

$$T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cos\theta = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}\left(T_1 + \frac{T_2}{2}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Imaginary part:**

$$T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \sin\theta = T_2 \cdot \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \sin\theta = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} T_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### 3.2.4 Solving for $T_2$ from Equation (ii)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= \frac{\sqrt{3} |\vec{V}_{ref}| T_s \sin\theta}{V_{dc}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{3} |\vec{V}_{ref}|}{V_{dc}} T_s \sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.5 Substituting $T_2$ into Equation (i) to Find $T_1$

From (i):

$$T_1 + \frac{T_2}{2} = \frac{3 T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cos\theta}{2V_{dc}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{3 T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cos\theta}{2V_{dc}} - \frac{T_2}{2} \\
&= \frac{3 T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cos\theta}{2V_{dc}} - \frac{\sqrt{3} |\vec{V}_{ref}| T_s \sin\theta}{2 V_{dc}}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the identity $\sin(60┬░ - \theta) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\theta - \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta$, multiply through by $\frac{2V_{dc}}{T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}|}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}|}{V_{dc}}\left(\frac{3\cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}\sin\theta}{2}\right) \\
&= \frac{T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}|}{V_{dc}} \cdot \sqrt{3} \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\sin\theta}{2}\right) \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{3} T_s |\vec{V}_{ref}|}{V_{dc}} \sin(60┬░ - \theta)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.6 Final Dwell-Time Equations (Generalized for Any Sector)

$$\boxed{T_1 = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cdot T_s}{V_{dc}} \cdot \sin(60┬░ - \theta)}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot |\vec{V}_{ref}| \cdot T_s}{V_{dc}} \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

$$\boxed{T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of $\vec{V}_{ref}$ measured from the start of the current sector (i.e., $0 \le \theta \le 60┬░$). $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem**: A three-phase VSI is fed from $V_{dc} = 600\,V$ DC. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 2\,kHz$. The reference space vector is $\vec{V}_{ref} = 250 \angle 35┬░$ V. The vector lies in Sector I. Compute $T_1$, $T_2$, and $T_0$.

### Step 1: Sampling Period

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{2000} = 500\,\mu s = 0.5\,ms$$

### Step 2: Angle Within Sector

$\vec{V}_{ref}$ is at 35┬░ from $\alpha$-axis, in Sector I (which spans 0┬░ to 60┬░). Therefore:

$$\theta = 35┬░$$

### Step 3: Compute $T_1$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot 250 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6}}{600} \cdot \sin(60┬░ - 35┬░) \\
&= \frac{1.732 \cdot 250 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}}{600} \cdot \sin(25┬░) \\
&= \frac{0.2165}{600} \cdot 0.4226 \\
&= 3.608 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.4226 \\
&= 152.5\,\mu s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute $T_2$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot 250 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-6}}{600} \cdot \sin(35┬░) \\
&= \frac{0.2165}{600} \cdot 0.5736 \\
&= 3.608 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.5736 \\
&= 206.9\,\mu s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute $T_0$

$$T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2 = 500 - 152.5 - 206.9 = 140.6\,\mu s$$

### Step 6: Verify

$T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = 152.5 + 206.9 + 140.6 = 500\,\mu s = T_s$ Ō£ō

### Step 7: Modulation Index Check

$$m = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot |\vec{V}_{ref}|}{V_{dc}} = \frac{1.732 \cdot 250}{600} = 0.7217$$

Since $m < \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$, the reference is in the **linear modulation region**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation of DC Utilization Comparison

### 3.4.1 SPWM Maximum Phase Voltage

For a pure sine PWM with third-harmonic injection disabled, the maximum peak fundamental phase voltage is bounded by the smaller apothem of the hexagonal space vector diagram. The apothem of the hexagon is:

$$a = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \cdot \cos(30┬░) = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

So for SPWM, $V_{phase,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.2887\,V_{dc}$ (this accounts for the inverter's own gain of 0.5, applied to the apothem). The corresponding line-to-line voltage is $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,V_{dc}$.

### 3.4.2 SVPWM Maximum Phase Voltage

SVPWM uses the **inscribed circle** of the hexagon as the limit (it is the largest circle that fits inside the hexagon without distortion). The inscribed circle radius equals the apothem:

$$R_{inscribed} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

So $V_{phase,max}^{SVPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,V_{dc}$ (line-to-neutral, peak), and $V_{LL,max} = V_{dc}$.

### 3.4.3 Percentage Gain

$$\text{Gain} = \frac{V_{LL,max}^{SVPWM}}{V_{LL,max}^{SPWM}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3} = 1.1547$$

$$\text{Percentage gain} = (\sqrt{3} - 1) \times 100\% = 15.47\%$$ $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Switching Sequence (Symmetric SVPWM for Sector I)

A symmetric, center-aligned switching pattern minimizes harmonic distortion and equalizes switching losses. For Sector I:

| Sub-interval | Switching State | Time |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\vec{V}_0$ (000) | $T_0/4$ |
| 2 | $\vec{V}_1$ (100) | $T_1/2$ |
| 3 | $\vec{V}_2$ (110) | $T_2/2$ |
| 4 | $\vec{V}_7$ (111) | $T_0/2$ |
| 5 | $\vec{V}_2$ (110) | $T_2/2$ |
| 6 | $\vec{V}_1$ (100) | $T_1/2$ |
| 7 | $\vec{V}_0$ (000) | $T_0/4$ |

Total: $T_0/2 + T_1 + T_2 = T_s$ Ō£ō (the pattern is mirrored across $T_s/2$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This symmetric 7-segment sequence is the one most commonly implemented in DSPs (TI TMS320F28379D, Microchip dsPIC33, Renesas RA6T2) and FPGAs for industrial motor drives.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: SVPWM Generation Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete SVPWM signal generation chain, from reference command to gate pulses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Voltage Command Vref and Angle theta] --> B[Clarke Transform: abc to alphabeta]
    B --> C[Sector Identification Module]
    C --> D[Sector Number 1 to 6]
    A --> E[Angle Normalization within Sector]
    E --> F[Dwell Time Calculator T1, T2, T0]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Switching Sequence Generator]
    G --> H[Compare Module with Triangular Carrier]
    H --> I[Gate Pulses: Sa, Sb, Sc]
    I --> J[Three Phase VSI]
    J --> K[Three Phase AC Motor Load]
    L[DC Link Voltage Vdc Sensor] --> F
    M[Position Sensor or Sensorless Estimator] --> A
```

**Explanation of the Block Diagram:**

1. **Reference Voltage Command**: The motor controller (e.g., FOC loop) produces the desired magnitude $\vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert$ and angle $\theta$ for the stator voltage vector.
2. **Clarke Transform**: Converts the three-phase $abc$ reference to a two-phase $\alpha\beta$ representation for sector identification.
3. **Sector Identification Module**: Determines which of the 6 sectors (I to VI) the reference vector lies in, by checking the angle quadrant.
4. **Angle Normalization**: Computes $\theta_{local} = \theta_{ref} \mod 60┬░$, the angle measured from the start of the current sector.
5. **Dwell Time Calculator**: Implements the $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_0$ equations from Section 3.
6. **Switching Sequence Generator**: Builds the symmetric 7-segment pattern for the identified sector.
7. **Compare Module**: The dwell times are converted to compare values, matched against a triangle carrier wave, and the resulting PWM signals become the gate pulses.
8. **Three-Phase VSI**: The IGBT/MOSFET inverter switches receive the gate pulses and produce the three-phase output voltages.
9. **DC Link Sensor**: Provides feedback of $V_{dc}$ so the dwell times can be recalculated dynamically.

## 4.2 Flowchart: SVPWM Algorithm (Software Implementation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start of PWM Interrupt] --> Q[Read Vdc, theta_ref, Vref_mag]
    Q --> R[Determine Sector from theta_ref]
    R --> S[Compute Local Angle theta = theta_ref mod 60]
    S --> T[Calculate T1, T2, T0]
    T --> U{Sector Number}
    U --> U1[Sector 1 or 4: Assign V1 and V2 order]
    U --> U2[Sector 2 or 5: Rotate Assignment]
    U --> U3[Sector 3 or 6: Rotate Assignment]
    U1 --> V[Build Symmetric 7-Segment Sequence]
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V
    V --> W[Load Compare Registers CMPA, CMPB, CMPC]
    W --> X[Wait for Next PWM Period]
    X --> P
```

**Implementation Notes:**

- The flowchart is typical of a real-time DSP/FPGA interrupt service routine (ISR), executed once every $T_s = 1/f_{sw}$.
- The "Compare Registers" hold the dwell times, which are compared against the triangle carrier inside the ePWM/eCAP module of the processor.
- The ISR latency must be less than $T_s$ for proper operation ŌĆö a key reason for using dedicated motor-control DSPs.

## 4.3 Hexagonal Space Vector Diagram (Schematic Representation)

The following Mermaid chart represents the **logical structure** of the SVPWM hexagon ŌĆö the physical 60┬░-spaced vectors and the six sectors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Vector V0 equals 000]:::zero
    A1[Vector V1 equals 100<br>at 0 degrees]:::active
    A2[Vector V2 equals 110<br>at 60 degrees]:::active
    A3[Vector V3 equals 010<br>at 120 degrees]:::active
    A4[Vector V4 equals 011<br>at 180 degrees]:::active
    A5[Vector V5 equals 001<br>at 240 degrees]:::active
    A6[Vector V6 equals 101<br>at 300 degrees]:::active
    A7[Vector V7 equals 111]:::zero

    S1[Sector I: 0 to 60 deg]:::sector
    S2[Sector II: 60 to 120 deg]:::sector
    S3[Sector III: 120 to 180 deg]:::sector
    S4[Sector IV: 180 to 240 deg]:::sector
    S5[Sector V: 240 to 300 deg]:::sector
    S6[Sector VI: 300 to 360 deg]:::sector

    A1 --- S1
    A2 --- S1
    A2 --- S2
    A3 --- S2
    A3 --- S3
    A4 --- S3
    A4 --- S4
    A5 --- S4
    A5 --- S5
    A6 --- S5
    A6 --- S6
    A1 --- S6

    classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zero fill:#888,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef sector fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each sector is bounded by two active vectors. For example, Sector I is bounded by $\vec{V}_1$ (at 0┬░) and $\vec{V}_2$ (at 60┬░). Any reference vector in Sector I is synthesized by time-averaging these two vectors plus the zero vector. The active vectors are shown in **gold (active states)** and zero vectors in **gray (zero states)**.

## 4.4 Comparison Chart: SVPWM vs SPWM (Engineering Trade-offs)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Choose Modulation Strategy}
    Q1 --> R1[Need Maximum DC Bus Utilization]
    Q1 --> R2[Need Simpler Implementation]
    Q1 --> R3[Need Lower Switching Loss]

    R1 --> S1[SVPWM: 15.47 percent higher]
    R2 --> S2[SPWM: Simpler code, easier DSP]
    R3 --> S3[SPWM: Lower harmonic distortion at low m]
```
