## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Key Formula Flash-Cards (Rapid Revision)**

| Concept | One-Line Recall |
|---------|----------------|
| **THD** | ŌłÜ(╬Ż VŌéÖ┬▓)/VŌéü, with n = 2, 3, ŌĆ” |
| **DF** | VŌéü/V_total = 1/ŌłÜ(1 + THD┬▓) |
| **HFŌéÖ** | VŌéÖ/VŌéü, dimensionless |
| **LOH** | ŌłÜ(╬Ż VŌéÖ┬▓ for n Ōēż N)/VŌéü |
| **Line harmonic orders (180┬░)** | n = 6k ┬▒ 1 (5, 7, 11, 13, ŌĆ”) |
| **Phase harmonic orders (180┬░)** | n = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, ŌĆ” |
| **Line harmonic orders (120┬░)** | n = 6k ┬▒ 1 (5, 7, 11, 13, ŌĆ”) |
| **Phase harmonic orders (120┬░)** | n = 6k ┬▒ 1 (3, 9, 15 absent) |
| **Fundamental line RMS (180┬░)** | (2ŌłÜ6/ŽĆ)┬ĘVŌéø Ōēł 0.7797 VŌéø |
| **THD of square wave (line)** | Ōēł 0.3108 or 31.08% |
| **DF of square wave (line)** | Ōēł 0.9549 |
| **LOH up to n=13** | Ōēł 0.2731 or 27.31% |
| **Triplen cancellation reason** | 3 ├Ś 120┬░ = 360┬░ ŌåÆ in-phase in all phases ŌåÆ cancels in difference |
| **PWM advantage** | LOH drops drastically; harmonics shift to high-frequency sidebands |
| **PWM fundamental** | mŌéÉ ┬Ę (VŌéø/2) for mŌéÉ Ōēż 1 (linear region) |
| **Square-wave fundamental** | (4VŌéø/ŽĆ) for phase, (4ŌłÜ3 VŌéø/ŽĆ) for line voltage peak |
| **IEEE 519 grid-tie limit** | THD Ōēż 5% |

### **Conceptual Checklist Before Exam**

- Ō£ģ Can I write the Fourier series of a 180┬░ square wave from scratch?
- Ō£ģ Do I know why `cos(ŽĆ/6) = ŌłÜ3/2` appears in the 120┬░ mode formula?
- Ō£ģ Can I prove that the 3rd harmonic cancels in `v_ab = v_an ŌłÆ v_bn`?
- Ō£ģ Am I clear on the difference between HF, THD, DF, and LOH?
- Ō£ģ Can I compute the THD of a 180┬░ VSI line voltage without a calculator (knowing 0.3108)?
- Ō£ģ Do I know which harmonic orders cause positive vs. negative sequence torque?
- Ō£ģ Have I memorized that `V_{1,rms,line} = 0.7797 VŌéø` for 180┬░ mode?
- Ō£ģ Can I justify why PWM is preferred for drives despite higher total THD?

### **Most Likely KTU Question Patterns**

1. **Derive THD of 180┬░ mode line voltage** ŌĆö asked 4 of last 5 semesters.
2. **Compare 180┬░ and 120┬░ modes** w.r.t. harmonic content ŌĆö high probability.
3. **"Why are triplens absent in line voltage?"** ŌĆö guaranteed to appear.
4. **Calculate LOH for a specified n** ŌĆö 3-mark Part A question.
5. **Justify PWM for grid-tie over square-wave** ŌĆö design-oriented question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induction Motor Drives and Vector Control Techniques: Induction Motor Drives - Torque-Speed Characteristics of Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Induction Motor Drives and Vector Control Techniques: Induction Motor Drives - Torque-Speed Characteristics of Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Torque-Speed Characteristic of a Three-Phase Induction Motor** is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the electromagnetic (air-gap) torque developed by the machine and the mechanical speed of the rotor shaft, expressed as a function of **slip (s)**. It is the single most important steady-state performance curve used to analyze, select, and control an induction motor drive in industrial applications.

The characteristic is governed by the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor and the **Thevenin equivalent** seen from the rotor branch. The standard expression, derived from power-balance in the rotor circuit, is:

$$T_{e} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{s}\right)}{\left(R_{1} + \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{s}\right)^{2} + \left(X_{1} + X_{2}^{'}\right)^{2}}$$

where:
- $T_{e}$ = Electromagnetic torque developed (Nm)
- $V_{1}$ = Per-phase stator supply voltage (V)
- $R_{1}, X_{1}$ = Per-phase stator resistance and leakage reactance ($\Omega$)
- $R_{2}^{'}, X_{2}^{'}$ = Per-phase rotor resistance and leakage reactance referred to stator ($\Omega$)
- $s$ = Per-unit slip
- $\omega_{s}$ = Synchronous angular speed (rad/s) = $2\pi N_{s} / 60$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Pedaled Bicycle on a Treadmill"**
>
> Imagine pedaling a bicycle whose rear wheel rests on a treadmill. The treadmill belt can move forward (helping you) or backward (loading you).
> - **Pedaling speed (synchronous speed $N_{s}$)** is fixed by the chain ring (supply frequency).
> - **Wheel speed (rotor speed $N_{r}$)** is the actual rolling speed.
> - **Slip (s)** = how much the wheel "skids" against the belt.
> - The faster you pedal while the belt resists, the more torque your legs produce ŌĆö up to a point. Beyond that "muscle-overload" point, your cadence collapses and torque crashes.
> - Increase load (e.g., a steeper treadmill) ŌåÆ wheel slows ŌåÆ slip rises ŌåÆ motor torque rises to balance the load. This is **self-regulating** behavior.
>
> This is exactly how an induction motor behaves: load increase ŌåÆ rotor slows ŌåÆ slip increases ŌåÆ torque rises ŌåÆ equilibrium.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

- It is a **core building block** for understanding V/f control, vector control, and direct torque control (DTC) studied later in the same module.
- It directly influences **drive selection**, **stability analysis**, and **starting strategy** (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD).
- A KTU 2024 Scheme examiner almost always asks for a derivation of the torque equation and a sketch of the torqueŌĆōslip curve in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Synchronous speed: $N_{s} = 120 f / P$ **RPM** (with $f$ in **Hz** and $P$ = number of poles).
- Slip at rated load: typically $s \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ (i.e., 2ŌĆō5 %) for standard induction motors.
- Slip at maximum torque (normal rotor): $s_{m} \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of the Theory

The torqueŌĆōspeed characteristic of an induction motor emerges from three layered analyses:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Referred to Stator)**

The induction motor is represented by a single-phase equivalent circuit (valid for balanced 3-phase steady-state operation):

```
Stator Side                Rotor Side (referred)
  ŌöĆŌöĆ[R1]ŌöĆŌöĆjX1ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆ[R2'/s]ŌöĆŌöĆjX2'ŌöĆŌöĆ
               Ōöé         Ōöé
              jXm        Ōöé
               Ōöé         Ōöé
  V1 ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

- $R_{2}^{'}/s$ is a **load-dependent virtual resistance**: as slip $s$ varies, the apparent rotor resistance changes, mimicking mechanical loading.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Thevenin Equivalent Seen from the Rotor Branch**

The stator side is reduced to a Thevenin source looking into the rotor terminals:

$$V_{th} \;=\; V_{1} \cdot \dfrac{jX_{m}}{R_{1} + j(X_{1} + X_{m})} \quad ; \quad Z_{th} \;=\; R_{th} + jX_{th} = \dfrac{jX_{m}(R_{1}+jX_{1})}{R_{1}+j(X_{1}+X_{m})}$$

For typical machines where $X_{m} \gg X_{1}$ and $X_{m} \gg R_{1}$:

$$V_{th} \approx V_{1} \cdot \dfrac{X_{m}}{X_{1}+X_{m}}, \qquad X_{th} \approx X_{1}, \qquad R_{th} \approx R_{1}\left(\dfrac{X_{m}}{X_{1}+X_{m}}\right)^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Thevenin reduction?** It collapses the entire stator into a single source feeding the rotor branch, allowing a closed-form algebraic expression for torque. This is the standard KTU-board derivation step.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Air-Gap Power and Electromagnetic Torque**

The power crossing the air gap (per phase) is:

$$P_{g,\phi} = I_{2}^{'2} \cdot \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{s}$$

The total air-gap power is $P_{g} = 3 I_{2}^{'2} R_{2}^{'}/s$. Mechanical (developed) power is $P_{m} = (1-s) P_{g}$.

Torque is mechanical power divided by mechanical angular velocity $\omega_{m} = (1-s)\,\omega_{s}$. The factors $(1-s)$ cancel:

$$\boxed{\,T_{e} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{g}}{\omega_{s}} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}(R_{2}^{'}/s)}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\,}$$

(Using the simplified $V_{1}$ in place of $V_{th}$ gives the **approximate** but universally-used expression.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Condition for Maximum Torque**

Differentiate $T_{e}$ w.r.t. $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT_{e}}{ds} = 0$. This yields:

$$s_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{1}^{2} + (X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}}$$

For $X_{1}+X_{2}^{'} \gg R_{1}$ (the usual case), the slip at maximum torque simplifies to:

$$s_{m} \;\approx\; \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{X_{1}+X_{2}^{'}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum (Pull-Out) Torque**

Substituting $s = s_{m}$ back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}}{2\left[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\right]}$$

Approximate form (neglecting $R_{1}$):

$$T_{max} \;\approx\; \dfrac{3\,V_{1}^{2}}{2\,\omega_{s}\,(X_{1}+X_{2}^{')}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Golden Rules of the TorqueŌĆōSlip Curve (high-yield KTU points):**
> 1. **$T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance $R_{2}^{'}$** ŌĆö but the slip at which it occurs is *directly proportional* to $R_{2}^{'}$.
> 2. **$T_{max} \propto V_{1}^{2}$** ŌĆö halving the supply voltage quarters the maximum torque.
> 3. **Starting torque $T_{st}$ is obtained by setting $s = 1$** in the torque equation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_{s} = 120 f / P$ | RPM, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = (N_{s}-N_{r})/N_{s}$ | per-unit (0 to 1) |
| 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_{r} = s f$ | Hz |
| 4 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_{e} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}(R_{2}^{'}/s)}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$ | Nm |
| 5 | Slip at $T_{max}$ (general) | $s_{m} = \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}}$ | per-unit |
| 6 | Slip at $T_{max}$ (approx) | $s_{m} \approx R_{2}^{'}/(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})$ | per-unit |
| 7 | Maximum torque (general) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}\left[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\right]}$ | Nm |
| 8 | Maximum torque (approx) | $T_{max} \approx \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})}$ | Nm |
| 9 | Starting torque (s=1) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2} R_{2}^{'}}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'})^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$ | Nm |
| 10 | Slip for max torque:start torque ratio | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 s_{m}(1+a)}{1+s_{m}^{2}}$, $a = R_{1}/R_{2}^{'}$ | ratio |
| 11 | Stable operating region | $0 < s < s_{m}$ | under $T_{max}$ |
| 12 | Unstable region | $s_{m} < s \leq 1$ | above $T_{max}$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drive selection in industry:** The shape of the curve tells engineers whether the motor can deliver starting torque for the load (e.g., conveyors, crushers) and how it behaves under sudden load surges.
- **V/f and vector control:** Both control strategies attempt to *reshape* this curve in real time ŌĆö keeping the flux constant so that $T_{max}$ remains available at all speeds.
- **Soft starters and DOL:** The high inrush current at $s=1$ (5ŌĆō7├Ś rated) traces directly to the low impedance of $R_{2}^{'}/s$ at starting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Electromagnetic Torque Equation

**Given:** Per-phase equivalent circuit with $V_{1}$ applied to stator, rotor branch having $R_{2}^{'}/s + jX_{2}^{'}$.

**Step A ŌĆö Rotor Current (per phase, referred to stator)**

The current in the rotor branch is:

$$I_{2}^{'} = \dfrac{V_{1}}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s) + j(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})}$$

Its magnitude squared:

$$I_{2}^{'2} = \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2} + (X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$$

**Step B ŌĆö Air-Gap Power (per phase ŌåÆ total)**

The per-phase power dissipated in $R_{2}^{'}/s$ represents the total air-gap power per phase:

$$P_{g,\phi} = I_{2}^{'2} \cdot \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{s}$$

Multiplying by 3 (for 3 phases):

$$P_{g} = 3 I_{2}^{'2} \cdot \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{s} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2} (R_{2}^{'}/s)}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2} + (X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$$

**Step C ŌĆö Mechanical Power Developed**

Mechanical power = (1 ŌłÆ s) ├Ś Air-gap power:

$$P_{m} = (1-s)\,P_{g} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}(R_{2}^{'}/s)(1-s)}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2} + (X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$$

**Step D ŌĆö Mechanical Angular Velocity**

Rotor mechanical speed: $\omega_{m} = (1-s)\,\omega_{s}$.

**Step E ŌĆö Electromagnetic Torque**

$$T_{e} = \dfrac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}} = \dfrac{(1-s)\,P_{g}}{(1-s)\,\omega_{s}} = \dfrac{P_{g}}{\omega_{s}}$$

The factors of $(1-s)$ cancel, giving the clean expression:

$$\boxed{\;T_{e} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}(R_{2}^{'}/s)}{(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2} + (X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Examiners often mark the cancellation of $(1-s)$ in Step E as a key conceptual step. Do not skip it; write the words "since mechanical power = torque ├Ś (1ŌłÆs)┬ĘŽēs, the (1ŌłÆs) factors cancel".

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Slip at Maximum Torque

**Step F ŌĆö Setup the derivative**

For mathematical convenience, define $y = R_{2}^{'}/s$. Then $T_{e} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{y}{(R_{1}+y)^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$.

Maximum occurs when $\dfrac{dT_{e}}{dy} = 0$ (or equivalently $\dfrac{dT_{e}}{ds} = 0$).

$$\dfrac{d}{dy}\!\left[\dfrac{y}{(R_{1}+y)^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}\right] = 0, \quad \text{where } X_{T} = X_{1}+X_{2}^{'}$$

**Step G ŌĆö Apply quotient rule**

Using $\dfrac{d}{dy}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{D}\right) = \dfrac{N'D - ND'}{D^{2}}$, with $N=y$, $N'=1$, $D=(R_{1}+y)^{2}+X_{T}^{2}$, $D'=2(R_{1}+y)$:

$$1\cdot D - y \cdot 2(R_{1}+y) = 0$$

$$(R_{1}+y)^{2} + X_{T}^{2} - 2y(R_{1}+y) = 0$$

**Step H ŌĆö Expand and simplify**

$$R_{1}^{2} + 2R_{1}y + y^{2} + X_{T}^{2} - 2R_{1}y - 2y^{2} = 0$$

$$R_{1}^{2} + X_{T}^{2} - y^{2} = 0$$

$$y^{2} = R_{1}^{2} + X_{T}^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; y = \sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}$$

**Step I ŌĆö Convert back to slip**

Since $y = R_{2}^{'}/s$:

$$s_{m} = \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\;s_{m} = \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}}\;}$$

For typical machines $X_{1}+X_{2}^{'} \gg R_{1}$:

$$s_{m} \approx \dfrac{R_{2}^{'}}{X_{1}+X_{2}^{'}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Torque

**Step J ŌĆö Substitute $s_{m}$ into torque expression**

The general torque expression (using $y$ form):

$$T_{e} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{y}{(R_{1}+y)^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}$$

At $y = \sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}$:

$$(R_{1}+y)^{2} = R_{1}^{2} + 2R_{1}\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}} + (R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}) = 2R_{1}^{2} + X_{T}^{2} + 2R_{1}\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}$$

Add $X_{T}^{2}$:

$$\text{Denominator} = 2R_{1}^{2} + 2X_{T}^{2} + 2R_{1}\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}} = 2\left[R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}+R_{1}\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}\right]$$

Factor using $R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2} = (\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}})^{2}$:

$$= 2\left[(\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}})\!\left(\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}} + R_{1}\right)\right] = 2\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}\,(R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}})$$

**Step K ŌĆö Form the maximum torque**

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{\omega_{s}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}}{2\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}\,(R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}})} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}\left[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\right]}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}\left[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\right]}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximate form (used in 90% of KTU numerical problems):**
> Neglect $R_{1}$ since $X_{1}+X_{2}^{'} \gg R_{1}$:
> $$T_{max} \approx \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})}$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has $R_{1}=0.5\;\Omega$, $R_{2}^{'}=0.5\;\Omega$, $X_{1}+X_{2}^{'}=2\;\Omega$. For $V_{1}=400/\sqrt{3}$ V (line), find (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip at maximum torque, (iii) maximum torque, (iv) starting torque. (Take approximate form.)

**Solution:**

(i) $N_{s} = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM, $\omega_{s} = 2\pi \times 1500/60 = 157.08$ rad/s.

(ii) $s_{m} = 0.5 / 2 = 0.25$.

(iii) $T_{max} = 3 \times (400/\sqrt{3})^{2} / [2 \times 157.08 \times 2]$
$V_{1} = 230.94$ V ŌåÆ $V_{1}^{2} = 53333.33$
$T_{max} = 3 \times 53333.33 / (2 \times 157.08 \times 2) = 160000 / 628.32 \approx 254.6$ Nm.

(iv) $T_{st}$ at $s=1$:
$T_{st} = \dfrac{3 \times 53333.33 \times 0.5}{157.08 \times [(0.5+0.5)^{2}+2^{2}]} = \dfrac{80000}{157.08 \times (1+4)} = \dfrac{80000}{785.4} \approx 101.9$ Nm.

> Ratio $T_{st}/T_{max} = 101.9/254.6 = 0.40$ (40%).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional flow** of equivalent-circuit quantities during the torque computation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  V1["V1 Per Phase Stator Voltage"] --> R1["R1 Stator Resistance"]
  R1 --> X1["jX1 Stator Leakage Reactance"]
  X1 --> N1["Node A Air Gap"]
  N1 --> XM["jXm Magnetising Reactance"]
  XM --> N2["Node B Neutral Return"]
  V1 -. return path .-> N2
  N1 --> R2["R2 dash over s Effective Rotor Resistance"]
  R2 --> X2["jX2 dash Rotor Leakage Reactance"]
  X2 --> N2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The element $\mathbf{R_{2}^{'}/s}$ is the **only slip-dependent** component. As $s \to 0$ (rotor at synchronous speed), this resistance $\to \infty$, forcing $I_{2}^{'} \to 0$ and torque $\to 0$. At $s = 1$ (starting), it equals $R_{2}^{'}$.

### 4.2 TorqueŌĆōSlip Characteristic with Operating Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Region1["Stable Operating Region 0 to sm"]
    R1A["Low Slip Region 0 to 0.05"] --> R1B["Normal Running Torque Steep Curve"]
    R1B --> R1C["Self Regulating Load Sharing"]
  end

  subgraph Peak["Peak Point"]
    P1["Maximum Torque Tmax at slip sm"]
    P2["Pull Out Torque Independent of R2 dash"]
  end

  subgraph Region2["Unstable Region sm to 1"]
    R2A["Slip Greater than sm"] --> R2B["Torque Decreases Sharply"]
    R2B --> R2C["Cannot Sustain Load Motor Stalls"]
  end

  subgraph Start["Starting Point s=1"]
    S1["Starting Torque Tst Computed from Circuit"]
    S2["High Inrush Current 5 to 7 Times Rated"]
  end

  Region1 --> Peak --> Region2
  Region1 -. stable equilibrium .-> Start
```

### 4.3 Effect of Rotor Resistance on the Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A["Normal R2 small"] --> B["sm Small Peak at Low Slip"]
  C["Increased R2 dash"] --> D["sm Shifts to Higher Slip"]
  D --> E["Tmax Unchanged Peak Stays at Same Height"]
  E --> F["Starting Torque Increases"]
  F --> G["Curve Pulls Rightward Along Slip Axis"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Diagram 4.3:** Increasing $R_{2}^{'}$ **does not change $T_{max}$** ŌĆö it only shifts the slip at which $T_{max}$ occurs to the right. This is the engineering principle behind **wound-rotor induction motors** with external rheostats used for high-starting-torque applications (e.g., elevators, hoists).

### 4.4 Thevenin Reduction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  V1["V1 Source"] --> R1["R1"] --> X1["jX1"] --> NodeA["Node A"]
  NodeA --> XM["jXm"] --> NodeB["Node B Return"]
  NodeA --> Thev["Thevenin Equivalent"]
  Thev --> Vth["Vth Approx V1 times Xm over X1+Xm"]
  Thev --> Zth["Zth equals Rth plus jXth"]
  Zth --> R2s["R2 dash over s"]
  R2s --> X2d["jX2 dash"]
  X2d --> NodeB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
Define slip of an induction motor. Explain why the slip cannot be zero in a practical induction motor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Slip $s$ is defined as the relative difference between synchronous speed $N_{s}$ and rotor speed $N_{r}$, expressed as a fraction of $N_{s}$:

$$s = \dfrac{N_{s}-N_{r}}{N_{s}}$$

In a practical induction motor, the rotor must always rotate at a speed *less* than synchronous speed. If the rotor were to run at $N_{s}$ ($s=0$), the rotor conductors would be stationary relative to the rotating magnetic field, no EMF would be induced in them ($E_{2} = s E_{2,\text{max}} = 0$), no rotor current would flow, and no torque would be produced. Hence, mechanical load would not be sustained and the rotor would decelerate ŌĆö making $s=0$ physically unattainable in motor mode. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Sample Paper]**
State whether the maximum torque of an induction motor depends on rotor resistance. Justify your answer with the relevant expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
No. The maximum (pull-out) torque of an induction motor is **independent of rotor resistance**. From the standard expression:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}\left[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}\right]}$$

This expression contains no $R_{2}^{'}$ term. However, the **slip at which maximum torque occurs** is directly proportional to $R_{2}^{'}$, given by $s_{m} = R_{2}^{'}/\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}$. Therefore, increasing $R_{2}^{'}$ shifts the peak torque to higher slip values (towards starting) without altering its magnitude. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). Internal choice: answer **either (a) OR (b) only** from the two alternatives provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
(a) Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque of a 3-phase induction motor from its per-phase equivalent circuit. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

(b) A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has per-phase parameters (referred to stator) $R_{1}=0.4\;\Omega$, $R_{2}^{'}=0.6\;\Omega$, $X_{1}=0.6\;\Omega$, $X_{2}^{'}=0.8\;\Omega$. For a line voltage of 415 V, compute (using approximate expressions):
   (i) Synchronous speed and slip at maximum torque.
   (ii) Maximum torque.
   (iii) Starting torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- State assumptions: balanced 3-phase supply, sinusoidal steady state, per-phase equivalent circuit valid, linear magnetic circuit. **(1 mark)**
- Draw per-phase equivalent circuit, identify $R_{2}^{'}/s$ as the slip-dependent effective resistance. **(1 mark)**
- Write rotor current expression $I_{2}^{'} = V_{1} / [(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)+j(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})]$. **(1 mark)**
- Express per-phase air-gap power $P_{g,\phi} = I_{2}^{'2} R_{2}^{'}/s$ and total $P_{g} = 3 I_{2}^{'2} R_{2}^{'}/s$. **(1 mark)**
- Relate mechanical power $P_{m} = (1-s)P_{g}$ and torque $T_{e} = P_{m}/\omega_{m}$ with $\omega_{m} = (1-s)\omega_{s}$. **(1 mark)**
- Cancel $(1-s)$ and write the final boxed torque expression. **(1 mark)**
- Units and physical meaning statement. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- $V_{1,\text{phase}} = 415/\sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V.
- $N_{s} = 120 \times 50/6 = 1000$ RPM; $\omega_{s} = 2\pi \times 1000/60 = 104.72$ rad/s.
- $X_{T} = X_{1}+X_{2}^{'} = 1.4\;\Omega$.
- (i) $s_{m} = R_{2}^{'}/X_{T} = 0.6/1.4 = 0.4286$ (using approx). **(2 marks)**
- (ii) $T_{max} = 3 V_{1}^{2}/(2 \omega_{s} X_{T}) = 3 \times (239.6)^{2} / (2 \times 104.72 \times 1.4) = 172320.5 / 293.21 \approx 587.7$ Nm. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) At $s=1$: Denominator = $(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'})^{2}+X_{T}^{2} = (1.0)^{2}+(1.4)^{2} = 1.0 + 1.96 = 2.96\;\Omega^{2}$.
  $T_{st} = 3 V_{1}^{2} R_{2}^{'}/(\omega_{s} \times 2.96) = 3 \times 57408 \times 0.6 / (104.72 \times 2.96) = 103334.4/309.97 \approx 333.4$ Nm. **(2 marks)**

**Final Answers:** $N_{s}=1000$ RPM, $s_{m}=0.4286$, $T_{max}\approx 587.7$ Nm, $T_{st}\approx 333.4$ Nm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Internal Alternative)**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Supplementary Exam Pattern]**
(a) With the help of the per-phase equivalent circuit, derive the expression for slip at maximum torque and the maximum torque of an induction motor. Show that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. **(7 marks)**

(b) Draw the torqueŌĆōslip characteristic of a 3-phase induction motor and label all critical points (starting torque, pull-out torque, normal operating region). Explain how the curve changes with (i) increase in rotor resistance and (ii) reduction in supply voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $s_{m}$ and $T_{max}$ (7 marks):**
- Write torque in $y$-form: $T_{e} = (3V_{1}^{2}/\omega_{s}) \cdot y/[(R_{1}+y)^{2}+X_{T}^{2}]$, where $y = R_{2}^{'}/s$. **(1 mark)**
- Differentiate with respect to $y$ and equate to zero. **(1 mark)**
- Simplify to obtain $y^{2} = R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}$, hence $s_{m} = R_{2}^{'}/\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+X_{T}^{2}}$. **(2 marks)**
- Substitute $y$ at $s_{m}$ back into torque expression and simplify. **(1 mark)**
- Arrive at final $T_{max}$ expression. **(1 mark)**
- **Independence argument:** $R_{2}^{'}$ does not appear in the final $T_{max}$ expression; therefore $T_{max}$ is independent of rotor resistance. However, $s_{m} \propto R_{2}^{'}$, so increasing $R_{2}^{'}$ only shifts the slip at which $T_{max}$ occurs. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Sketch and Effects (7 marks):**
- Sketch the characteristic: torque rises from 0 at $s=0$ to $T_{max}$ at $s=s_{m}$, then drops to $T_{st}$ at $s=1$. Label stable ($s < s_{m}$) and unstable ($s > s_{m}$) regions. **(3 marks)**
- (i) **Effect of increased $R_{2}^{'}$:** $T_{max}$ remains the same height; $s_{m}$ shifts to the right (towards $s=1$); $T_{st}$ increases. Useful for high-starting-torque applications. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) **Effect of reduced supply voltage:** $T_{max}$ and $T_{st}$ both fall as the **square** of the voltage (since $T \propto V_{1}^{2}$); slip at $T_{max}$ is unchanged. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** ŌĆö students often derive per-phase torque but forget to multiply by 3 for the total 3-phase torque. **ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks.**
> 2. **Skipping the cancellation of $(1-s)$** ŌĆö examiners specifically look for the line $T_{e} = P_{m}/\omega_{m} = (1-s)P_{g}/[(1-s)\omega_{s}]$. **ŌłÆ1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong assumption direction** ŌĆö saying "approximate $T_{max}$ neglects rotor resistance" is **wrong**; it neglects *stator* resistance. **ŌłÆ1 mark.**
> 4. **Units inconsistency** ŌĆö writing $N_{s}$ in rad/s while using 120$f/P$ (which gives RPM). Convert explicitly using $\omega_{s} = 2\pi N_{s}/60$. **ŌłÆ0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Mixing up $R_{2}^{'}$ (referred) and $R_{2}$ (actual)** ŌĆö in deep-bar / cage motors the rotor is already referred. State it clearly. **ŌłÆ1 mark.**
> 6. **Plotting torqueŌĆōslip with $N_{r}$ on the wrong axis** ŌĆö the characteristic is conventionally $T_{e}$ vs. $s$ (slip), not $T_{e}$ vs. $N_{r}$. The latter is its mirror image.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Slip definition:** $s = (N_{s}-N_{r})/N_{s}$; range $0 < s \leq 1$ in motor mode.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_{s} = 120 f / P$ RPM.
- **Rotor frequency:** $f_{r} = s \cdot f$.
- **Rotor effective resistance:** $R_{2}^{'}/s$ ŌĆö slip-dependent; becomes $R_{2}^{'}$ at start, $\to \infty$ at synchronous speed.
- **Master torque equation (3-phase):**
  $$T_{e} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}(R_{2}^{'}/s)}{\omega_{s}\left[(R_{1}+R_{2}^{'}/s)^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}\right]}$$
- **Slip at maximum torque:** $s_{m} = R_{2}^{'}/\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}} \approx R_{2}^{'}/(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})$.
- **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}[R_{1}+\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})^{2}}]} \approx \dfrac{3 V_{1}^{2}}{2\omega_{s}(X_{1}+X_{2}^{'})}$.
- **$T_{max}$ is independent of $R_{2}^{'}$; $s_{m}$ is directly proportional to $R_{2}^{'}$.**
- **$T_{max} \propto V_{1}^{2}$** ŌĆö voltage-sensitive torque.
- **Operating regions:** $0 < s < s_{m}$ ŌåÆ stable; $s_{m} < s \leq 1$ ŌåÆ unstable (motor stalls here under constant load).
- **Starting torque** (at $s=1$): use the full torque equation with $s=1$.
- **Engineering uses:** drive selection, V/f control, vector control, soft-starting, braking (plugging, regenerative).
- **Practical slip range at rated load:** 2ŌĆō5 % for standard cage motors.
- **The Thevenin reduction** ($V_{th}$, $Z_{th}$) is a key simplifying step used in vector control derivations later in the same module ŌĆö remember it well.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Control by Varying Stator Frequency and Voltage, Voltage Source Inverter Driven Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed Control by Varying Stator Frequency and Voltage, Voltage Source Inverter Driven Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Speed Control by Varying Stator Frequency and Voltage** is a scalar control method applied to three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors (SCIM) in which the stator supply frequency **f** and the stator line voltage **V** are varied *simultaneously* in a defined ratio such that the air-gap flux $\Phi_g$ remains nominally constant throughout the operating speed range. The fundamental relationship governing this scheme is the **Volts-per-Hertz (V/f) Law**, expressed as:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant (below base speed)}$$

This method is the **industrial workhorse** for variable-speed drives in pumps, fans, conveyors, HVAC systems, and machine tools, owing to its simplicity, ruggedness of the squirrel-cage motor, and absence of slip rings or brushes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** A Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) feeding a PWM-modulated variable-voltage, variable-frequency (VVVF) output is the *de facto* hardware realization of V/f control. The student is expected to draw the closed-loop V/f control architecture and the six-step / PWM inverter leg topology.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Bicycle on a Hill"

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle** up a hill, and the hill is your **motor's mechanical load**.

- Your **leg cadence (pedalling rate)** is analogous to the **stator frequency (f)** ŌĆö it dictates how fast the wheel *wants* to spin (synchronous speed).
- Your **gear ratio + pushing force** together represent the **stator voltage (V)** ŌĆö the effective "push" delivered per cycle.

Now consider the two extremes:

| Scenario | What happens? | Motor Equivalent |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| You pedal fast but barely push on pedals | Wheel spins fast but **no torque** | High frequency, low voltage ŌåÆ flux collapse, weak motor |
| You push very hard but pedal very slowly | Massive force, almost no motion | Low frequency, full voltage ŌåÆ **flux saturation**, copper burn-out |

The **golden balance** is achieved when the **push (V) and pedalling rate (f) are proportionally scaled** ŌĆö this keeps your "rotational energy per pedal-stroke" constant. **That constant push-per-cadence is exactly the V/f ratio**, and it preserves the motor's magnetic muscle (air-gap flux).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Line-to-line RMS stator voltage: **400 V (typical industrial)**
> - Rated frequency: **50 Hz (India)** / 60 Hz
> - Base speed $N_{base}$ : the speed at which V and f both reach their rated maximum.
> - Below base speed ŌåÆ **Constant Torque Region**; above base speed ŌåÆ **Constant Power (Field Weakening) Region**.

---

## 1.3 Why V/f Control? ŌĆö The Governing Physics in One Sentence

The **air-gap flux $\Phi_g$** of an induction motor is proportional to the ratio $V/f$ (from the induced EMF equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi$). If $V/f$ is held constant, flux stays constant, and the motor can deliver its **rated torque at any speed** below base speed ŌĆö exactly what most industrial loads demand.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö The Induced EMF and Flux Linkage

The per-phase RMS EMF induced in the stator winding of an induction motor is given by the **transformer EMF equation** (modified for a distributed short-pitched winding):

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_g \, N_1 \, K_{w1}$$

where:
- $E_1$ = RMS induced EMF per phase (V)
- $f$ = stator supply frequency (Hz)
- $\phi_g$ = air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $N_1$ = number of turns per phase
- $K_{w1}$ = winding factor (Ōēł 0.95 for a typical 3-phase winding)

Since the stator impedance drop is small at rated frequency, $E_1 \approx V_1$ (the applied voltage). Rearranging:

$$\phi_g = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, K_{w1}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi_g \propto \frac{V}{f}$$

**Conclusion:** To maintain $\phi_g$ = rated flux at all speeds, **V must be changed in proportion to f**.

---

## 2.2 The Two Operating Regions of V/f Control

### Region I ŌĆö Constant Torque Region (Below Base Speed)

- Frequency is varied from $f_{min}$ (typically 3 Hz) to $f_{rated}$ (50 Hz).
- Voltage is varied proportionally so that $V/f$ = constant.
- Air-gap flux $\phi_g$ = rated flux.
- Motor delivers **constant rated torque** at all speeds.
- The mechanical output power rises linearly with speed.

### Region II ŌĆö Constant Power Region ŌĆö Field Weakening (Above Base Speed)

- Frequency is increased beyond $f_{rated}$, but **stator voltage cannot exceed rated voltage** (limited by DC bus / insulation).
- Therefore $V/f$ ratio **falls**, and $\phi_g$ decreases ("field weakening").
- Since $T \propto \phi_g \cdot I_r$, and $\phi_g$ falls while current remains at rated value, **torque falls inversely with speed**.
- Mechanical output power $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ remains **approximately constant**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Region of Validity |
|---|----------------------|--------------------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | RPM | All regions |
| 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | All regions |
| 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu (0 to 1) | All regions |
| 4 | Rotor mechanical speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM | All regions |
| 5 | Air-gap flux (approx.) | $\phi_g \approx \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 K_{w1}}$ | Wb | Neglects stator drop |
| 6 | Thevenin voltage referred to rotor | $V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$ | V | Equivalent ckt |
| 7 | Thevenin impedance | $Z_{th} = \dfrac{jX_m (R_1 + jX_1)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$ | $\Omega$ | Equivalent ckt |
| 8 | Rotor-branch current (referred) | $I_2' = \dfrac{V_{th}}{Z_{th} + R_2'/s}$ | A | Equivalent ckt |
| 9 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{I_2'^2 \, (R_2'/s)}{1}$ | N┬Ęm | All regions |
| 10 | Torque (simplified) | $T_e \approx \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 \, (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | N┬Ęm | Stator drop neglected |
| 11 | Maximum (pull-out) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{(R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + X_{total}^2})}$ | N┬Ęm | Pull-out slip |
| 12 | Slip at maximum torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_2' + X_{th})^2}}$ | pu | Stable/unstable boundary |
| 13 | Rated V/f ratio | $\left(\dfrac{V}{f}\right)_{rated} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$ | V/Hz | Below base speed |
| 14 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | W | Air-gap power |
| 15 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | W | All slip |
| 16 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_r$ | W | Shaft output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state that at **pull-out slip**, the maximum torque is **independent of rotor resistance**, but $s_{max}$ is **directly proportional to rotor resistance**. Memorize these two facts ŌĆö they fetch 2ŌĆō3 marks in Part A regularly.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Industry / Domain | Application of V/f VSI Drive |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| HVAC & Building Services | Fan and blower speed control for energy saving |
| Water Treatment Plants | Pump flow regulation |
| Textile Mills | Spindle speed control of looms |
| Paper & Pulp Mills | Sectional drive of rollers |
| elevators / Lifts | Smooth start-stop without jerking |
| Cement Industry | Conveyor and kiln drive |
| Electric Vehicles | Auxiliary motor control (pumps, blowers) |

**Real-world impact:** Variable-torque loads (fans, pumps) obey the **affinity law** $P \propto N^3$. Reducing speed by 20% cuts power demand by nearly **50%**, making VFDs the single biggest energy-saving retrofit in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Synchronous Speed and the Linear Speed-Frequency Law

**Given:** A 3-phase induction motor with $P$ poles supplied at frequency $f$ Hz.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Frequency to Electrical Angular Velocity**

The stator magnetic field rotates at the synchronous electrical angular velocity:

$$\omega_{se} = 2\pi f \;\; \text{(rad/s, electrical)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Electrical to Mechanical Angular Velocity**

For a $P$-pole machine, there are $P/2$ pole-pairs, so the mechanical angular velocity is:

$$\omega_{sm} = \frac{\omega_{se}}{P/2} = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \;\; \text{(rad/s, mechanical)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conversion to Synchronous Speed in RPM**

$$N_s = \omega_{sm} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{4\pi f}{P} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Simplification**

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \;\; \text{RPM}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Air-Gap Flux under V/f Control

**Starting Point:** The per-phase induced EMF of the stator (transformer EMF equation):

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_g \, N_1 \, K_{w1}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Approximation $E_1 \approx V_1$**

Under normal operation with low stator impedance drop (typically 2ŌĆō5% of rated voltage):

$$V_1 \approx E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_g \, N_1 \, K_{w1}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solving for Air-Gap Flux**

$$\phi_g = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 K_{w1}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Defining the V/f Ratio**

$$K_{vf} \triangleq \frac{V_1}{f}$$

Substituting:

$$\phi_g = \frac{K_{vf}}{4.44 \, N_1 K_{w1}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The Governing Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\phi_g = \text{constant} \;\;\iff\;\; \frac{V_1}{f} = \text{constant}}$$

**Physical meaning:** The constant $K_{vf}$ at rated condition is the *design flux* of the motor. Any departure from this ratio changes the operating flux ŌĆö increase V/f ŌåÆ saturation ŌåÆ huge magnetizing current; decrease V/f ŌåÆ under-excitation ŌåÆ reduced torque capability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Torque Equation and Its Slip-Dependence

**Starting Point:** From the **exact per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor (referred to stator):

$$I_2' = \frac{V_{th}}{Z_{th} + R_2'/s}$$

where $V_{th}$ and $Z_{th}$ are the Thevenin equivalents looking back from the rotor branch.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Air-Gap Power**

The power dissipated in the rotor branch (i.e., crossing the air gap) is:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \, I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Torque from Air-Gap Power**

Electromagnetic torque equals air-gap power divided by synchronous mechanical angular velocity:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_{sm}} = \frac{3 I_2'^2 (R_2'/s)}{\omega_{sm}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substituting $I_2'$**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_{sm}} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_{th} + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum Torque Condition**

Setting $\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$, the slip at maximum torque is:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum Torque Value**

Substituting $s_{max}$ back into the torque equation:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_{sm}} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Two Critical Observations**

(i) $T_{max} \propto V_{th}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{max} \propto V^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{max} \propto f^2$ (since $V \propto f$ in V/f mode).

(ii) $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$, but the **slip $s_{max}$ at which it occurs is directly proportional** to $R_2'$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV ŌĆö V/f Profile Including Voltage Boost at Low Frequency

**The Problem:** Below ~10 Hz, the stator impedance drop $I_1(R_1 + jX_1)$ is no longer negligible, so the approximation $E_1 \approx V_1$ fails. The flux begins to *drop* despite maintaining V/f constant.

**The Engineering Fix ŌĆö Voltage Boost (Torque Boost):**

A small additive voltage $V_{boost}$ (typically 5ŌĆō10% of rated) is added at low frequency:

$$V_1(f) = K_{vf} \cdot f + V_{boost}$$

The boost compensates for the $I_1 R_1$ drop at low speed and restores rated torque capability down to about 3 Hz.

**Linear V/f Law:**

$$\boxed{V_1(f) = \left(\frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}\right) \cdot f + V_{boost}}$$

The complete V/f profile has three segments:

| Segment | Frequency Range | V/f Behaviour | Operating Mode |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|
| Boost region | 0 to $f_{min}$ (Ōēł 3 Hz) | $V = K_{vf} f + V_{boost}$ | Boosted |
| Linear region | $f_{min}$ to $f_{rated}$ | $V = K_{vf} f$ | Constant flux |
| Field weakening | $f_{rated}$ to $f_{max}$ (Ōēł 100 Hz) | $V = V_{rated}$ (saturated) | Constant power |

---

## 3.5 Derivation V ŌĆö Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) Output Voltage

A **two-level VSI** uses a fixed DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$. Each phase output (line-to-neutral) can take only two values: $+V_{dc}/2$ or $-V_{dc}/2$ (with respect to the fictitious mid-point).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Fundamental RMS Phase Voltage (Sinusoidal PWM)**

For a Sinusoidal PWM inverter with modulation index $m_a$:

$$V_{ph,1}^{rms} = \frac{m_a \, V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

where $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{tri,peak}}$ is the amplitude modulation ratio, valid for $m_a \le 1$ (linear region).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency Control**

The frequency of the output fundamental is set by the frequency of the sinusoidal reference:

$$f_{out} = f_{control}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combined V/f Realization**

The inverter simultaneously produces:

$$V \propto m_a \quad \text{and} \quad f \propto f_{control}$$

A coordinated control law $m_a = k \cdot f_{control}$ enforces constant V/f ratio in real time.

$$\boxed{V_{ph,1}^{rms} = \frac{(k \cdot f_{control}) \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Closed-Loop V/f Control Architecture ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL [Control and Reference Stage]
        SP[Speed Reference RPMref] --> ERR[Summer Node]
        FB1[Rotor Speed Feedback N] --> ERR
        ERR --> PI[PI Speed Controller]
        PI --> VFQ[V f Function Generator]
        FREF[Frequency Reference fref] --> VFQ
        VFQ --> MAG[Modulation Index ma Output]
        VFQ --> SIN[Sinusoidal Reference Generator]
    end

    subgraph PWM [PWM Inverter Stage]
        SIN --> COMP[PWM Comparator]
        TRI[Triangular Carrier Wave] --> COMP
        MAG --> COMP
        COMP --> G1[Gate Pulses to IGBTs S1 to S6]
    end

    subgraph PWR [Power Stage]
        G1 --> VSI[Two Level Voltage Source Inverter]
        VDC[DC Link Vdc] --> VSI
        VSI --> IM[Three Phase Induction Motor]
        IM --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    end

    IM -- Tachogenerator Feedback --> FB1

    style CTRL fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style PWM fill:#fff4e8,stroke:#b35900
    style PWR fill:#e8ffe8,stroke:#1f7a1f
```

## 4.3 Torque-Speed Characteristics Under V/f Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GRAPH [Family of T vs N Curves]
        DIR[Operating Region Description]
    end

    A1[Curve 1 f 10 Hz V low] --> A2[Pull out torque slightly below rated]
    B1[Curve 2 f 25 Hz V medium] --> B2[Pull out torque near rated]
    C1[Curve 3 f 50 Hz V rated] --> C2[Pull out torque equals rated]
    D1[Curve 4 f 75 Hz V fixed at rated Field Weakening] --> D2[Pull out torque reduced]

    A2 --> NOTE1[Constant Torque Region Below Base Speed]
    B2 --> NOTE1
    C2 --> NOTE1
    D2 --> NOTE2[Constant Power Region Above Base Speed]

    style NOTE1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7a1a
    style NOTE2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the graph:**
- All curves in the **constant-torque region** have nearly the **same pull-out torque**, because $T_{max} \propto f^2 / f = f$ after accounting for $V \propto f$ ... (actually $T_{max} \propto (V/f)^2 \cdot f$ simplifies such that for ideal V/f, the pull-out torque remains nearly constant).
- In the **field-weakening region**, the pull-out torque drops as $1/f$ (approximately).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define V/f control of an induction motor. Why is it necessary to vary the voltage along with frequency?**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 110 words):**
V/f control is a scalar speed-control method for 3-phase induction motors in which the stator voltage **V** and supply frequency **f** are varied together to keep their ratio constant. This is necessary because the air-gap flux $\phi_g = V / (4.44 f N_1 K_{w1})$ is directly proportional to the V/f ratio. If only frequency is changed while voltage is held fixed, the flux either collapses (at low frequency, over-fluxing and saturation) or weakens (at high frequency). Maintaining V/f constant preserves rated flux and hence rated torque capability across the entire speed range below base speed. Above base speed, V is held at rated value while f is increased, allowing field-weakening operation in the constant-power region.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 + Flux equation 1 + Justification 1.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by voltage boost in a V/f drive? When is it applied?**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 90 words):**
Voltage boost refers to the additional stator voltage $V_{boost}$ added to the linear V/f profile at low operating frequencies (typically below 10 Hz). It is required because, at low frequency, the stator impedance drop $I_1(R_1 + jX_1)$ becomes a significant fraction of the applied voltage, causing the air-gap flux to fall below rated value despite V/f being maintained constant. The boost compensates for this drop, restoring rated flux and rated torque at starting and low-speed conditions. The law becomes $V_1(f) = (V_{rated}/f_{rated}) \cdot f + V_{boost}$. Typical boost value is 5ŌĆō10% of rated voltage.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition 1 + Cause 1 + Mathematical expression 1.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the open-loop V/f control scheme of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor using a voltage source inverter (VSI). Discuss the role of PWM in synthesizing variable voltage and frequency from a fixed DC link. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor is controlled by a V/f drive. The rated line voltage is 400 V, rated frequency is 50 Hz, rated slip is 4%. The motor drives a constant-torque load.
  - (i) Determine the synchronous speed, the rated rotor speed, and the rotor speed when the drive is set to 30 Hz.
  - (ii) If the load torque is 60 N┬Ęm, calculate the air-gap power and rotor copper loss at 30 Hz operation, assuming the rotor branch has $R_2' = 0.4 \, \Omega$ and $X_2' = 1.2 \, \Omega$ per phase, and the Thevenin voltage is 90% of the phase voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Open-Loop V/f Control Scheme

**Block Diagram:**
Reference block ŌåÆ V/f function generator ŌåÆ Sinusoidal reference (variable amplitude + variable frequency) ŌåÆ PWM Comparator ŌåÆ Triangular carrier ŌåÆ Gate pulses ŌåÆ 2-level VSI ŌåÆ Fixed DC link ŌåÆ 3-phase induction motor.

**Operating Principle:**
- A **fixed DC-link voltage $V_{dc}$** is obtained from the AC mains via an uncontrolled diode rectifier and a large filter capacitor.
- The **V/f function generator** receives a frequency command $f_{ref}$ from the operator/PLC and outputs both a frequency $f_{ref}$ and a proportional modulation index $m_a = (V_{rated}/f_{rated}) \cdot f_{ref} / V_{dc}$.
- The **sinusoidal reference generator** produces three sinusoidal waveforms $V_a^*, V_b^*, V_c^*$ of amplitude $m_a \cdot V_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{ref}$.
- Each sinusoidal reference is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** ($f_c = 2$ to 20 kHz).
- The comparator output generates **gate pulses** for the IGBTs such that the upper switch is ON when the reference exceeds the carrier.
- The line-to-line inverter output is a **PWM waveform** whose fundamental component has RMS value $\dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$ (line-to-line) and frequency $f_{ref}$.
- Hence **voltage variation is achieved by $m_a$** and **frequency variation by $f_{ref}$**, simultaneously ŌĆö implementing V/f control.

**Key advantage of PWM:** Low harmonic distortion, reduced torque pulsations, smaller DC-link ripple current, and elimination of low-order harmonics compared to six-step (square-wave) operation.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$ poles, $f_{rated} = 50$ Hz, $V_{LL} = 400$ V (line)
- $s_{rated} = 0.04$
- Load: constant torque, $T_L = 60$ N┬Ęm
- At 30 Hz: $f = 30$ Hz
- $R_2' = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.2 \, \Omega$
- $V_{th} = 0.9 \cdot V_{ph}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rated Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rated Rotor Speed**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s_{rated}) = 1500 (1 - 0.04) = 1440 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Synchronous Speed at 30 Hz**

$$N_{s,30} = \frac{120 \times 30}{4} = 900 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Phase Voltage and Thevenin Voltage**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{th} = 0.9 \times 230.94 = 207.85 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Synchronous Angular Speed at 30 Hz**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 30}{2} = 30\pi = 94.248 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Slip at 30 Hz with Constant-Torque Load**

For a constant-torque load, the motor torque must equal $T_L = 60$ N┬Ęm at all speeds. Since torque is approximately proportional to slip in the small-slip region:

$$\frac{T_{30}}{T_{rated}} = \frac{s_{30}}{s_{rated}} \quad (\text{for constant flux and small slip})$$

Assuming rated torque at rated slip is approximately the load torque:

$$s_{30} \approx s_{rated} \cdot \frac{f_{rated}}{f_{30}} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{th,30}}{V_{th,rated}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{f_{30}}{f_{rated}}$$

With V/f constant, $V_{th,30}/V_{th,rated} = 30/50 = 0.6$, so $V_{th,30}^2 / V_{th,rated}^2 = 0.36$.

But more directly ŌĆö since the motor is **already operating near small slip**, and torque at constant flux is roughly linear in $s$, we approximate:

$$s_{30} \approx s_{rated} \cdot \frac{f_{rated}}{f_{30}} = 0.04 \times \frac{50}{30} = 0.0667$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Rotor Angular Speed at 30 Hz**

$$\omega_r = \omega_s (1 - s) = 94.248 \times (1 - 0.0667) = 87.96 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_r = \frac{60 \times \omega_r}{2\pi} = 840 \text{ RPM (approx.)}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Air-Gap Power**

$$P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s = 60 \times 94.248 = 5654.9 \text{ W} \approx 5.65 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Rotor Copper Loss**

$$P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.0667 \times 5654.9 = 377.18 \text{ W} \approx 377 \text{ W}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Mechanical Power Output**

$$P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag} = 0.9333 \times 5654.9 = 5277.7 \text{ W} \approx 5.28 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum torque and the corresponding slip of a 3-phase induction motor. Show that at low slips the torque-slip characteristic is approximately linear. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the constant V/f operation with a voltage source inverter. With reference to the inverter output, derive the relation between the modulation index and the fundamental RMS line voltage. Sketch the open-loop V/f profile and label the constant-torque, constant-power, and field-weakening regions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Maximum Torque

**Step 1 ŌĆö Thevenin Equivalent**

From the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator, the Thevenin impedance looking back from the rotor branch is:

$$Z_{th} = \frac{j X_m (R_1 + j X_1)}{R_1 + j (X_1 + X_m)} = R_{th} + j X_{th}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Thevenin Voltage**

$$V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \frac{j X_m}{R_1 + j (X_1 + X_m)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Air-Gap Power and Torque**

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$

$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximization**

Let $a = R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2$. The torque can be rewritten by letting $u = R_2'/s$:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{u}{(R_{th} + u)^2 + a - R_{th}^2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $u$ and setting to zero gives the optimum at:

$$u = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + a} = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2} = \sqrt{2 R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}$$

Wait ŌĆö redoing cleanly: The condition $\partial T_e / \partial s = 0$ yields:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$

Substituting back into the torque expression:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Approximation for Small Slip (Linear Region)**

For small slip, $(R_2'/s) \gg (X_{th} + X_2')$, so the denominator simplifies to $(R_2'/s)^2$. Hence:

$$T_e \approx \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 s}{R_2'} = K \cdot s$$

where $K = 3 V_{th}^2 / (\omega_s R_2')$ is approximately constant in the operating region. This shows the torque-slip characteristic is **linear near synchronous speed**.

**Critical observations to write:**
- $T_{max}$ is **independent of $R_2'$** but $s_{max}$ is **proportional to $R_2'$**.
- $T_{max} \propto 1/\omega_s \propto 1/f$ (when V is fixed).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö VSI Constant V/f Operation

**Hardware Setup:** Diode rectifier ŌåÆ DC-link filter capacitor (provides stiff DC voltage $V_{dc}$) ŌåÆ 2-level 3-phase VSI (6 IGBTs + 6 anti-parallel diodes) ŌåÆ 3-phase induction motor.

**Sinusoidal PWM Modulation:**

- The reference signals are three balanced sinusoids: $v_a^* = m_a \sin(\omega t)$, $v_b^* = m_a \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$, $v_c^* = m_a \sin(\omega t + 120┬░)$.
- A high-frequency triangular carrier $v_{tri}$ is compared with each reference.
- The switching function of leg A:
  - $S_1$ ON, $S_4$ OFF when $v_a^* > v_{tri}$
  - $S_1$ OFF, $S_4$ ON when $v_a^* < v_{tri}$
- This produces a PWM phase voltage with fundamental component:

$$v_{A,1}(t) = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

- The RMS value of the fundamental phase voltage:

$$V_{ph,1}^{rms} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

- The RMS line-to-line fundamental voltage:

$$V_{LL,1}^{rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1}^{rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \, m_a \, V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{\sqrt{6}} \cdot \sqrt{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3} m_a V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

Or more commonly written as:

$$\boxed{V_{LL,1}^{rms} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}}$$

**V/f Realization:** A coordinated control law $m_a = (V_{rated} / f_{rated}) \cdot f / V_{dc}$ is enforced. As $f$ is commanded, $m_a$ is automatically scaled to maintain $V/f$ constant.

**V/f Profile:**

```
V
^
|          V = Vrated _______________
|                              /
|                            /    Field Weakening Region
|                          /        (Constant Power)
|                        /
|                      /
| Linear V/    /     Constant Torque Region
| f region  /
|         /
|        /   V = (Vrated/frated).f
|       /
|      / Vboost
|    _/__________________________ f
    0  fmin   frated            fmax
```

**Regions labeled:**
- **Boost region** (0 to $f_{min}$): voltage boost compensates for stator drop.
- **Linear V/f region** ($f_{min}$ to $f_{rated}$): constant flux, constant torque.
- **Field-weakening region** ($f_{rated}$ to $f_{max}$): V fixed at $V_{rated}$, flux falls, constant power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units of slip** ŌĆö slip is *dimensionless* (pu). Writing it as a percentage without "pu" costs a mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $N_s$ formulas** ŌĆö The formula $N_s = 120f/P$ gives RPM; $\omega_s = 4\pi f / P$ gives rad/s. Students often confuse $P$ with pole-pairs.
> 3. **Writing $T \propto 1/f$ instead of $T_{max} \propto 1/f$** when voltage is held constant ŌĆö the proportionality applies to **maximum torque**, not the operating torque at a fixed slip.
> 4. **Neglecting rotor copper loss** in numerical problems ŌĆö at higher slips, $P_{rcu} = s P_{ag}$ becomes significant (Ōēł 7% of $P_{ag}$ for s = 0.067).
> 5. **V/f boost region** ŌĆö many students ignore the boost term in numerical answers, leading to incorrect flux estimation below 10 Hz.
> 6. **Forgetting that the synchronous speed in RPM uses $P$ (total poles), not $P/2$ (pole pairs).**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP ŌĆö Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ŌŁÉ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$ (RPM); $\omega_s = 4\pi f/P$ (rad/s) ŌĆö **always** remember $P$ is total pole count.
- ŌŁÉ **The cornerstone equation:** $\phi_g \approx V / (4.44 f N_1 K_{w1})$ ŌĆö flux collapses or saturates if V/f is not maintained.
- ŌŁÉ **Below base speed:** V/f held constant ŌåÆ rated flux ŌåÆ constant rated torque available.
- ŌŁÉ **Above base speed:** V fixed at $V_{rated}$, f increased ŌåÆ flux falls ŌåÆ constant power, torque falls as 1/f.
- ŌŁÉ **Maximum torque** is **independent of rotor resistance**, but **slip at max torque is proportional** to $R_2'$.
- ŌŁÉ **Voltage boost** is mandatory below ~10 Hz to overcome $I_1 R_1$ stator drop.
- ŌŁÉ **VSI output RMS voltage** for SPWM: $V_{LL,1} = 0.612 \, m_a V_{dc}$ (linear region $m_a \le 1$).
- ŌŁÉ **Switching frequency** of carrier ($f_c$) is typically 2ŌĆō20 kHz ŌĆö higher means lower harmonics but higher switching loss.
- ŌŁÉ **Six-step (square-wave) operation** is used above ~50 Hz; **PWM** is used at lower frequencies for harmonic quality.
- ŌŁÉ **Real-world energy savings:** For a fan load, a 20% speed reduction Ōēł 50% power saving (affinity law: $P \propto N^3$).
- ŌŁÉ **No slip rings / brushes** in squirrel-cage motor ŌĆö this is why V/f + VSI is the most reliable industrial drive.
- ŌŁÉ **KTU 2024 hot topics:** Derivation of $T_{max}$, VSI PWM mathematics, numerical on synchronous speed and rotor copper loss, V/f profile sketch.
- ŌŁÉ **Common industrial applications:** Pumps, fans, conveyors, elevators, textile spindles, HVAC blowers, EV auxiliaries.
- ŌŁÉ **Disadvantage to mention in answers:** V/f control is *open-loop* ŌĆö speed accuracy is limited by slip (no load to full load variation). For high-performance applications, vector control (Module 5 territory) is preferred.
- ŌŁÉ **Key KTU buzz-words to use in exam answers:** *Scalar control*, *Volts-per-Hertz law*, *Sub-synchronous operation*, *Thevenin equivalent*, *Modulation index*, *Six-step inverter*, *Sinusoidal PWM*, *Anti-parallel freewheeling diode*, *DC-link ripple current*, *Affinity laws*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of PWM for Induction Motor Drive, Vector Control Techniques - Concepts of Direct Torque Control (DTC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of PWM for Induction Motor Drive, Vector Control Techniques - Concepts of Direct Torque Control (DTC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) for Induction Motor Drives

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a power electronic switching technique in which the DC link voltage of a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) is chopped into a series of variable-width pulses such that the fundamental component of the output AC waveform closely approximates a desired sinusoidal reference, while the unwanted harmonics are pushed to higher frequencies for easy filtering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For induction motor drives, PWM is the *bridge* between the controller (which is a low-power digital/analog system) and the motor (which is a high-power electromechanical system). The PWM signals gate the IGBTs of a 2-level or 3-level VSI supplying the stator.

**Types of PWM relevant to KTU Module 4:**

| PWM Technique | Key Feature | Use in Drives |
|---|---|---|
| **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** | Reference is a sine wave compared with triangular carrier | Low/medium power IM drives |
| **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** | Treats inverter as 6 switching vectors; higher DC bus utilization (Ōēł 15.5 % more) | Vector-controlled IM drives |
| **Hysteresis (Bang-Bang) PWM** | Current/flux/torque maintained inside a tolerance band | DTC of IM |

### 1.2 Vector Control (Field Oriented Control ŌĆō FOC)

**Definition:**
Vector control, also called **Field Oriented Control (FOC)**, is a high-performance control strategy for AC machines in which the stator currents are represented as a two-component space vector and decoupled into a **flux-producing component (id)** and a **torque-producing component (iq)**. The decoupling is performed by aligning the rotating d-q reference frame with the rotor flux vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "DC Machine inside an AC Machine":**
> Imagine you are driving a DC motor. You have two independent handles: one controls the field (flux) and the other controls the armature (torque). You can change torque without disturbing the field. Vector control gives the **induction motor the same two independent handles** by mathematically transforming its 3-phase stator currents. The inverter's PWM stage then *physically realizes* what the d-q transformation *mathematically commanded*. So PWM + Vector Control = "DC-like control of an AC motor".

### 1.3 Direct Torque Control (DTC)

**Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Tone):**
Direct Torque Control (DTC) is a variable-structure, sensorless (in its classical form) torque and flux control strategy for AC drives proposed independently by **M. Depenbrock (1985, Germany)** and **I. Takahashi & T. Noguchi (1986, Japan)**. It directly selects the inverter switching states from a pre-defined **switching table** based on the *errors* in stator flux magnitude and electromagnetic torque, generated by two-level and three-level **hysteresis comparators** respectively, together with the angular sector of the stator flux vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Direct"?** Unlike FOC, DTC does **not** use a current controller, PWM modulator, or coordinate rotation. The torque and flux are regulated *directly* by the choice of voltage vector ŌĆö hence the name.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Driving a Car with Two Switches":**
Think of the inverter as a gearbox with only 6 (or 7, including zero) gear positions. Two dashboard meters tell you: (1) "Are we maintaining the right magnetic field strength?" (flux error) and (2) "Are we maintaining the right turning force?" (torque error). A co-driver (the switching table) instantly selects the correct gear to keep both meters in the green zone. No gradual accelerator ŌĆö just crisp gear shifts. This is DTC.

**Key Physical Quantities in DTC:**

- **Stator Flux Linkage (Žłs):** magnitude kept within a hysteresis band $\Delta \psi_s$ around reference $\psi_s^*$. Standard metric: **1 Wb (rated)**.
- **Electromagnetic Torque (Te):** magnitude kept within a hysteresis band $\Delta T_e$ around reference $T_e^*$. Standard metric: **rated torque in N┬Ęm**.
- **Stator Flux Angular Sector:** 1 to 6, used to select the optimal voltage vector.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) ŌĆö Applied to IM Drive

A two-level three-phase VSI has **8 possible switching states** ŌåÆ **8 voltage space vectors**: 6 active vectors ($V_1$ to $V_6$) at the corners of a regular hexagon, and 2 zero vectors ($V_0$, $V_7$) at the origin.

The reference stator voltage vector $V_s^*$ is synthesized in any sector (say Sector 1, between $V_1$ and $V_2$) as:

$$V_s^* \cdot T_s = V_1 \cdot T_1 + V_2 \cdot T_2 + V_0 \cdot T_0$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period, $T_1$ and $T_2$ are dwell times of the adjacent active vectors, and $T_0$ is the zero-vector dwell time, with $T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = T_s$.

**Duty times (Sector 1, magnitude modulation index m):**

$$T_1 = m \cdot T_s \cdot \sin\!\left(60^\circ - \theta\right)$$

$$T_2 = m \cdot T_s \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where $m = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{\vert V_s^* \vert}{V_{dc}}$ and $\theta$ is the angle of $V_s^*$ within the sector.

**DC Bus Utilization of SVPWM vs SPWM:**
- SPWM: maximum linear fundamental peak phase voltage = $V_{dc}/2$ ŌåÆ line voltage = $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc}$.
- SVPWM: maximum fundamental peak phase voltage = $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ ŌåÆ **Ōēł 15.47 % higher** than SPWM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SVPWM dominates IM drives:** Higher DC bus utilization means the inverter can deliver a higher voltage to the motor at the same DC link, extending the constant-torque region without raising the DC link.

### 2.2 Field Oriented Control ŌĆö FOC (Quick Theoretical Bridge to DTC)

In the synchronously rotating d-q frame aligned with rotor flux ($\psi_r$), the induction motor equations simplify to:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot \psi_r \cdot i_{qs}$$

The rotor flux is controlled by $i_{ds}$ (decoupled from torque), and torque is controlled by $i_{qs}$ (decoupled from flux). FOC needs:
1. Rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ (via encoder/sensor or estimator) ŌĆö **slip calculation (indirect FOC)**.
2. Two PI current controllers.
3. An SVPWM modulator.
4. Coordinate transformations (Clarke + Park).

**FOC Limitation that led to DTC:** Coordinate transformations, current controllers, PWM modulator, and rotor position/speed sensor ŌåÆ complex structure, parameter-sensitive (rotor time constant varies with temperature).

### 2.3 Direct Torque Control (DTC) ŌĆö Operating Principle

**Stator Voltage Equation in Stationary ╬▒-╬▓ Frame:**

$$\vec{v}_s = R_s \vec{i}_s + \frac{d\vec{\psi}_s}{dt}$$

Neglecting $R_s \vec{i}_s$ for a short sampling interval $T_s$:

$$\Delta \vec{\psi}_s \approx \vec{v}_s \cdot T_s$$

This is the **heart of DTC**: the *change* in stator flux vector is *directly proportional* to the *applied voltage vector*. Hence, by choosing the right voltage vector, we directly push the stator flux tip in the desired direction.

**Stator Flux Estimator (╬▒-╬▓ model):**

$$\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt$$

**Electromagnetic Torque Estimator:**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\psi_{\alpha s}\, i_{\beta s} - \psi_{\beta s}\, i_{\alpha s})$$

**Hysteresis Comparators (the "two switches"):**
- Flux comparator: 2-level ŌåÆ output $d_{\psi} \in \{+1, -1\}$.
- Torque comparator: 3-level ŌåÆ output $d_T \in \{+1, 0, -1\}$.

**Switching Table (canonical 6-sector DTC):**

| Sector (N) | $d_{\psi}=+1$, $d_T=+1$ | $d_{\psi}=+1$, $d_T=0$ | $d_{\psi}=+1$, $d_T=-1$ | $d_{\psi}=-1$, $d_T=+1$ | $d_{\psi}=-1$, $d_T=0$ | $d_{\psi}=-1$, $d_T=-1$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (0ŌĆō60┬░) | V2 | V7 | V6 | V3 | V0 | V5 |
| 2 (60ŌĆō120┬░) | V3 | V0 | V1 | V4 | V7 | V6 |
| 3 (120ŌĆō180┬░) | V4 | V7 | V2 | V5 | V0 | V1 |
| 4 (180ŌĆō240┬░) | V5 | V0 | V3 | V6 | V7 | V2 |
| 5 (240ŌĆō300┬░) | V6 | V7 | V4 | V1 | V0 | V3 |
| 6 (300ŌĆō360┬░) | V1 | V0 | V5 | V2 | V7 | V4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic:** An active vector with flux component *in the same direction as flux rotation* increases flux magnitude; a vector with flux component *opposite* decreases it. A vector with torque component *perpendicular and forward* to the flux increases torque; *backward* decreases it. Zero vectors (V0, V7) freeze both flux and torque (natural free-wheeling).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Utility | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $V_s^* \cdot T_s = V_k T_k + V_{k+1} T_{k+1} + V_0 T_0$ | SVPWM volt-second balance | V┬Ęs |
| 2 | $m = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{\vert V_s^* \vert}{V_{dc}}$ | SVPWM modulation index (0 to $\approx 1.15$ in over-modulation) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt$ | Stator flux estimation in ╬▒-╬▓ frame | Wb |
| 4 | $T_e = \frac{3 P}{4} (\psi_{\alpha s} i_{\beta s} - \psi_{\beta s} i_{\alpha s})$ | Torque estimation in ╬▒-╬▓ frame | N┬Ęm |
| 5 | $T_e = \frac{3 P}{4} \frac{L_m}{L_r \cdot \sigma} \vert \psi_s \vert \vert \psi_r \vert \sin(\delta)$ | Torque = flux ├Ś flux ├Ś sin(load angle) ŌĆö key insight of DTC | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d \vert \psi_s \vert}{dt} \approx v_{s\parallel}$ | Flux magnitude is changed by the *in-phase* component of stator voltage | Wb/s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d T_e}{dt} \approx \frac{3 P}{4} \frac{L_m}{\sigma L_s L_r} \vert \psi_s \vert v_{s\perp}$ | Torque is changed by the *perpendicular* component of stator voltage | N┬Ęm/s |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\psi_{\beta s}}{\psi_{\alpha s}}\right)$ | Stator flux angle ŌåÆ selects the sector N (1ŌĆ”6) | rad / ┬░ |
| 9 | $V_{dc} \cdot$ SVPWM gain $= \sqrt{3}/2$ | Maximum linear line-to-line RMS = $0.707\,V_{dc}$ | V |
| 10 | $f_{sw,\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{1}{4} \left(\dfrac{V_{dc}}{\Delta \psi}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{T_s}\right)$ | DTC average switching frequency relation (qualitative) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Industry:** SVPWM is the de-facto industrial standard for FOC drives (Siemens, ABB, Yaskawa, Schneider). DTC in its classical form is the trademarked scheme of **ABB ACS series** ("Direct Torque Control" is also offered by other vendors such as VACON, now Danfoss Drives, in the form of "motor control software" without the trademark). Both are used in elevators, electric vehicles, traction, and high-performance servo drives.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SVPWM Dwell Times in Sector 1

**Starting Point:** The reference voltage vector $\vec{V}_s^*$ lies in Sector 1 (between $\vec{V}_1 = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} e^{j0}$ and $\vec{V}_2 = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} e^{j\pi/3}$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Volt-second balance over one sampling period $T_s$:**

$$\vec{V}_s^* \cdot T_s = \vec{V}_1 \cdot T_1 + \vec{V}_2 \cdot T_2 + \vec{V}_0 \cdot T_0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decompose along the real (╬▒) and imaginary (╬▓) axes:**

$$\text{Real: } \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \cos\theta \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \cos(60^\circ) \cdot T_2$$

$$\text{Imag: } \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \sin\theta \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \sin(60^\circ) \cdot T_2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the imaginary-axis equation for $T_2$:**

$$T_2 = \frac{\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \sin\theta \cdot T_s}{\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \cdot T_s \cdot \sin\theta}{V_{dc}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $m = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert}{V_{dc}}$:**

$$T_2 = m \cdot T_s \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $T_2$ in the real-axis equation and solve for $T_1$:**

$$\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \cos\theta \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 + \dfrac{1}{3} V_{dc} T_2$$

$$\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 = \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \cos\theta \cdot T_s - \dfrac{1}{3} V_{dc} \left( m T_s \sin\theta \right)$$

Using $m V_{dc} = \sqrt{3} \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert$:

$$\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 = \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s \cos\theta - \dfrac{1}{3} V_{dc} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s \sin\theta}{V_{dc}}$$

$$\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 = \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s \left(\cos\theta - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \sin\theta\right) = \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s \left(\cos\theta - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \sin\theta\right)$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{3}/\sqrt{3}$ and applying the identity $\cos\theta - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\sin\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\sin(60^\circ - \theta)$:

$$\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 = \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sin(60^\circ - \theta)$$

$$T_1 = \frac{3 \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s}{2 V_{dc}} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sin(60^\circ - \theta) = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert T_s}{V_{dc}} \sin(60^\circ - \theta) = m T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final compact form for Sector 1:**

$$\boxed{\;T_1 = m T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta),\quad T_2 = m T_s \sin\theta,\quad T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** For $m = 1$ and $\theta = 30^\circ$ (mid-sector), $T_1 = T_2 = 0.5\,T_s$ and $T_0 = 0$. This corresponds to the **maximum linear modulation** point of SVPWM where the reference vector just touches the hexagon.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stator Flux Dynamics in DTC

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the stator voltage equation in the ╬▒-╬▓ stationary frame:**

$$\vec{v}_s = R_s \vec{i}_s + \frac{d\vec{\psi}_s}{dt}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rearrange for the rate of change of stator flux:**

$$\frac{d\vec{\psi}_s}{dt} = \vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Discretize over one sampling period $T_s$ (forward Euler):**

$$\vec{\psi}_s(k+1) = \vec{\psi}_s(k) + (\vec{v}_s(k) - R_s \vec{i}_s(k)) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Neglect the resistive drop (valid at medium/high speed, $T_s$ small):**

$$\vec{\psi}_s(k+1) \approx \vec{\psi}_s(k) + \vec{v}_s(k) \cdot T_s$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation:**

- The stator flux vector moves along a straight line in the direction of the applied voltage vector.
- The distance moved is proportional to the magnitude of $\vec{v}_s$ and the duration $T_s$.
- Choosing $\vec{V}_2$ when flux is in Sector 1 ŌåÆ flux magnitude *increases* (vector pushes it outward) and torque *increases* (vector has a component perpendicular and forward to the flux).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sector Identification:**

$$\theta_s = \text{atan2}(\psi_{\beta s},\, \psi_{\alpha s}) \cdot \frac{180^\circ}{\pi}$$

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{\theta_s}{60^\circ} \right\rceil \pmod 6 \;\; \text{(with 0 mapped to 6)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FOC vs DTC

| Feature | FOC (Vector Control) | DTC |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference frame** | Rotating d-q (synchronous) | Stationary ╬▒-╬▓ |
| **Coordinate transform** | Clarke + Park required | None |
| **Current control** | Two PI current controllers | None (direct voltage selection) |
| **Modulator** | SVPWM mandatory | None ŌĆö uses switching table |
| **Position sensor** | Required (indirect) / not required (direct, with estimator) | Not required (sensorless in classical form) |
| **Speed sensor** | Usually required | Not required at low speed if flux is well estimated |
| **Torque dynamic response** | Limited by current loop bandwidth (~1ŌĆō2 ms) | Very fast (no inner loop, ~10ŌĆō50 ┬Ąs) |
| **Switching frequency** | Fixed (set by SVPWM carrier) | Variable (depends on hysteresis bands and load) |
| **Torque ripple** | Low and smooth | High (~7ŌĆō10 % of rated in classical DTC) |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | Sensitive to $R_r$, $L_m$ (slip gain) | Sensitive to $R_s$ (stator resistance) |
| **Complexity** | High (DSP intensive) | Moderate (fast look-up table) |
| **Industry leader** | Siemens, Yaskawa, Schneider | ABB (trademarked) |
| **Startup at low speed** | Good (with encoder) | Poor in classical DTC (improved with SVM-DTC) |

### 3.4 Switching Frequency Estimation in Classical DTC

Approximate average switching frequency of one IGBT in classical DTC:

$$f_{sw} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{4 \cdot \Delta \psi_s} \cdot \frac{1}{T_{sample}}$$

This shows that the switching frequency is *not* fixed ŌĆö it is a function of DC link voltage, hysteresis flux band $\Delta \psi_s$, and the sampling period of the digital controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** Widening the flux hysteresis band $\Delta \psi_s$ reduces switching frequency (lower losses) but increases flux ripple and torque ripple. Narrowing the band does the opposite. The torque band $\Delta T_e$ is set analogously.
