## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Classical DTC of Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC[DC LINK Vdc] --> INV[VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER 2 LEVEL VSI]
    INV --> IM[INDUCTION MOTOR]
    IM --> IS[STATOR CURRENT SENSOR ia ib]
    IM --> WS[SHAFT SPEED SENSOR OPTIONAL]
    IS --> ABC2ALBE[CLARKE TRANSFORM abc TO alphabeta]
    ABC2ALBE --> FEST[STATOR FLUX ESTIMATOR]
    ABC2ALBE --> TEST[TORQUE ESTIMATOR]
    DC --> ABC2ALBE
    FEST --> COMP1[FLUX HYSTERESIS 2 LEVEL]
    TEST --> COMP2[TORQUE HYSTERESIS 3 LEVEL]
    FEST --> SECT[FLUX SECTOR N 1 to 6]
    TREF[TORQUE REF Te star] --> COMP2
    FREF[FLUX REF psi star] --> COMP1
    COMP1 --> LUT[SWITCHING LOOK UP TABLE]
    COMP2 --> LUT
    SECT --> LUT
    LUT --> GATE[GATING SIGNALS Sa Sb Sc]
    GATE --> INV
    WS --> SPD[SPEED ESTIMATOR OPTIONAL]
    SPD --> SPREG[SPEED PI REGULATOR]
    SPREG --> TREF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The red-shaded (high-level control) and blue-shaded (estimator) blocks form the "brain". The brown-shaded power block (VSI + Motor) is the "muscle". Notice the **absence of a PWM modulator** in DTC ŌĆö the LUT directly drives the inverter.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö FOC (Indirect) with SVPWM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WREF[SPEED REF omega star] --> SPI[SPEED PI CONTROLLER]
    SPI --> TREF[TORQUE REF Te star]
    TREF --> DECO[DECOUPLING BLOCK]
    PSI[FLUX REF psi star] --> DECO
    DECO --> IDQREF[id star AND iq star]
    IDQREF --> PICURR[PI CURRENT CONTROLLERS id AND iq]
    PICURR --> INVPARK[INVERSE PARK TRANSFORM]
    THETA[ROTOR FLUX ANGLE theta e] --> INVPARK
    THETA --> PARK[PARK TRANSFORM]
    IS[STATOR CURRENTS ia ib] --> CLARKE[CLARKE TRANSFORM]
    CLARKE --> PARK
    PARK --> IDQMEAS[id AND iq MEASURED]
    IDQMEAS --> PICURR
    INVPARK --> VALPHABETA[VALPHA Vbeta REFERENCE]
    VALPHABETA --> SVPWM[SVPWM MODULATOR]
    SVPWM --> GATE[GATING SIGNALS Sa Sb Sc]
    GATE --> INV[VSI INVERTER]
    INV --> IM[INDUCTION MOTOR]
    IM --> IS
    IM --> WS[SPEED SENSOR]
    WS --> SPI
    WS --> THETA
    SLIP[SLIP CALCULATOR] --> THETA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Compare with the DTC diagram. FOC has *three* closed loops (speed, d-current, q-current) and two coordinate transformations. DTC has *one* open-loop (torque and flux regulated by hysteresis, not PI). This is the visual essence of why DTC has faster torque response.

### 4.3 SVPWM Sector Diagram ŌĆö Voltage Vector Hexagon

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HEX[STATOR VOLTAGE VECTOR HEXAGON]
        SECT1[SECTOR 1 0 to 60 DEG]
        SECT2[SECTOR 2 60 to 120 DEG]
        SECT3[SECTOR 3 120 to 180 DEG]
        SECT4[SECTOR 4 180 to 240 DEG]
        SECT5[SECTOR 5 240 to 300 DEG]
        SECT6[SECTOR 6 300 to 360 DEG]
    end
    V0[V0 ZERO VECTOR 000]
    V1[V1 ACTIVE 100]
    V2[V2 ACTIVE 110]
    V3[V3 ACTIVE 010]
    V4[V4 ACTIVE 011]
    V5[V5 ACTIVE 001]
    V6[V6 ACTIVE 101]
    V7[V7 ZERO VECTOR 111]
    V1 --- V2
    V2 --- V3
    V3 --- V4
    V4 --- V5
    V5 --- V6
    V6 --- V1
    SECT1 -. uses .-> V1
    SECT1 -. uses .-> V2
    SECT2 -. uses .-> V2
    SECT2 -. uses .-> V3
    SECT3 -. uses .-> V3
    SECT3 -. uses .-> V4
    SECT4 -. uses .-> V4
    SECT4 -. uses .-> V5
    SECT5 -. uses .-> V5
    SECT5 -. uses .-> V6
    SECT6 -. uses .-> V6
    SECT6 -. uses .-> V1
    VREF[REF VECTOR V star] -. synthesized by .-> SECT1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Each sector of the hexagon is synthesized by the **two adjacent active vectors** plus a **zero vector**. The maximum reachable *inscribed circle* radius is $\dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$; the *hexagon* radius is $\dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{3}$. Linear modulation is limited to the inscribed circle; over-modulation (1.0 < m < 1.15) traces the hexagonal boundary; square-wave mode (m = 1.15) uses only the 6 active vectors.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
*Compare SPWM and SVPWM techniques used in induction motor drives in terms of DC bus utilization and harmonic performance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- SPWM uses sinusoidal reference compared with a triangular carrier. The maximum peak fundamental phase voltage that can be obtained in the linear region is $V_{dc}/2$, giving a line-to-line RMS of $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2}}\,V_{dc} \approx 0.612\,V_{dc}$. **(1 mark)**
- SVPWM treats the inverter as a six-step voltage source and synthesizes the reference by the two adjacent active vectors and a zero vector. The maximum fundamental phase voltage is $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$, giving a line-to-line RMS of $\dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_{dc}$. **(1 mark)**
- Hence, SVPWM provides **15.47 %** higher DC bus utilization than SPWM, and the current harmonic distortion (THD) is lower at the same switching frequency. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]**
*List the key functional differences between Field Oriented Control (FOC) and Direct Torque Control (DTC) of an induction motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FOC | DTC |
|---|---|---|
| Inner control loop | Two PI current controllers | Two hysteresis comparators |
| PWM stage | SVPWM modulator required | Switching table, no modulator |
| Torque dynamic response | Limited by current-loop bandwidth | Very fast (direct) |
| Coordinate transform | Required (Clarke + Park) | Not required |
| Torque ripple | Low | High |
| Parameter sensitivity | $R_r$, $L_m$ (rotor side) | $R_s$ (stator side) |

*(Any 3 valid points ŌĆö 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Module 4 Internal Choice Style]**

**Question A:**
*(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) for a two-level three-phase VSI feeding an induction motor drive. Derive the dwell-time expressions for Sector 1. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With the help of a switching table, explain the operating principle of Direct Torque Control (DTC) of an induction motor. State the merits and demerits of DTC over FOC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and dwell-time derivation ŌĆö 7 marks**

A two-level VSI has 8 switching states producing 6 active voltage vectors ($V_1$ŌĆō$V_6$, magnitude $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc}$) and 2 zero vectors ($V_0$, $V_7$). The reference vector $\vec{V}_s^*$ in any sector is synthesized as the time-average of the two adjacent active vectors and a zero vector:

$$\vec{V}_s^* \cdot T_s = \vec{V}_k \cdot T_k + \vec{V}_{k+1} \cdot T_{k+1} + \vec{V}_0 \cdot T_0$$

with $T_k + T_{k+1} + T_0 = T_s$. **(1 mark)**

For Sector 1, $V_k = V_1$ (at 0┬░) and $V_{k+1} = V_2$ (at 60┬░). Resolving along ╬▒ and ╬▓:

- ╬▒-axis: $\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \cos\theta \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} T_1 + \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \cos 60^\circ \cdot T_2$ **(1 mark)**
- ╬▓-axis: $\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert \sin\theta \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{dc} \sin 60^\circ \cdot T_2$ **(1 mark)**

Solving:

$$T_2 = m T_s \sin\theta, \qquad T_1 = m T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta)$$

where $m = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert}{V_{dc}}$. **(2 marks)**

The zero-vector time is $T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$, and it is distributed symmetrically as $V_0$ and $V_7$ to minimize switching losses (symmetric SVPWM, 7-segment sequence). **(1 mark)**

The implementation: reference $\vec{V}_s^*$ is generated from the FOC controller in ╬▒-╬▓, its angle $\theta$ is detected, sector is identified, dwell times are calculated, and the gating signals are produced by a digital timer (e.g., DSP TMS320F28xxx). **(1 mark)**

**(b) DTC ŌĆö 7 marks**

Classical DTC of an induction motor (TakahashiŌĆōNoguchi scheme) consists of: **(1 mark)**
- A 2-level VSI with 6 active and 2 zero voltage vectors.
- A stator flux estimator: $\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt$ in ╬▒-╬▓ frame. **(1 mark)**
- A torque estimator: $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} (\psi_{\alpha s} i_{\beta s} - \psi_{\beta s} i_{\alpha s})$. **(1 mark)**
- A 2-level hysteresis flux comparator (band $\Delta \psi_s$) producing $d_{\psi} \in \{+1, -1\}$. **(1 mark)**
- A 3-level hysteresis torque comparator (band $\Delta T_e$) producing $d_T \in \{+1, 0, -1\}$. **(1 mark)**
- A 6-sector identification block and a switching look-up table (as in Section 2.3). **(1 mark)**

**Merits of DTC over FOC (any 2 ŌĆö partial):**
- Very fast torque dynamic response (no inner current loop).
- No coordinate transformation, no PWM modulator, no current sensor bandwidth limit.
- Sensorless operation possible at medium/high speed.
- Simpler control structure (look-up table replaces PI tuning).

**Demerits of DTC vs FOC (any 1 ŌĆö for full 1 mark):**
- Higher torque and flux ripple (variable switching frequency).
- Higher acoustic noise and difficulty in EMC compliance.
- Poor performance at very low speed (stator resistance variation affects flux estimation).
- Higher sample-rate requirement on the DSP/FPGA.

*(If examiner expects more than 2 merits and 1 demerit for full 1 mark, expand accordingly.)* **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Module 4 Internal Choice Style]**

**Question B:**
*(a)* With a functional block diagram, describe the indirect Field Oriented Control (FOC) of a voltage-fed induction motor drive. Derive the torque expression in the synchronously rotating reference frame. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Derive the stator-flux and torque estimation equations used in Direct Torque Control (DTC). Explain how the choice of voltage vector changes the stator flux magnitude and the electromagnetic torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Indirect FOC ŌĆö 7 marks**

Block diagram: speed reference ŌåÆ speed PI ŌåÆ torque reference $T_e^*$ ŌåÆ flux reference $\psi_r^*$ (constant in base-speed region) ŌåÆ decoupling ŌåÆ $i_{ds}^*$, $i_{qs}^*$ ŌåÆ two PI current controllers ŌåÆ inverse Park ŌåÆ SVPWM ŌåÆ VSI ŌåÆ induction motor ŌåÆ current and speed sensors feed back to the controllers. **(2 marks)**

The rotor flux is aligned with the d-axis; hence $\psi_{qr} = 0$ and $\psi_{dr} = \psi_r$. **(1 mark)**

Torque in d-q frame:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot \psi_r \cdot i_{qs}$$

Since $\psi_r = \dfrac{L_m \cdot i_{ds}}{1 + T_r s}$ (rotor-flux dynamics), $i_{ds}$ controls $\psi_r$ and $i_{qs}$ controls $T_e$ **independently**. **(2 marks)**

The slip angular frequency needed for indirect FOC is $\omega_{sl} = \dfrac{L_m R_r}{L_r \psi_r} i_{qs}$, and the synchronous angle is $\theta_e = \int (\omega_{sl} + \omega_r)\, dt$. **(1 mark)**

The rotor flux angle is essential ŌĆö hence the name "indirect" ŌĆö and is synthesized from rotor speed (encoder) plus slip. **(1 mark)**

**(b) DTC derivations ŌĆö 7 marks**

Stator voltage equation in stationary ╬▒-╬▓:

$$\vec{v}_s = R_s \vec{i}_s + \dfrac{d\vec{\psi}_s}{dt}$$

Rearranging and integrating gives the stator flux estimator:

$$\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi_{\alpha s},\, \psi_{\beta s} \text{ components} \;\; \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

Torque in ╬▒-╬▓:

$$T_e = \frac{3 P}{4} (\psi_{\alpha s} i_{\beta s} - \psi_{\beta s} i_{\alpha s}) \;\; \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

The change in flux vector over one sampling period (neglecting $R_s$ drop) is:

$$\Delta \vec{\psi}_s = \vec{v}_s \cdot T_s \;\; \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Effect of voltage vector on flux magnitude and torque:**

Decompose the applied voltage vector into:
- $v_{s\parallel}$ ŌĆö component along $\vec{\psi}_s$ ŌåÆ changes flux magnitude: $\dfrac{d \vert \psi_s \vert}{dt} \approx v_{s\parallel}$ **(1 mark)**
- $v_{s\perp}$ ŌĆö component perpendicular to $\vec{\psi}_s$ ŌåÆ changes the load angle $\delta$ and therefore torque: $\dfrac{d T_e}{dt} \propto v_{s\perp} \vert \psi_s \vert$ **(1 mark)**

Hence, an active vector with a positive in-phase component **increases** the flux magnitude, an active vector with a negative in-phase component **decreases** it, a forward perpendicular component **increases** the torque, and a backward perpendicular component **decreases** it. The switching table exploits this to regulate both quantities within their respective hysteresis bands. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (DTC):** A *very common* mistake is to state the stator-flux estimator as $\vec{\psi}_s = \int \vec{v}_s\, dt$ (forgetting the $R_s \vec{i}_s$ drop). At **low speed**, the resistive drop dominates the voltage and the flux estimation fails. This costs 1 to 2 marks in Part B. Always write the full expression $\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt$ and add a one-line justification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (SVPWM):** Many students write the SVPWM dwell times as $T_1 = T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta)/2$ etc., forgetting the modulation index $m$. Always include $m$ in the final expression: $T_1 = m T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta)$. This costs 1 mark if omitted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (FOC vs DTC):** A frequent slip-up is to say DTC uses PWM. Classical DTC **does NOT** use PWM ŌĆö it uses a switching table. If the question asks for "DTC" without qualification, do not draw an SVPWM block. The SVM-DTC variant is a *different* scheme.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PWM** is the gating strategy of the inverter; SVPWM gives **15.47 %** higher DC bus utilization than SPWM.
- [x] **SVPWM dwell times (Sector 1):** $T_1 = m T_s \sin(60^\circ - \theta)$, $T_2 = m T_s \sin\theta$, $T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$, where $m = \sqrt{3}\,\vert \vec{V}_s^* \vert / V_{dc}$.
- [x] **FOC (Vector Control):** Decouples flux and torque in the rotor-flux-aligned d-q frame; needs rotor flux angle, two PI current controllers, SVPWM, and usually a speed/position sensor.
- [x] **DTC (Direct Torque Control):** Sensorless (classical), no PWM, no coordinate transform ŌĆö uses a switching table driven by **2-level flux hysteresis** and **3-level torque hysteresis**.
- [x] **DTC estimators:** $\vec{\psi}_s = \int (\vec{v}_s - R_s \vec{i}_s)\, dt$ and $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} (\psi_{\alpha s} i_{\beta s} - \psi_{\beta s} i_{\alpha s})$.
- [x] **DTC core idea:** $\Delta \vec{\psi}_s \approx \vec{v}_s T_s$ ŌĆö the *voltage vector directly moves the flux tip*.
- [x] **DTC switching table** has 6 sectors ├Ś 6 (flux, torque) combinations = 36 entries.
- [x] **Merits of DTC:** very fast torque response, simple, sensorless.
- [x] **Demerits of DTC:** variable switching frequency, high torque ripple, low-speed flux-estimation error due to $R_s$ variation.
- [x] **Industry leaders:** SVPWM + FOC ŌĆö Siemens, Yaskawa; DTC ŌĆö ABB ACS series.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** classical DTC has *no* PWM modulator; the SVM-DTC variant does ŌĆö don't confuse them in the answer script.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** always include the modulation index $m$ in SVPWM dwell-time formulas; always include the $R_s \vec{i}_s$ term in the DTC flux estimator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Oriented Control (FOC)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Field Oriented Control (FOC)

# Module 4 ŌĆö Field Oriented Control (FOC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Oriented Control (FOC)**, also universally referred to as **Vector Control**, is a sophisticated variable-frequency drive (VFD) control technique employed for three-phase AC machines (PMSM, BLDC, and Induction Motors) in which the stator phase currents are mathematically transformed into a synchronously rotating, orthogonal two-axis reference frame ($d$ŌĆō$q$ frame) aligned with the rotor flux linkage vector, thereby enabling **decoupled, independent control of machine flux and electromagnetic torque** ŌĆö mimicking the natural orthogonal control architecture of a separately excited DC machine.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Two-Knob Steering Wheel"

Imagine you are driving an automatic car. In a standard **DC motor**, the driver has two completely independent knobs:
* **Knob A (Field Current, $I_f$)** ŌåÆ Sets the magnetic field strength inside the motor (the *flux*).
* **Knob B (Armature Current, $I_a$)** ŌåÆ Sets the turning force (the *torque*).

These two knobs never interfere with each other ŌĆö turn one, the other stays put. This is why DC motors have historically produced such beautifully smooth and responsive control.

Now look at a **three-phase AC motor** (say, a PMSM). In its *natural* $a$ŌĆō$b$ŌĆō$c$ stationary frame, the three stator currents are all sinusoidally varying in time and are heavily cross-coupled by mutual inductances that change with rotor position. If you push current into phase A, it disturbs the flux of phase B, which in turn affects phase C ŌĆö the **"knobs are tangled together."** This is why naive V/Hz scalar control gives sluggish, coupled response.

**Field Oriented Control is the mathematical "untangler."** Through two consecutive coordinate transformations ŌĆö the **Clarke Transform** (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓) and the **Park Transform** (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq) ŌĆö the tangled, time-varying AC currents are converted into two clean DC-like quantities: $i_d$ (flux-producing current, *along the rotor field*) and $i_q$ (torque-producing current, *perpendicular to the rotor field*). Now we once again have **two independent DC knobs**, and high-performance dynamic control becomes possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> * Rotor electrical speed $\omega_r$ ŌĆö rad/s
> * Synchronous speed $\omega_e = P/2 \cdot \omega_r$ ŌĆö rad/s (where $P$ = number of poles)
> * Flux reference $\lambda_m$ ŌĆö Weber (Wb) ŌĆö typically a constant for PMSM surface-mount machines
> * DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ ŌĆö Volts (V)
> * Switching frequency $f_{sw}$ ŌĆö typically **5ŌĆō20 kHz** in industrial drives

### 1.2 Why FOC Matters ŌĆö Engineering Motivation

| Motor Property | Scalar V/Hz Control | Field Oriented Control (FOC) |
|---|---|---|
| Torque response | Slow, ~50ŌĆō100 ms | **Fast, ~1ŌĆō5 ms** |
| Speed regulation | ~1ŌĆō2% steady-state error | **< 0.01% with closed loop** |
| Low-speed torque | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Torque ripple | High at low speed | **Very low** |
| Dynamic stiffness | Low | **High** |
| Complexity | Simple | High (DSP/MCU intensive) |

> [!NOTE]
> FOC is the **de facto industry standard** for high-performance servo drives, EV traction motors (Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius IPM), robotics (KUKA, Fanuc), CNC spindles, and aerospace actuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Reference Frames Used in FOC

To understand FOC, you must internalize the **three reference frames** that coexist in the controller's mathematics:

1. **Three-Phase Stationary Frame (aŌĆōbŌĆōc):** The physical reality ŌĆö three sinusoidal currents offset by 120┬░ in space, attached to the stator.
2. **Two-Phase Stationary Frame ($\alpha$ŌĆō$\beta$):** A mathematical equivalent with two orthogonal (90┬░ apart) axes fixed to the stator. Achieved via **Clarke Transform**.
3. **Two-Phase Rotating Frame ($d$ŌĆō$q$):** A second mathematical equivalent with two orthogonal axes that *rotate with the rotor flux vector* at synchronous speed $\omega_e$. Achieved via **Park Transform**. In this frame, balanced sinusoidal currents become **pure DC quantities** ŌĆö this is the central trick of FOC.

### 2.2 Clarke Transformation (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓)

The Clarke Transform (also called the $\alpha\beta 0$ transform) projects the three-phase quantities onto two orthogonal stationary axes. It assumes a balanced three-phase system with no zero-sequence ($i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$).

The transformation matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \\ i_0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a balanced system, $i_0 = 0$, so we drop the zero-sequence row. The inverse Clarke Transform reconstructs the three-phase commands from $\alpha\beta$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.3 Park Transformation ($\alpha\beta$ ŌåÆ dq)

The Park Transform rotates the stationary $\alpha\beta$ axes by the rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ to align with the rotating $dq$ axes:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Inverse Park Transform (dq ŌåÆ $\alpha\beta$):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & -\sin\theta_e \\ \sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $\theta_e$ so critical?** This is the rotor flux angle (electrical). In **Direct FOC**, it is *measured or estimated* directly (via Hall sensors, encoders, or sensorless observers). In **Indirect FOC**, it is *computed* by integrating the slip frequency added to the measured rotor speed: $\theta_e = \int (\omega_r + \omega_{sl})\,dt$. A wrong $\theta_e$ means the $d$-axis is no longer aligned with the flux, and the beautiful decoupling collapses ŌĆö the controller begins *fighting itself*, producing oscillation, torque ripple, and at high speeds, instability.

### 2.4 PMSM Model in the $d$ŌĆō$q$ Rotating Frame

For a Surface-Mount PMSM (SPMSM), the per-phase equivalent voltage equations in the $dq$ frame are remarkably clean and **decoupled except for the back-EMF cross-coupling term**:

$$
v_d = R_s i_d + L_s \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_s i_q
$$

$$
v_q = R_s i_q + L_s \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_s i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m
$$

where:
* $R_s$ = stator phase resistance ($\Omega$)
* $L_s = L_d = L_q$ for SPMSM (rotor saliency neglected)
* $\lambda_m$ = permanent-magnet flux linkage (Wb)
* $\omega_e$ = electrical synchronous speed (rad/s)

> [!NOTE]
> The terms $-\omega_e L_s i_q$ and $+\omega_e L_s i_d$ are **cross-coupling terms**. They arise because the rotating frame's $d$-axis voltage is disturbed by $q$-axis current (and vice versa). The FOC controller cancels these via **feed-forward decoupling** ŌĆö subtracting $j\omega_e L_s i_q$ from the $d$-axis command and $j\omega_e (L_s i_d + \lambda_m)$ from the $q$-axis command, so the inner current loops become pure first-order LPF plants.

### 2.5 Electromagnetic Torque Equation (SPMSM)

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m \cdot i_q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observe the profound decoupling:** Torque $T_e$ depends *only* on $i_q$, and flux depends *only* on $i_d$. Setting $i_d^* = 0$ (for SPMSM, since $\lambda_m$ is the permanent magnet and cannot be weakened via $i_d$ positively) gives **maximum torque per ampere (MTPA)** operation. This is precisely the analogue of the DC machine where $T_e \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ with $\phi$ held constant.

For an Interior PMSM (IPMSM) where $L_d \neq L_q$ (reluctance torque exists):

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]
$$

For an Induction Motor (IM), the rotor flux is produced by induction (not permanent), so $i_d$ controls the rotor flux amplitude:

$$
\lambda_r = \frac{L_m}{1 + \tau_r s} \, i_d \quad (\text{where } \tau_r = L_r / R_r)
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot \lambda_r \cdot i_q
$$

### 2.6 Two Variants: Direct vs Indirect FOC

| Feature | Direct FOC (DFOC) | Indirect FOC (IFOC) |
|---|---|---|
| Flux angle $\theta_e$ | Measured/observed directly | Computed via slip relation |
| Flux sensing | Required (sensor or observer) | Not required |
| Speed sensor | Required | **Required** (for slip computation) |
| Sensitivity to parameter errors | Lower (flux is real) | Higher (slip depends on $R_r, L_m$) |
| Industry adoption | Robotics, traction | HVAC, pumps, general-purpose drives |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_e = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \omega_r$ | Electrical speed Ōåö mechanical speed | rad/s |
| 2 | $\theta_e = \int_0^t \omega_e(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Flux angle accumulation | rad |
| 3 | $v_d = R_s i_d + L_s \dot{i_d} - \omega_e L_s i_q$ | d-axis stator voltage | V |
| 4 | $v_q = R_s i_q + L_s \dot{i_q} + \omega_e L_s i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$ | q-axis stator voltage | V |
| 5 | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m i_q$ | SPMSM electromagnetic torque | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{P}{2}[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q]$ | IPMSM electromagnetic torque | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | $\lambda_r = \tfrac{L_m \, i_d}{1 + \tau_r s}$ | IM rotor flux dynamics | Wb |
| 8 | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{P}{2} \cdot \tfrac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot \lambda_r \cdot i_q$ | IM electromagnetic torque | N┬Ęm |
| 9 | $\omega_{sl} = \tfrac{R_r \, i_q}{L_r \, i_d}$ | IM slip angular frequency | rad/s |
| 10 | $V_{max} = \tfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Maximum linear-modulation phase voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize formulas (3), (4), (5), and (9). These four appear in **nearly every past KTU University Exam question** on FOC. Marks are routinely lost when students confuse the cross-coupling signs.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

FOC is the cornerstone of:
* **Electric vehicle traction** (Tesla Model S/X/3 rear unit, BMW i3, Tata Nexon EV)
* **Industrial servo drives** (Siemens SINAMICS, ABB ACS880, Yaskawa Sigma-7)
* **Robotics and CNC** (zero-velocity holding torque, precise contouring)
* **Aerospace actuators** (More Electric Aircraft ŌĆö EMA, EMA-LG)
* **HVAC compressors** (BLDC inverter compressors in modern refrigerators/AC units)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Clarke Transformation (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓)

**Starting Point ŌĆö Three-Phase Balanced Currents:**

$$
i_a = I_m \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
i_b = I_m \cos(\omega t - 120^\circ)
$$

$$
i_c = I_m \cos(\omega t + 120^\circ)
$$

The constraint for a balanced system is:

$$
i_a + i_b + i_c = 0
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the $\alpha$ŌĆō$\beta$ axes.**
The $\alpha$-axis is aligned with phase A. The $\beta$-axis is orthogonal to $\alpha$, leading by 90┬░. We project each phase onto the $\alpha$-axis using $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$, $\cos(120^\circ) = -1/2$, and project onto the $\beta$-axis using $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$, $\sin(120^\circ) = \sqrt{3}/2$ (and negated for the trailing phase).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum the projections.**

$$
i_\alpha = i_a \cos(0^\circ) + i_b \cos(120^\circ) + i_c \cos(-120^\circ)
$$

$$
i_\alpha = i_a \cdot 1 + i_b \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + i_c \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right)
$$

$$
i_\alpha = i_a - \tfrac{1}{2}i_b - \tfrac{1}{2}i_c
$$

$$
i_\beta = i_a \sin(0^\circ) + i_b \sin(120^\circ) + i_c \sin(-120^\circ)
$$

$$
i_\beta = i_b \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + i_c \cdot \left(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(i_b - i_c)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Eliminate the dependency on all three phase currents using $i_c = -(i_a + i_b)$:**

$$
i_\alpha = i_a - \tfrac{1}{2}i_b - \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(-(i_a + i_b)\bigr) = i_a - \tfrac{1}{2}i_b + \tfrac{1}{2}i_a + \tfrac{1}{2}i_b = \tfrac{3}{2}i_a
$$

Dividing by $\tfrac{3}{2}$ to preserve amplitude (amplitude-invariant form):

$$
i_\alpha = i_a
$$

$$
i_\beta = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(i_b - i_c)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write in compact matrix form (with the standard $\tfrac{2}{3}$ scaling factor):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $2/3$ factor?** It ensures amplitude preservation, i.e., a balanced 3-phase set with peak $I_m$ produces $|i_\alpha + j i_\beta| = I_m$. This is the *amplitude-invariant* form preferred in FOC. The alternative *power-invariant* form uses $\sqrt{2/3}$ but is rarely used in motor drives.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Park Transformation ($\alpha\beta$ ŌåÆ dq)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the rotating reference frame.**
The $d$-axis makes angle $\theta_e$ with the $\alpha$-axis at time $t$. The $q$-axis leads the $d$-axis by 90┬░. Any vector $\vec{I}_s$ in the $\alpha\beta$ plane can be expressed in the $dq$ plane by rotating it *backward* by $\theta_e$:

$$
\vec{I}_s = i_\alpha + j i_\beta = i_d + j i_q
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the rotation operator.**
Multiplying by $e^{-j\theta_e}$:

$$
i_d + j i_q = (i_\alpha + j i_\beta)(\cos\theta_e - j \sin\theta_e)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand by distributive multiplication:**

$$
i_d + j i_q = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e - j i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + j i_\beta \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e
$$

Grouping real and imaginary parts:

$$
i_d = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e
$$

$$
i_q = -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express in matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The inverse is obtained by setting $\theta_e \to -\theta_e$ (or transposing since the matrix is orthonormal).

### 3.3 Derivation of the PMSM Torque Equation in the $dq$ Frame

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the general electromagnetic torque from co-energy:**

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \left[ i_q \, \lambda_d - i_d \, \lambda_q \right]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the flux linkages for a PMSM:**

$$
\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_m
$$

$$
\lambda_q = L_q i_q
$$

(For SPMSM, the magnet flux $\lambda_m$ appears on the $d$-axis only.)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand:**

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ i_q(L_d i_d + \lambda_m) - i_d(L_q i_q) \right]
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ L_d i_d i_q + \lambda_m i_q - L_q i_d i_q \right]
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]
$$

For an SPMSM where $L_d = L_q = L_s$, the reluctance term $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q = 0$ and we recover:

$$
\boxed{\;T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m \, i_q\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **famous decoupling equation** of FOC. Torque is now a linear, single-input function of $i_q$. Flux linkage is independently controlled by $i_d$. This is mathematically identical in form to a separately excited DC motor: $T = K \phi I_a$. This mathematical isomorphism is the entire reason FOC exists.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Slip Frequency for Indirect FOC (Induction Motor)

**Step 1 ŌĆö In the $dq$ frame, the rotor voltage equation of an IM at steady-state (derivative = 0) yields:**

$$
0 = R_r i_{dr} + j \omega_{sl} (L_m i_d + L_r i_{dr})
$$

where $\omega_{sl} = \omega_e - \omega_r$ is the slip frequency.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $i_{dr}$:**

$$
i_{dr} = -\frac{j \omega_{sl} L_m}{R_r + j \omega_{sl} L_r} \, i_d
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The rotor flux linkage is $\lambda_r = L_m i_d + L_r i_{dr}$.** Eliminating $i_{dr}$ and taking magnitudes in steady state (where $j\omega_{sl} L_r \gg R_r$ for $\omega_{sl} > 1$ rad/s):

$$
\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r \, i_q}{L_r \, i_d} \cdot \frac{L_m}{\lambda_r}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Using $\lambda_r \approx L_m i_d$ (steady-state):**

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r \, i_q}{L_r \, i_d}\;}
$$

This is the slip relation used in the IFOC integrator to compute $\theta_e$:

$$
\theta_e = \int_0^t \bigl(\omega_r(\tau) + \omega_{sl}(\tau)\bigr)\,d\tau
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete FOC Drive (Direct FOC of PMSM)

The figure below maps the complete signal flow of a closed-loop Direct Field Oriented Control system for a 3-phase PMSM. Each labeled block performs one stage of the control computation. Note how the current feedback path is *twice-transformed* (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq) before being compared to the references.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REF[Reference Generation]
        refA[Speed Reference omega_star]
        refB[PI Speed Controller]
        refC[Q Axis Current Reference iq_star]
        refD[D Axis Current Reference id_star eq 0 for SPMSM]
    end

    subgraph FFB[Feed Forward Decoupling]
        ffbA[Cross Coupling Canceller omega_e Ls iq]
        ffbB[Back EMF Canceller omega_e lambda_m]
    end

    subgraph CC[Current Control Loops]
        ccA[PI Controller D Axis]
        ccB[PI Controller Q Axis]
        ccC[Inverse Park Transform]
        ccD[Space Vector Modulation SVM]
    end

    subgraph POW[Power Stage]
        pwrA[Three Phase VSI Inverter]
        pwrB[DC Bus Vdc]
    end

    subgraph MTR[Motor and Sensing]
        mtrA[Three Phase PMSM Stator]
        mtrB[Rotor Position Encoder]
        mtrC[Phase Current Sensors Iabc]
    end

    subgraph TX[Reference Frame Transforms]
        txA[Clarke Transform abc to alpha beta]
        txB[Park Transform alpha beta to dq]
        txC[Position to Flux Angle theta_e]
    end

    refA --> refB --> refC
    refC --> ccB
    refD --> ccA

    mtrC --> txA --> txB
    txB -->|id_meas| ccA
    txB -->|iq_meas| ccB

    mtrB --> txC --> ccC
    mtrB --> txC --> ffbA
    mtrB --> txC --> ffbB

    ffbA --> ccA
    ffbB --> ccB

    ccA --> ccC
    ccB --> ccC
    ccC --> ccD --> pwrA --> mtrA

    pwrB --> pwrA
    mtrA -->|omega_r| refB
```

**Block-Level Reading Guide for Examiners:**

| Block | Function | Typical DSP Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| Clarke Transform | 3-phase ŌåÆ 2-phase stationary | ~10 cycles |
| Park Transform | 2-phase stationary ŌåÆ rotating | ~12 cycles |
| Inverse Park | Rotating ŌåÆ stationary | ~12 cycles |
| SVPWM | Generate 6-switch PWM timing | ~30 cycles |
| PI Controllers | Track $i_d$, $i_q$ references | ~8 cycles each |
| Total loop latency | Measured end-to-end | **~50ŌĆō100 ┬Ąs** |

### 4.2 Inner Current Loop Decoupling ŌĆö Detail View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Id_star Reference] --> nodeB[Sum Node D]
    nodeC[Id Measured] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[PI Controller D Axis]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sum Node D Voltage]
    nodeF[omega_e Ls iq Feed Forward] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG[Vd Command]

    nodeH[Iq_star Reference] --> nodeI[Sum Node Q]
    nodeJ[Iq Measured] --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeK[PI Controller Q Axis]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Sum Node Q Voltage]
    nodeM[omega_e Ls id plus omega_e lambda_m Feed Forward] --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeN[Vq Command]

    nodeG --> nodeO[Inverse Park Transform]
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP[Space Vector Modulation]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Voltage Source Inverter]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Three Phase PMSM]
    nodeR --> nodeC
    nodeR --> nodeJ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how the feed-forward terms (blue arrows in the original diagrams) **cancel** the cross-coupling terms in the PMSM voltage equations (Section 2.4). This is what makes each axis a *first-order* plant $v_d = R_s i_d + L_s \dot{i_d}$ ŌĆö trivial to control with a single PI loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define Field Oriented Control. Mention any two advantages of FOC over scalar V/Hz control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Field Oriented Control (FOC), also called vector control, is a technique used to control three-phase AC machines in which the stator phase currents are transformed into a two-phase rotating reference frame ($d$ŌĆō$q$ frame) that is aligned with the rotor flux vector. This transformation produces DC-like current components $i_d$ (flux-producing) and $i_q$ (torque-producing), enabling decoupled control of machine flux and torque ŌĆö analogous to a separately excited DC motor.

**Advantages (1 mark ŌĆö any two):**
1. **Fast dynamic torque response** (~1ŌĆō5 ms vs 50ŌĆō100 ms for V/Hz) due to inner current control loop.
2. **Excellent low-speed performance** including full torque at zero speed (essential for robotics and EV hill-hold).
3. **Independent control** of flux and torque, enabling smooth MTPA and field-weakening operation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the Park transformation? State its matrix form.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Park transformation converts stationary-frame two-phase quantities ($i_\alpha, i_\beta$) into rotating-frame two-phase quantities ($i_d, i_q$), where the rotating frame is aligned with the rotor flux linkage vector at electrical angle $\theta_e$.

**Matrix form (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

In balanced sinusoidal steady state, sinusoidal $i_\alpha$ and $i_\beta$ become **constant DC values** $i_d$ and $i_q$ ŌĆö which is the central mathematical convenience exploited by FOC. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

### (a) Derive the Clarke and Park transformations used in the Field Oriented Control of a three-phase AC machine. Show how the stationary frame quantities are related to the rotating frame quantities. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**(i) Clarke Transformation (3.5 marks):**
Consider a balanced 3-phase system: $i_a = I_m \cos\omega t$, $i_b = I_m \cos(\omega t - 120┬░)$, $i_c = I_m \cos(\omega t + 120┬░)$, with the constraint $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$.

The $\alpha$-axis is chosen along phase A, and the $\beta$-axis is 90┬░ ahead in space. Projecting all three phase currents onto these two axes gives:

$$
i_\alpha = i_a - \tfrac{1}{2} i_b - \tfrac{1}{2} i_c
$$

$$
i_\beta = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(i_b - i_c)
$$

Using the constraint $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ to eliminate $i_c$, and applying amplitude scaling factor $\tfrac{2}{3}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

**(ii) Park Transformation (3.5 marks):**
Let the rotor flux vector make angle $\theta_e$ with the stationary $\alpha$-axis. To express the stator current vector in the $dq$ rotating frame, we rotate it by $-\theta_e$:

$$
i_d + j i_q = (i_\alpha + j i_\beta) e^{-j\theta_e} = (i_\alpha + j i_\beta)(\cos\theta_e - j\sin\theta_e)
$$

Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$
\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

This shows that the rotating-frame currents are obtained from the stationary-frame currents via a rotation by $\theta_e$. For balanced sinusoidal steady-state inputs, the output $i_d$ and $i_q$ are **pure DC quantities** ŌĆö the foundational property exploited by FOC for decoupled torque and flux control.

---

### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the Direct Field Oriented Control of a three-phase PMSM. Discuss the role of the inner current control loops. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the functional block diagram in **Section 4.1** above.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. **Outer Speed Loop:** A speed reference $\omega_r^*$ is compared with the measured rotor speed $\omega_r$ (from encoder). The error is fed to a PI speed controller whose output is the **torque-producing current reference $i_q^*$**.
2. **Flux Reference:** For an SPMSM, the flux-producing current reference is set as $i_d^* = 0$ (Maximum Torque Per Ampere).
3. **Inner Current Loops:** The measured phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are transformed via **Clarke + Park** transforms into $i_d$ and $i_q$ (using rotor position $\theta_e$ from the encoder). These are compared with their respective references $i_d^*$ and $i_q^*$ and the errors are processed by two **PI controllers** (one per axis).
4. **Decoupling & SVPWM:** The PI outputs are compensated by feed-forward decoupling terms ($-\omega_e L_s i_q$ in $d$-axis, $\omega_e (L_s i_d + \lambda_m)$ in $q$-axis) and then inverse-Park transformed to stationary $\alpha\beta$ voltages. **Space Vector Modulation (SVM)** generates the six PWM signals for the 3-phase VSI.
5. **Inverter ŌåÆ Motor:** The VSI applies the synthesized voltages to the PMSM stator, producing the desired $i_d$, $i_q$, flux, and torque.

**Role of Inner Current Loops:** The inner current loops provide **bandwidth an order of magnitude higher** than the outer speed loop (typically 1ŌĆō5 kHz current bandwidth vs 100ŌĆō500 Hz speed bandwidth). This decouples flux and torque dynamics, gives **instantaneous torque control** (essential for EV and servo applications), and protects the machine via hardware-level current limiting.

---

## **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Indirect Field Oriented Control (IFOC) of a three-phase Induction Motor. How is the slip frequency computed? (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
In Indirect FOC of an Induction Motor, the rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ is **not measured directly** but is *computed* by integrating the sum of the measured rotor speed $\omega_r$ and the computed slip frequency $\omega_{sl}$. Only rotor speed is sensed; rotor flux is *estimated* from the slip relation.

**Slip Frequency Derivation (2 marks):**
From the steady-state rotor voltage equation in the $dq$ frame, the slip frequency is derived as:

$$
\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \frac{i_q^*}{i_d^*}
$$

where $R_r$ is the rotor resistance and $L_r$ is the rotor inductance per phase. This relation depends on the current commands $i_q^*$ and $i_d^*$, and critically on the **rotor time constant $\tau_r = L_r / R_r$**, which is sensitive to temperature.

**Block Diagram (1 mark) ŌĆö Refer to Section 4 framework with these modifications:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    speedRef[Speed Ref omega_star] --> speedPI[PI Speed Controller]
    speedPI --> iqRef[iq_star Reference]
    idRef[id_star Flux Reference]
    iqRef --> slipCalc[Slip Calculator omega_sl eq Rr iq over Lr id]
    idRef --> slipCalc
    speedMeas[Measured Rotor Speed omega_r] --> sumNode[Summing Junction]
    slipCalc --> sumNode
    sumNode --> intBlock[Integrator]
    intBlock --> theta_e[Flux Angle theta_e]
    theta_e --> parkBlock[Park and Inverse Park Transforms]
    parkBlock --> svpwmBlock[Space Vector Modulation]
    svpwmBlock --> inverterBlock[Three Phase VSI]
    inverterBlock --> imMotor[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    imMotor --> speedMeas
    imMotor --> currentSens[Phase Current Sensors]
    currentSens --> parkBlock
```

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The slip calculator produces $\omega_{sl} = (R_r/L_r)(i_q^*/i_d^*)$ from the current references.
2. Adding $\omega_{sl}$ to the measured $\omega_r$ gives the synchronous speed $\omega_e$.
3. Integrating $\omega_e$ produces the flux angle $\theta_e$ used in all Park transforms.
4. Feedback currents are transformed and compared with references; PI control + SVPWM completes the loop.

**Critical Parameter Sensitivity:** IFOC performance degrades sharply if $R_r$ drifts from its nominal value due to rotor heating. A typical 50% error in $R_r$ can cause **flux orientation errors of 10ŌĆō20┬░**, leading to significant torque ripple. Online $R_r$ estimation or temperature-compensation models are commonly used in industrial drives.

---

### (b) Derive the electromagnetic torque equation of a PMSM in the $d$ŌĆō$q$ rotating reference frame. Show that for an SPMSM, torque is independent of $i_d$. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö General torque expression from co-energy (1 mark):**

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \left[ i_q \lambda_d - i_d \lambda_q \right]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö PMSM flux linkages in the $dq$ frame (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_m
$$

$$
\lambda_q = L_q i_q
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substituting and expanding (2 marks):**

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \bigl[ i_q(L_d i_d + \lambda_m) - i_d(L_q i_q) \bigr]
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \bigl[ L_d i_d i_q + \lambda_m i_q - L_q i_d i_q \bigr]
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \bigl[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \bigr]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Specializing to SPMSM where $L_d = L_q = L_s$ (2 marks):**
The reluctance term $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ vanishes identically. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;T_e^{SPMSM} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m \, i_q\;}
$$

This shows that for an SPMSM, the electromagnetic torque depends **only on $i_q$** and is completely **independent of $i_d$**. Setting $i_d^* = 0$ (MTPA) therefore achieves the desired torque with minimum stator current magnitude, eliminating $i^2R$ stator losses. This mathematical decoupling ŌĆö torque $\propto i_q$, flux $\propto \lambda_m$ (constant) ŌĆö is the precise analogue of a separately excited DC motor where $T \propto \phi I_a$. This is the *raison d'├¬tre* of Field Oriented Control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong sign on cross-coupling terms** in the $dq$ voltage equations. Memorize: $v_d$ has $-\omega_e L_s i_q$; $v_q$ has $+\omega_e L_s i_d$. Mixing signs causes immediate loss of 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2}$** in the torque equation. The naive answer "$\lambda_m i_q$" loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Conflating Clarke and Park transforms.** Clarke is *abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓* (still stationary). Park is *╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq* (rotating with the rotor flux). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$** in the Clarke derivation. This is worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing Direct FOC with Indirect FOC** in block diagram questions. In DFOC, the rotor flux angle is *measured* (sensor/observer). In IFOC, it is *computed* via slip integration. Mixing the two is a 3-mark penalty.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet part of the answer. KTU examiners explicitly reward SI units in tabular columns.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

* **FOC = Vector Control** ŌĆö control stator current in a rotating frame aligned with rotor flux.
* **Two transforms used:** Clarke (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓, *stationary*) and Park (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq, *rotating with $\theta_e$*).
* **Three frames:** abc (physical), ╬▒╬▓ (stationary 2-phase), dq (rotating 2-phase aligned with flux).
* **Decoupling result:** For SPMSM ŌĆö $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m i_q$ (depends only on $i_q$); flux is fixed by $\lambda_m$.
* **Inner current loops** (PI on $i_d$, $i_q$) operate at high bandwidth (1ŌĆō5 kHz) and provide instantaneous torque control.
* **Feed-forward decoupling** cancels $-\omega_e L_s i_q$ in $d$-axis and $\omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ in $q$-axis, reducing each axis to a first-order LPF plant.
* **Direct FOC:** rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ is **measured/observed** (encoder, resolver, or sensorless observer).
* **Indirect FOC:** $\theta_e = \int (\omega_r + \omega_{sl})\,dt$, where slip $\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r i_q}{L_r i_d}$ for IM.
* **SVPWM** is used (not sinusoidal PWM) because it gives ~15.5% higher DC bus utilization and lower harmonic distortion.
* **MTPA for SPMSM:** set $i_d^* = 0$ ŌĆö minimum stator current for a given torque.
* **Field weakening for high speed:** set $i_d^* < 0$ to reduce effective air-gap flux, allowing back-EMF to fit within DC bus limits.
* **KTU Hot Formulas:** Memorize the $dq$ voltage equations, the torque equation, and the slip relation. These account for ~60% of Part B marks.
* **Real-world applications:** EV traction motors (Tesla, Tata Nexon EV), industrial servos (Siemens SINAMICS), CNC spindles, robotics, and aerospace EMAs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Vector Control and Scalar Control, Application of Vector Control Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET601 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Vector Control and Scalar Control, Application of Vector Control Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scalar Control (V/f Control)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Scalar Control* is a method of controlling induction motor speed by varying the magnitude (scalar quantity) of voltage and frequency in a fixed ratio, keeping the air-gap flux constant. It operates on the **steady-state equivalent circuit** of the motor and uses only the **magnitude and frequency** of voltage/current ŌĆö not their spatial orientation or instantaneous phase.

**Mathematical Foundation of Scalar Control:**

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \quad \text{(for constant flux region below base speed)}$$

**Key Idea:** As frequency is increased, voltage is also increased proportionally so that the **air-gap flux ($\phi$)** remains nominal.

$$\phi \approx \frac{V}{f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Scalar control is also called **V/f control** or **Volts-per-Hertz control**. It is the *simplest* and most widely used open-loop speed control method for induction motors in industry (fans, pumps, conveyors).

**Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö Driving a Car with a Single Pedal:**

Imagine you are driving an automatic car on a highway. You have only **one control** ŌĆö the accelerator pedal. You press it harder, the car goes faster; you release it, the car slows down. You have **no independent control over steering angle, braking force, or wheel torque**. You are simply commanding a *scalar magnitude* (speed).

**Scalar Control works exactly like this:**
- You command a single quantity ŌåÆ **frequency (or speed)**
- The controller adjusts **voltage magnitude proportionally**
- You do **not** independently control flux and torque

This works fine for **steady-state** applications (pumps, fans), but fails when **fast, precise torque response** is needed.

---

### 1.2 Vector Control (Field Oriented Control ŌĆö FOC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Vector Control*, also known as **Field Oriented Control (FOC)**, is a technique that decouples the stator current into two orthogonal components ŌĆö the **flux-producing component ($i_d$)** and the **torque-producing component ($i_q$)** ŌĆö by aligning the rotor flux vector with the direct axis in a synchronously rotating reference frame (d-q frame). This enables **independent control of flux and torque**, similar to a separately excited DC motor.

**Mathematical Foundation of Vector Control:**

In the d-q reference frame rotating at synchronous speed $\omega_e$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$$

Where:
- $i_d$ ŌåÆ **flux component** (direct axis current)
- $i_q$ ŌåÆ **torque component** (quadrature axis current)

**Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö Driving a Car with a Steering Wheel and Accelerator:**

Now imagine driving a **Formula 1 car** on a race track. You have **two independent controls**:
1. **Steering wheel** ŌåÆ controls *direction* (this is like the **flux component $i_d$**)
2. **Accelerator pedal** ŌåÆ controls *speed/torque* (this is like the **torque component $i_q$**)

You can steer while accelerating, brake while cornering, and respond **instantaneously** to track changes. Each control action is **decoupled** and works independently.

**Vector Control works exactly like this:**
- $i_d$ is commanded **independently** to set the desired flux
- $i_q$ is commanded **independently** to set the desired torque
- The drive responds **as fast as a DC motor** ŌĆö a key reason FOC is the *gold standard* for high-performance drives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask *"Why is vector control superior to scalar control?"* The crisp answer is: **Vector control decouples flux and torque, enabling fast, independent, and precise torque response ŌĆö just like a DC motor ŌĆö while scalar control can only control magnitude, not orientation.**

---

### 1.3 The Two Control Philosophies ŌĆö A Side-by-Side First Look

| Aspect | Scalar Control | Vector Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Variable** | Magnitude only (scalar) | Magnitude + Phase (vector) |
| **Reference Frame** | Stationary (stator) | Rotating (synchronous, d-q) |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Steady-state model | Dynamic model |
| **Coupling** | Flux & Torque are coupled | Flux & Torque are decoupled |
| **Analogy** | Single-pedal car | F1 car with steering + accelerator |

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental conceptual difference is **coupling**. In scalar control, changing voltage to alter torque also alters flux (they are intertwined). In vector control, the d-q transformation mathematically *untangles* them, allowing independent (orthogonal) control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle ŌĆö Scalar Control (V/f Method)

**Step-by-Step Operation Logic:**

1. **Speed Reference ($N_{ref}$)** is given by the user.
2. The reference frequency is computed from synchronous speed: $f_{ref} = \frac{P \cdot N_{ref}}{120}$
3. The reference voltage is computed using the V/f law: $V_{ref} = k \cdot f_{ref}$ where $k = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$
4. A **PWM inverter** generates a variable-frequency, variable-voltage output.
5. This is fed to the induction motor stator.
6. The motor runs at a speed slightly less than synchronous (slip-based).

**Induction Motor EMF Equation (Per Phase):**

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w$$

Where:
- $E$ ŌåÆ RMS induced EMF per phase
- $f$ ŌåÆ Supply frequency (Hz)
- $N$ ŌåÆ Number of turns per phase
- $\phi$ ŌåÆ Air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $k_w$ ŌåÆ Winding factor

Rearranging for flux:

$$\phi = \frac{E}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w} \approx \frac{V}{f} \quad \text{(if } E \approx V \text{)}$$

**If V/f is held constant ŌåÆ $\phi$ remains constant ŌåÆ torque capability is preserved across the speed range.**

**Below Base Speed (Constant Torque Region):**
- $V/f$ = constant
- Flux = rated
- Torque = rated

**Above Base Speed (Field Weakening Region):**
- V is clamped at rated value
- Frequency increases ŌåÆ V/f decreases ŌåÆ flux weakens
- Torque decreases (constant power region)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that V/f control requires a **small voltage boost ($V_{boost}$)** at very low frequencies to overcome the stator resistance drop ($I_s R_s$). Without boost, flux collapses at low speed and torque drops to zero.

$$V_{ref}(f) = V_{boost} + k \cdot f$$

---

### 2.2 Working Principle ŌĆö Vector Control (Indirect FOC)

**Step-by-Step Operation Logic:**

1. **Speed Reference ($N_{ref}$)** is given by the user.
2. A **PI speed controller** compares $N_{ref}$ with actual speed $N$ and outputs the **torque reference $T_e^{ref}$**.
3. The torque reference is converted to a **$i_q$ reference**: $i_q^{ref} = \frac{T_e^{ref}}{K_t}$
4. The **flux reference $i_d^{ref}$** is set based on desired flux (usually rated flux below base speed).
5. **Measured three-phase stator currents** ($i_a, i_b, i_c$) are transformed:
   - **Clarke Transform (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓):** stationary ╬▒-╬▓ components
   - **Park Transform (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq):** rotating d-q components using rotor flux angle $\theta_e$
6. **Current controllers** (typically PI) regulate $i_d$ and $i_q$ independently.
7. **Inverse Park + Inverse Clarke** transforms generate reference voltages $V_a^*, V_b^*, V_c^*$.
8. **SVPWM / SVM** modulator generates inverter switching signals.

**Core Transformations Used:**

**Clarke Transformation (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓, stationary frame):**

$$i_\alpha = i_a$$

$$i_\beta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} (i_a + 2i_b)$$

**Park Transformation (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq, rotating frame at angle $\theta_e$):**

$$i_d = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e$$

$$i_q = -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e$$

**Inverse Park Transformation (dq ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓):**

$$i_\alpha = i_d \cos\theta_e - i_q \sin\theta_e$$

$$i_\beta = i_d \sin\theta_e + i_q \cos\theta_e$$

**Slip Frequency Relation (Indirect FOC):**

$$\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \frac{i_q}{i_d} = \frac{L_m \cdot i_q}{\tau_r \cdot \lambda_r}$$

**Rotor Flux Angle:**

$$\theta_e = \int (\omega_{sl} + \omega_r) \, dt$$

Where $\omega_r$ is the actual rotor electrical speed.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{V}{f} = \text{const}$ | V/f law for scalar control | V/Hz |
| 2 | $\phi \approx \frac{V}{4.44 f N k_w}$ | Air-gap flux (steady-state) | Wb |
| 3 | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot (R_r/s)}{(R_r/s)^2 + X_r^2}$ | Torque-speed eq. (scalar, approx) | N┬Ęm |
| 5 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot (\lambda_d \cdot i_q - \lambda_q \cdot i_d)$ | Electromagnetic torque in d-q | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$ | Torque (vector, $\lambda_r$ aligned with d-axis) | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | $\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r \cdot i_q}{L_r \cdot i_d}$ | Slip frequency (indirect FOC) | rad/s |
| 8 | $\theta_e = \int (\omega_{sl} + \omega_r) \, dt$ | Flux angle (indirect FOC) | rad |
| 9 | $i_d^{ref} = \frac{\lambda_{r}^{ref}}{L_m}$ | Flux-producing current reference | A |
| 10 | $i_q^{ref} = \frac{2 \cdot L_r \cdot T_e^{ref}}{3 \cdot P \cdot L_m^2 \cdot i_d}$ | Torque-producing current reference | A |
| 11 | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |

> [!IMPORTANT]

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Drive Type | Real-World Applications | Why Used? |
|---|---|---|
| **Scalar V/f** | Pumps, fans, blowers, HVAC, conveyors, simple machine tools | Low cost, simple, adequate for steady-state loads |
| **Vector FOC** | CNC machines, robotics, elevators, electric vehicles, rolling mills, paper machines, servo systems | High dynamic response, precise torque/speed, wide speed range including field weakening |
| **Direct Torque Control (DTC)** | High-performance industrial drives, traction, marine propulsion | Even faster torque response than FOC, no PWM modulator needed |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Decoupling of Torque and Flux in d-q Frame

**Starting Point ŌĆö 3-Phase Stator Currents in Stationary Frame:**

The three-phase stator currents are space-shifted by $120┬░$ in space and time. In a balanced system:

$$i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$$

**Step 1: Clarke Transformation (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓ stationary frame)**

The Clarke transform projects the three-phase quantities onto two orthogonal stationary axes ($\alpha$ and $\beta$). Using the power-invariant form:

$$i_\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left[ i_a - \frac{1}{2} i_b - \frac{1}{2} i_c \right]$$

$$i_\beta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left[ 0 + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i_b - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i_c \right]$$

**Substitution using $i_c = -i_a - i_b$ (balanced system):**

$$i_\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left[ i_a - \frac{1}{2} i_b + \frac{1}{2}(i_a + i_b) \right] = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot \frac{3}{2} i_a = \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \, i_a$$

$$i_\beta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot \sqrt{3} \left[ i_b - \frac{1}{2}(i_a + i_b) \right] = \sqrt{2} \left[ \frac{i_b - i_a}{2} \right]$$

For simplicity (scaling factor), many texts use:

$$i_\alpha = i_a, \quad i_\beta = \frac{i_a + 2i_b}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2: Park Transformation (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq rotating frame)**

The d-q frame rotates at the synchronous electrical speed $\omega_e$ such that the d-axis is aligned with the rotor flux vector $\vec{\lambda_r}$. The angular position of the d-axis w.r.t. stationary $\alpha$-axis is $\theta_e$.

The current space vector in stationary frame:

$$\vec{i_s} = i_\alpha + j \, i_\beta$$

In the rotating d-q frame, this vector appears as:

$$\vec{i_s}^{dq} = \vec{i_s} \cdot e^{-j\theta_e}$$

Separating real (d-axis) and imaginary (q-axis) parts:

$$i_d = \text{Re}\{\vec{i_s} \cdot e^{-j\theta_e}\} = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e$$

$$i_q = \text{Im}\{\vec{i_s} \cdot e^{-j\theta_e}\} = -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e$$

**Step 3: Derivation of Electromagnetic Torque in d-q Frame**

Starting from the generalized torque expression for any rotating machine (in terms of flux linkages and currents):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\lambda_d \cdot i_q - \lambda_q \cdot i_d)$$

Where $P$ is the number of poles, and $\lambda_d, \lambda_q$ are d-q flux linkages.

In the d-q frame rotating with rotor flux:

$$\lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r, \quad \lambda_{qr} = 0$$

Substituting these flux conditions:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\lambda_r \cdot i_q - 0 \cdot i_d) = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_r \cdot i_q$$

Now, expressing rotor flux in terms of stator d-axis current and machine parameters:

$$\lambda_r = \frac{L_m}{1 + s \tau_r} \cdot i_d$$

In steady state ($s = 0$):

$$\lambda_r = L_m \cdot i_d$$

Substituting back:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot L_m \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$$

For a squirrel-cage induction motor where the rotor leakage is included ($L_r = L_m + L_l$):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$$

**Conclusion of Derivation:** Because $i_d$ (flux) and $i_q$ (torque) are **multiplied but not added**, we can control them *independently* by regulating each with its own PI controller. This is the *heart* of vector control decoupling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Slip Frequency for Indirect FOC

In indirect FOC, the rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ is *estimated* (not directly measured) using slip and rotor speed.

**Rotor Voltage Equation in d-q Frame (rotor is short-circuited, $V_{dr} = V_{qr} = 0$):**

$$0 = R_r \, i_{dr} + \frac{d\lambda_{dr}}{dt} - \omega_{sl} \lambda_{qr}$$

$$0 = R_r \, i_{qr} + \frac{d\lambda_{qr}}{dt} + \omega_{sl} \lambda_{dr}$$

**Steady-state assumption** (rotor flux is constant, so derivatives are zero) and **flux alignment** ($\lambda_{qr} = 0, \lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r$):

$$0 = R_r \, i_{qr} + \omega_{sl} \lambda_r$$

$$\omega_{sl} = -\frac{R_r \, i_{qr}}{\lambda_r}$$

**Express $i_{qr}$ and $\lambda_r$ in terms of stator-side quantities:**

The rotor flux in d-q frame:

$$\lambda_r = \frac{L_m}{1 + s\tau_r} \cdot i_d \quad \xrightarrow{\text{steady-state}} \quad \lambda_r = L_m \, i_d$$

The rotor q-axis current relates to stator q-axis current as:

$$i_{qr} = -\frac{L_m}{L_r} i_q$$

Substituting:

$$\omega_{sl} = -\frac{R_r \cdot \left(-\frac{L_m}{L_r} i_q\right)}{L_m \, i_d} = \frac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \frac{i_q}{i_d}$$

**Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \frac{i_q}{i_d} = \frac{i_q}{\tau_r \, i_d}}$$

Where $\tau_r = L_r / R_r$ is the **rotor time constant**.

**Physical Interpretation:** The slip frequency is *directly proportional* to the torque command ($i_q$) and *inversely proportional* to the flux level ($i_d$). This relationship is the key to indirect FOC ŌĆö once we know $i_d$ and $i_q$, we can compute the slip, add the rotor speed, and integrate to get $\theta_e$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

This is the **most important table for KTU exams** ŌĆö comparison questions appear almost every year.

| Parameter | Scalar Control (V/f) | Vector Control (FOC) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Model** | Steady-state equivalent circuit | Dynamic d-q model (Park's equations) | FOC is more accurate |
| **Variables Controlled** | Magnitude of V and f (scalars) | Magnitude **and phase** of current/voltage (vectors) | FOC controls spatial orientation |
| **Reference Frame** | Stationary (stator) $\alpha$-$\beta$ | Rotating (synchronous) d-q | FOC transforms to rotating frame |
| **Coupling** | Flux and torque are **coupled** | Flux and torque are **decoupled** | Core advantage of FOC |
| **Torque Response** | Slow (typically 50ŌĆō100 ms) | Very fast (1ŌĆō5 ms) | FOC ~20├Ś faster |
| **Speed Range** | Limited (1:10 to 1:20) | Wide (1:100 to 1:1000+) | FOC enables field weakening |
| **Speed Accuracy** | Low (┬▒2 to 5% open-loop) | High (┬▒0.01% closed-loop) | FOC uses encoder/observer |
| **Starting Torque** | Poor (150ŌĆō200% typical, no precise control) | Excellent (0ŌĆō100% controlled from 0 rpm) | FOC has full torque at standstill |
| **Current Requirement** | Higher for same torque | Optimized ŌĆö minimum current for given torque | FOC is more efficient |
| **Complexity** | Low ŌĆö single V/f lookup | High ŌĆö 3 transforms, 2 PI loops, PWM | FOC needs DSP/MCU |
| **Cost** | Low | High (sensors + processing) | FOC needs encoder/observer |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Difficult | Easy ŌĆö natural extension | FOC handles 4-quadrant |
| **Parameter Sensitivity** | Low | High (rotor time constant $\tau_r$ critical) | FOC needs online tuning |
| **Applications** | Pumps, fans, HVAC, conveyors | CNC, robotics, EVs, elevators, servo systems | Application dictates choice |
| **Dynamic Performance** | Poor ŌĆö sluggish | Excellent ŌĆö DC-motor-like | FOC is the benchmark |
| **Steady-State Performance** | Adequate | Excellent | Both OK in steady state |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Scalar (V/f) Control Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Speed Reference Nref] --> B[V/f Profile Generator]
    B --> C[Voltage Reference Vref]
    C --> D[PWM Inverter]
    B --> E[Frequency Reference fref]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    F --> G[Load]
    F --> H[Tachogenerator / Speed Sensor]
    H --> I[Summing Junction]
    A --> I
    I --> B
```

**Description of Flow:**

1. The user sets a **speed reference** (Nref).
2. A **V/f profile generator** creates paired voltage and frequency references using the V/f law (with low-frequency boost).
3. The **PWM inverter** synthesizes a variable-voltage, variable-frequency three-phase output.
4. The **induction motor** drives the load.
5. A **speed sensor** (tachogenerator or encoder) feeds back actual speed.
6. The error is computed at the summing junction and adjusts the V/f profile.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Indirect Vector Control (FOC) Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Speed Reference Nref] --> B[Speed PI Controller]
    H[Rotor Speed Feedback] --> B
    B --> C[Torque Reference Teref]
    C --> D[IQ Reference Calculator]
    E[Flux Reference Lambdaref] --> F[ID Reference Calculator]
    D --> G[Current Control Loop Park Inverse and SVPWM]
    F --> G
    G --> I[Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter]
    I --> J[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    J --> K[Load]
    J --> L[Current Sensors ia ib]
    J --> M[Encoder or Speed Sensor]
    L --> N[Clarke Transform abc to alpha beta]
    N --> O[Park Transform alpha beta to dq]
    O --> P[ID and IQ Feedback]
    P --> G
    M --> Q[Slip Angle Calculator]
    D --> Q
    F --> Q
    Q --> R[Flux Angle theta e Integrator]
    M --> R
    R --> O
    R --> G
```

**Description of Flow:**

1. The **outer speed loop** (PI) generates torque reference from speed error.
2. The **torque reference** is converted to $i_q^{ref}$ (torque current).
3. The **flux reference** (constant below base speed) is converted to $i_d^{ref}$ (flux current).
4. The **measured currents** $i_a, i_b$ go through **Clarke** (abcŌåÆ╬▒╬▓) and **Park** (╬▒╬▓ŌåÆdq) transforms to produce actual $i_d, i_q$.
5. **Current PI controllers** regulate $i_d$ and $i_q$ independently.
6. The **Inverse Park** transform generates voltage references in the ╬▒╬▓ frame.
7. **SVPWM** generates switching signals for the inverter.
8. The **slip calculator** uses $i_d, i_q$ to compute $\omega_{sl}$.
9. Adding $\omega_{sl}$ to $\omega_r$ and integrating gives $\theta_e$ for Park transforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Drawing the **slip angle estimator block** is often the differentiator between a 7-mark and full-mark answer in a vector control question. Always include the feedback of $\theta_e$ to the Park transform block.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flow ŌĆö Which Control to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Drive Control Method] --> B{Is the load steady state with low dynamic requirements?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Scalar V/f Control]
    B -- No --> D{Are fast torque response and precise speed needed?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Is encoder or sensor feedback available?}
    D -- No --> F[Consider Scalar with Closed Loop Speed PI]
    E -- Yes --> G[Use Vector Control Indirect FOC with Encoder]
    E -- No --> H[Use Vector Control with Sensorless Observer]
    C --> I[Pumps Fans HVAC Conveyors]
    G --> J[CNC Robotics Servo Systems EVs]
    H --> K[Cost Sensitive High Performance Applications]
```

**Engineering Decision Logic:**

- **Steady-state, low-dynamics load?** ŌåÆ V/f control (cheaper, simpler)
- **High-dynamics, precise control needed, encoder available?** ŌåÆ FOC with encoder
- **High-dynamics but sensorless preferred?** ŌåÆ Sensorless FOC
- **Extreme torque dynamics (sub-millisecond)?** ŌåÆ Direct Torque Control (DTC)
