## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Compare scalar control and vector control of induction motor drives based on mathematical model and dynamic response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Scalar Control | Vector Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Model** | Uses the *steady-state* per-phase equivalent circuit of the induction motor. Only magnitude of voltage and frequency are controlled. | Uses the *dynamic* d-q model obtained through Clarke and Park transformations. Both magnitude and spatial phase of stator current are controlled. |
| **Dynamic Response** | Slow ŌĆö typically 50ŌĆō100 ms torque response. Sluggish during transients, load changes, and speed reversals. | Very fast ŌĆö 1ŌĆō5 ms torque response. DC-motor-like instantaneous torque control. |

**Key differentiator (1 extra sentence for 3-mark completeness):** Vector control decouples flux and torque by aligning rotor flux with the d-axis, allowing independent and instantaneous control of each, while scalar control cannot separate them, leading to coupled sluggish response.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any four applications where vector-controlled drives are preferred over scalar-controlled drives. Justify your answer briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **CNC Machine Tools** ŌĆö Require precise speed/position control and rapid torque response for contour cutting. Vector control provides sub-millisecond torque updates essential for surface finish quality.

2. **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** ŌĆö Need instant torque from standstill, regenerative braking, and wide constant-power field-weakening range. Vector control delivers all of these; scalar control cannot start an EV on an incline.

3. **Robotics & Servo Systems** ŌĆö Joint actuators demand fast, accurate, decoupled torque control for trajectory tracking. Vector control gives the *positioning accuracy* and *dynamic stiffness* required.

4. **Elevators and Cranes** ŌĆö Require precise levelling at floors and holding full load at standstill without slip. Vector control provides 100% torque at zero speed, scalar control cannot.

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of indirect vector control (Field Oriented Control) of an induction motor drive. Compare its performance with scalar V/f control on the basis of (a) torque response, (b) starting torque, (c) speed range, and (d) parameter sensitivity.**

##### **Solution:**

###### **Part (a): Working of Indirect Vector Control ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2):**

The indirect vector control system consists of the following functional blocks:

**1. Outer Speed Control Loop:**
- A **PI speed controller** compares the reference speed $\omega_{ref}$ with the actual speed $\omega_r$ (from encoder).
- The error generates the **torque reference $T_e^{ref}$**.

**2. Decoupling Calculation:**
- Flux reference: $i_d^{ref} = \lambda_r^{ref} / L_m$ (constant below base speed)
- Torque reference: $i_q^{ref} = \dfrac{2 L_r}{3 P L_m^2 \, i_d} \cdot T_e^{ref}$

**3. Coordinate Transformation (Feedback Path):**
- Measured currents $i_a, i_b$ ŌåÆ **Clarke transform** ŌåÆ $i_\alpha, i_\beta$ (stationary ╬▒-╬▓ frame)
- $i_\alpha, i_\beta$ ŌåÆ **Park transform** using flux angle $\theta_e$ ŌåÆ $i_d, i_q$ (rotating d-q frame)

**4. Current Control Loops:**
- Two **PI controllers** regulate $i_d$ and $i_q$ independently.
- Output: voltage references $V_d^{ref}, V_q^{ref}$ in d-q frame.

**5. Inverse Transformation and Modulation:**
- **Inverse Park** ($V_d, V_q \rightarrow V_\alpha, V_\beta$) using $\theta_e$.
- **SVPWM** generates gating signals for the **3-phase VSI** inverter.

**6. Flux Angle Estimation (Indirect Method):**
- Slip frequency: $\omega_{sl} = \dfrac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \dfrac{i_q}{i_d}$
- Synchronous speed: $\omega_e = \omega_{sl} + \omega_r$
- Flux angle: $\theta_e = \int \omega_e \, dt$

**Key Concept (1 mark):** Indirect FOC estimates the flux angle from current and speed, while direct FOC measures flux using sensors. Indirect is more common in industry.

###### **Part (b): Comparison with Scalar V/f Control ŌĆö 7 marks (4 sub-parts ├Ś ~1.75 marks each)**

| Parameter | Scalar (V/f) | Vector (FOC) | Detailed Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) Torque Response** | Slow (50ŌĆō100 ms) | Very fast (1ŌĆō5 ms) | In scalar, flux and torque are coupled; changing V to alter torque also changes flux, requiring time to re-establish steady state. In FOC, $i_q$ directly controls torque without disturbing $i_d$ (flux), giving near-instantaneous response. |
| **(b) Starting Torque** | Limited (150ŌĆō200% rated, no precision) | Full 0ŌĆō300% controlled torque from zero speed | Scalar control cannot maintain rated flux at zero Hz without large $V_{boost}$, and even then, no closed-loop torque control exists. FOC uses the $i_d - i_q$ decoupling ŌĆö with $i_d$ set to rated flux value and $i_q$ at any desired value, full torque is available at zero speed. |
| **(c) Speed Range** | 1:10 to 1:20 (typical) | 1:100 to 1:1000+ (with field weakening) | Scalar control is limited because at very low speeds, stator resistance drop dominates, and at very high speeds, inverter voltage ceiling limits field weakening. FOC uses *vector decomposition* of $i_d$ and $i_q$ ŌĆö reducing $i_d$ above base speed enables smooth field weakening and constant power operation to very high speeds. |
| **(d) Parameter Sensitivity** | Low (only V/f ratio matters) | High (rotor time constant $\tau_r = L_r / R_r$ is critical) | FOC performance depends critically on the accuracy of $\tau_r$ because the slip frequency $\omega_{sl} = i_q / (\tau_r \, i_d)$ and the flux angle $\theta_e$ are computed from it. Temperature variations alter $R_r$, causing detuning. Online tuning or adaptive schemes are needed. Scalar control, being open-loop, is unaffected. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Skipping the flux angle estimator block** in the FOC block diagram costs 2 marks. Always include the slip calculation + integrator.
> 2. **Confusing Indirect vs Direct FOC** costs 1 mark. State clearly: *Indirect = estimate $\theta_e$ from current/speed; Direct = measure rotor flux directly (e.g., via flux sensing coils).*
> 3. **Forgetting the V/f boost** in scalar control section costs 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Saying "vector control is better" without justification** in comparison ŌĆö be specific about *which parameter* and *why*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of scalar (V/f) control of induction motor drive. Discuss the need for voltage boost at low frequencies. (7 marks)**

**(b) Enumerate the major applications of vector-controlled induction motor drives in modern industry. Explain in detail why scalar control is unsuitable for these applications. (7 marks)**

##### **Solution:**

###### **Part (a): Scalar (V/f) Control ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1):**

The scalar V/f control drive consists of:

1. **Reference Speed Input** ($N_{ref}$ or $\omega_{ref}$).
2. **V/f Profile Generator** ŌĆö produces paired $(V_{ref}, f_{ref})$ with low-frequency boost.
3. **PWM Inverter** ŌĆö generates variable-voltage, variable-frequency 3-phase output.
4. **Induction Motor** driving the load.
5. **Speed Sensor** (optional for open-loop) ŌĆö provides feedback for closed-loop version.

**V/f Law:**

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = k = \text{constant}$$

This ensures **constant air-gap flux** below base speed.

**Need for Voltage Boost at Low Frequencies (3 marks):**

At very low frequencies (e.g., 1ŌĆō5 Hz), the stator resistance drop $I_s R_s$ becomes a **significant fraction** of the applied voltage $V$. The actual air-gap EMF is:

$$E = V - I_s R_s$$

**Numerical Example:** Consider a 400V, 50 Hz motor. The V/f ratio is $k = 8$ V/Hz. At 2 Hz, $V_{ref} = 16$ V. But if $I_s R_s$ drop is 12V, then $E = 4$ V ŌĆö flux is only 25% of rated!

**Solution:** Add a voltage boost $V_{boost}$ at low frequencies:

$$V_{ref}(f) = V_{boost} + k \cdot f$$

Typical boost: 5ŌĆō10% of rated voltage, gradually reducing to zero by ~10 Hz.

**V/f Curve Characteristics:**

- **0 to $f_{rated}$:** Constant torque region ŌĆö V/f constant.
- **Above $f_{rated}$:** Constant power region ŌĆö V saturated at $V_{rated}$, f increases, flux decreases (field weakening).

###### **Part (b): Applications of Vector-Controlled Drives ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Major Applications:**

1. **CNC Machine Tools and Machining Centres (2 marks):**
   - Need: Precise position, speed, and torque control for cutting accuracy.
   - Why scalar fails: Open-loop V/f cannot maintain tool position during load transients; speed droop occurs; surface finish quality degrades.

2. **Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (2 marks):**
   - Need: Instant torque from standstill, regenerative braking, wide speed range (city cruising to highway).
   - Why scalar fails: Cannot start EV on an incline with full load; cannot regen-brake efficiently; poor field weakening for high-speed cruising.

3. **Robotics and Industrial Servo Systems (1.5 marks):**
   - Need: Fast, decoupled joint torque control for trajectory tracking.
   - Why scalar fails: Coupled flux-torque means torque transients disturb flux, causing oscillation and poor tracking.

4. **Elevators, Cranes, and Hoists (1.5 marks):**
   - Need: Precise levelling at floors, full-load hold at standstill.
   - Why scalar fails: No torque at zero speed with V/f; cannot hold position without mechanical brake slip.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Drawing V/f curve without mentioning voltage boost** = 1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Just listing application names without "why scalar fails"** = up to 3 marks deduction in Part (b).
> 3. **Forgetting the V/f constant and field-weakening regions** in the V/f profile = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up constant torque vs constant power regions** above and below base speed = 0.5 mark deduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Scalar Control = V/f Control** ŌĆö magnitude-only, steady-state model, open-loop typical, low cost.
- [ ] **Vector Control = Field Oriented Control (FOC)** ŌĆö magnitude + phase, dynamic d-q model, decoupled flux and torque.
- [ ] **Core Formula ŌĆö Scalar:** $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ to keep flux constant.
- [ ] **Core Formula ŌĆö Vector:** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$ ŌĆö flux and torque *multiplied*, hence *decoupled*.
- [ ] **Slip Frequency (Indirect FOC):** $\omega_{sl} = \dfrac{R_r}{L_r} \cdot \dfrac{i_q}{i_d} = \dfrac{i_q}{\tau_r \, i_d}$.
- [ ] **Flux Angle:** $\theta_e = \displaystyle\int (\omega_{sl} + \omega_r) \, dt$.
- [ ] **Voltage Boost Required** in V/f control at low frequencies to overcome $I_s R_s$ drop.
- [ ] **Field Weakening Region:** Above base speed, V is saturated, f increases, flux drops ŌåÆ constant power.
- [ ] **Two transformations used in FOC:** Clarke (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓) and Park (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq).
- [ ] **Decoupling principle:** In d-q frame aligned with rotor flux, $i_d$ controls flux only, $i_q$ controls torque only.
- [ ] **Vector control applications:** CNC, Robotics, EVs, Elevators, Servo systems, Paper/Steel mills, Marine propulsion.
- [ ] **Scalar control applications:** Pumps, Fans, Blowers, HVAC, Conveyors, Compressors.
- [ ] **Starting torque:** Scalar = limited/poor; Vector = full controlled torque at 0 RPM.
- [ ] **Speed range:** Scalar = 1:10 to 1:20; Vector = 1:100 to 1:1000+ (with field weakening).
- [ ] **Torque response:** Scalar = 50ŌĆō100 ms; Vector = 1ŌĆō5 ms (DC-motor-like).
- [ ] **Draw both block diagrams** (Scalar and FOC) from memory ŌĆö they appear in almost every KTU paper.
- [ ] **Mention $\tau_r$ parameter sensitivity** in any vector control answer ŌĆö examiners love this point.
- [ ] **DT vs ID Component Names:** $i_d$ = direct axis = flux; $i_q$ = quadrature axis = torque.
- [ ] **Equation Count to Memorize:** at least 5 core equations ŌĆö V/f law, torque (d-q), slip frequency, flux angle, $i_d^{ref}$ formula.
- [ ] **Examiner's pet topic:** *"Compare scalar and vector control"* ŌĆö expect a 14-mark question almost every exam cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** If you are short on time, draw the **vector control block diagram accurately** (with all feedback loops and the slip angle estimator) and present the **comparison table** ŌĆö these two alone typically fetch 8ŌĆō10 of the 14 marks in a typical KTU question on this topic.
